topics.title: Characterization and Classification of Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characterization and Classification of Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

In the rigorous framework of **Signal Processing for Robotics (PCCPT402)**, a **signal** is formally defined as any physical quantity that varies with respect to one or more independent variables (most commonly **time**), and which conveys information about the state, behavior, or environment of a physical system.

Mathematically, a signal is expressed as a function:
$$ x(t) \quad \text{or} \quad x[n] $$

where $t$ is a continuous independent variable and $n$ is a discrete integer index.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** A signal is a function of one or more independent variables that carries information. In robotics, signals are generated by sensors (vision, LiDAR, IMU, encoders) and are processed to enable perception, planning, and control.

### Real-World Analogy (The "Heartbeat" Intuition)

Think of a signal as the **heartbeat trace on a medical monitor**:
- The horizontal axis is **time** (the independent variable).
- The vertical axis is the **voltage** representing heart muscle contraction (the signal value).
- Just as a doctor interprets the shape, frequency, and rhythm of your heartbeat to diagnose health, a roboticist interprets sensor signals to perceive the world.

A robot's encoder gives a position signal, a microphone gives an audio signal, and a camera gives a 2D image signal. **All are functions carrying information.**

## 1.2 Why Do We Classify Signals?

Classification is the **cornerstone of signal processing** because the mathematical tools we apply depend on signal type. For example:
- Fourier Transform applies directly to **energy signals**, not power signals.
- Z-transform is the natural tool for **discrete-time** signals.
- Symmetry properties (**even/odd**) drastically simplify convolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must master classification based on: continuity, periodicity, determinism, symmetry, and energy/power — all of which are heavily tested in ESE.

## 1.3 Primary Characterization Axes

Signals are characterized along **five primary axes** as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus:

| # | Characterization Axis | Two Extremes |
|---|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Domain** | Continuous-Time (CT) vs Discrete-Time (DT) |
| 2 | **Periodicity** | Periodic vs Aperiodic |
| 3 | **Symmetry** | Even vs Odd |
| 4 | **Energy/Power** | Energy Signal vs Power Signal |
| 5 | **Determinism** | Deterministic vs Random (Stochastic) |

A signal can be simultaneously continuous, periodic, even, deterministic, and an energy signal — these classifications are **orthogonal properties** that we layer on top of each other.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification by Domain (Continuous vs Discrete vs Digital)

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time (CT) Signal
Defined for every value of time $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
$$ x(t), \quad t \in \mathbb{R} $$
**Examples:** Analog voltage from a temperature sensor, speech waveform, motor shaft position (before sampling).

### 2.1.2 Discrete-Time (DT) Signal
Defined only at discrete instants, usually $n \in \mathbb{Z}$. Often obtained by sampling $x(t)$.
$$ x[n] = x(nT_s), \quad n \in \mathbb{Z} $$
where $T_s$ is the sampling period (in **seconds/sample**).

### 2.1.3 Digital Signal
A discrete-time signal whose amplitude is also **quantized** to a finite set (e.g., 16-bit ADC gives $2^{16}$ levels).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world robotic example:** A LiDAR unit produces a **discrete-time digital signal** — discrete in time (one reading per pulse) AND quantized in amplitude (finite ADC resolution).

---

## 2.2 Classification by Periodicity

### 2.2.1 Periodic Signal
A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** if there exists a smallest positive constant $T_0$ such that:
$$ x(t + T_0) = x(t) \quad \forall t \in \mathbb{R} $$
$T_0$ is called the **fundamental period**. The reciprocal is the **fundamental frequency**:
$$ f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} \quad [\text{Hz}] \quad ; \quad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0} \quad [\text{rad/s}] $$

If this condition is never satisfied, the signal is **aperiodic**.

### 2.2.2 Aperiodic Signal
A signal for which no finite $T_0$ satisfies the periodicity condition. Examples: decaying exponential $e^{-at}u(t)$, Gaussian pulse, single rectangular pulse.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A signal with $x(t) = 0$ for all $t$ is periodic with **any** $T_0$, but conventionally we say it has no well-defined fundamental period (a degenerate case).

---

## 2.3 Classification by Symmetry (Even / Odd)

### 2.3.1 Even Signal
$$ x(t) = x(-t) $$
Mirror-symmetric about the vertical axis. Example: $x(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)$.

### 2.3.2 Odd Signal
$$ x(-t) = -x(t) $$
Anti-symmetric about the origin. Example: $x(t) = \sin(\omega_0 t)$.

### 2.3.3 Signal Decomposition Theorem
**Every** signal can be uniquely decomposed into even and odd parts:
$$ x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t) $$
where:
$$ x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \quad ; \quad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} $$

This is a **high-yield KTU question** (decomposition is tested in nearly every ESE).

---

## 2.4 Classification by Energy and Power

### 2.4.1 Energy of a Signal
The total **normalized energy** delivered to a $1\,\Omega$ resistor:
$$ E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt \quad \text{(CT)} $$
$$ E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 \quad \text{(DT)} $$

A signal with $0 < E < \infty$ is an **energy signal** (necessarily $P = 0$).

### 2.4.2 Power of a Signal
The **time-averaged power**:
$$ P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt \quad \text{(CT)} $$
$$ P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2 \quad \text{(DT)} $$

A signal with $0 < P < \infty$ is a **power signal** (necessarily $E = \infty$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mutually Exclusive Rule:** A signal CANNOT be both an energy signal AND a power signal. It can be neither (e.g., $x(t) = t$), or one of them, but not both.

### 2.4.3 Special Case: Periodic Signals
For a periodic signal with period $T_0$, the average power is computed over a single period:
$$ P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt $$

---

## 2.5 Classification by Determinism

### 2.5.1 Deterministic Signal
Completely specified as a known function of time. No uncertainty. Example: $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$.

### 2.5.2 Random (Stochastic) Signal
Cannot be described by an explicit mathematical function; characterized by **statistical properties** (mean, variance, PDF, PSD). Example: thermal noise in a sensor, EEG signals.

> [!NOTE]
> In robotics, **all real sensor signals contain a stochastic (noise) component** — hence the importance of statistical signal processing.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Continuous-Time | Discrete-Time | Units |
|---|---------|-----------------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | Signal Notation | $x(t)$ | $x[n]$ | — |
| 2 | Energy $E$ | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Joules |
| 3 | Power $P$ | $\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Watts |
| 4 | Periodicity | $x(t+T_0) = x(t)$ | $x[n+N_0] = x[n]$ | $T_0$: s, $N_0$: samples |
| 5 | Even Test | $x(t) = x(-t)$ | $x[n] = x[-n]$ | — |
| 6 | Odd Test | $x(-t) = -x(t)$ | $x[-n] = -x[n]$ | — |
| 7 | Even Part | $\frac{x(t)+x(-t)}{2}$ | $\frac{x[n]+x[-n]}{2}$ | — |
| 8 | Odd Part | $\frac{x(t)-x(-t)}{2}$ | $\frac{x[n]-x[-n]}{2}$ | — |
| 9 | Fundamental Freq. | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 2\pi / T_0$ | $\Omega_0 = 2\pi / N_0$ | rad/s or rad/sample |
| 10 | Periodic Power | $\frac{1}{T_0}\int_0^{T_0}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ | $\frac{1}{N_0}\sum_{n=0}^{N_0-1}\vert x[n]\vert^2$ | Watts |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Robotics perception:** Encoder signals are **periodic**; classification into power signals simplifies real-time torque computation.
- **Communication:** Speech and EMG signals are **aperiodic energy signals**; spectral analysis is used.
- **Control systems:** Even/Odd decomposition of an **error signal** isolates symmetric disturbances, helping reject them via synchronous averaging.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Even-Odd Decomposition of a Mixed Signal

**Problem:** Decompose $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t)$ into its even and odd parts.

> Recall: $u(t)$ is the unit step (1 for $t \geq 0$, 0 otherwise); $u(-t)$ is its time-reversed version.

### Step 1 — Write $x(-t)$ explicitly

We must find $x(-t)$ by substituting $-t$ for $t$:

$$ x(-t) = e^{-2(-t)}u(-t) + e^{2(-t)}u(-(-t)) $$

$$ x(-t) = e^{2t}u(-t) + e^{-2t}u(t) $$

> **Conversion logic:** Inside the unit step function, replace $t \to -t$. The two terms simply swap which side of the step they live on.

### Step 2 — Add $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) + x(-t) &= \left[ e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t) \right] + \left[ e^{2t}u(-t) + e^{-2t}u(t) \right] \\
&= 2e^{-2t}u(t) + 2e^{2t}u(-t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Even Part

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e(t) &= \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2} \\
&= \frac{2e^{-2t}u(t) + 2e^{2t}u(-t)}{2} \\
&= e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t) \\
&= e^{-2\vert t \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The combined expression equals $e^{-2\vert t \vert}$, a classic even (two-sided decaying exponential) signal.

### Step 4 — Subtract $x(t)$ and $x(-t)$ and divide by 2

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) - x(-t) &= \left[ e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t) \right] - \left[ e^{2t}u(-t) + e^{-t}u(t) \right] \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$ x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = 0 $$

> **Conclusion:** $x(t)$ is purely even. Indeed, $x(t) = e^{-2\vert t \vert}$ is symmetric, confirming $x_o(t) = 0$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Energy and Power of a Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Compute the energy and average power of a single rectangular pulse:
$$ x(t) = A \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t}{T}\right) = \begin{cases} A, & \vert t \vert < T/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

### Step 1 — Energy Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt \\
&= \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} A^2 \, dt \\
&= A^2 \cdot \Big[ t \Big]_{-T/2}^{T/2} \\
&= A^2 \cdot \left( \frac{T}{2} - \left(-\frac{T}{2}\right) \right) \\
&= A^2 T
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The squared pulse has constant amplitude $A^2$ over a window of width $T$, so the integral equals area $= A^2 T$.

### Step 2 — Power Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \lim_{T_1 \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T_1}\int_{-T_1}^{T_1}\vert x(t)\vert^2 \, dt \\
&= \lim_{T_1 \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T_1} \cdot A^2 T \\
&= \lim_{T_1 \to \infty} \frac{A^2 T}{2T_1} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Classification

| Property | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Energy $E$ | $A^2 T$ (finite, since $T$ is finite and $A \neq 0$) |
| Power $P$ | $0$ |
| Class | **Energy Signal** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students mistakenly classify the rectangular pulse as a power signal because it has finite duration. **Duration $\neq$ Power classification.** A signal of *finite* duration is almost always an **energy signal**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Power of a Sinusoidal Signal

**Problem:** Find the average power of $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \lim_{T_1 \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T_1}\int_{-T_1}^{T_1} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt \\
&= \lim_{T_1 \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{2T_1}\int_{-T_1}^{T_1} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt \\
&= \lim_{T_1 \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T_1}\left[ \int_{-T_1}^{T_1} 1 \, dt + \int_{-T_1}^{T_1}\cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \, dt \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The cosine term averages to zero over a long symmetric interval:

$$ P = \lim_{T_1 \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T_1} \cdot 2T_1 = \frac{A^2}{2} $$

**Result:** Average power of a sinusoid is $A^2/2$ (independent of frequency and phase).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Energy vs Power Signal Quick-Decision Table

| Signal $x(t)$ | $E$ | $P$ | Class |
|---------------|-----|-----|-------|
| $A\cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\infty$ | $A^2/2$ | **Power** |
| Rectangular pulse of width $T$, height $A$ | $A^2 T$ | $0$ | **Energy** |
| $e^{-at}u(t), \; a>0$ | $1/(2a)$ | $0$ | **Energy** |
| Unit step $u(t)$ | $\infty$ | $1/2$ | **Power** |
| $1$ (constant DC) | $\infty$ | $1$ | **Power** |
| $t \cdot u(t)$ (ramp) | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | **Neither** |
| $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ (complex exponential) | $\infty$ | $1$ | **Power** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Signal Classification Hierarchy

The following Mermaid block renders a complete taxonomic map of all signal categories in Module 1. Each branch is annotated with the **mathematical test** that defines membership.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["SIGNAL x t or x n"]
    rootA --> dom["By DOMAIN"]
    rootA --> per["By PERIODICITY"]
    rootA --> sym["By SYMMETRY"]
    rootA --> ep["By ENERGY or POWER"]
    rootA --> det["By DETERMINISM"]

    dom --> cts["Continuous Time x t"]
    dom --> dts["Discrete Time x n"]
    dom --> dig["Digital Quantized x n"]

    per --> pers["Periodic x t plus T0 equals x t"]
    per --> apers["Aperiodic No T0 exists"]

    sym --> ev["Even x minus t equals x t"]
    sym --> od["Odd x minus t equals minus x t"]
    sym --> mx["Mixed Decomp via xe and xo"]

    ep --> es["Energy Signal 0 less than E less than infinity"]
    ep --> ps["Power Signal 0 less than P less than infinity"]

    det --> ds["Deterministic Exact function known"]
    det --> rs["Random Statistical description only"]

    cts --- noteA["Robotic Example Strain gauge voltage"]
    dts --- noteB["Robotic Example Sampled encoder count"]
    dig --- noteC["Robotic Example ADC output from IMU"]
    pers --- noteD["Robotic Example Motor RPM signal"]
    apers --- noteE["Robotic Example Single LiDAR return"]
    ev --- noteF["Robotic Example Symmetric force profile"]
    od --- noteG["Robotic Example Sine reference command"]
    es --- noteH["Robotic Example Transient impact response"]
    ps --- noteI["Robotic Example Continuous motor current"]
    ds --- noteJ["Robotic Example Reference trajectory"]
    rs --- noteK["Robotic Example Thermal sensor noise"]

    style rootA fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b2545,stroke-width:3px
    style dom fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style per fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style sym fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style ep fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style det fill:#455a64,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Energy-Signal vs Power-Signal Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Given Signal x t"]
    q1{"Is Total Energy E finite?"}
    es["ENERGY SIGNAL E less than infinity P equals 0"]
    q2{"Is Average Power P finite and nonzero?"}
    ps["POWER SIGNAL P less than infinity E equals infinity"]
    neither["NEITHER E equals infinity P equals infinity"]
    stop["End"]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> es --> stop
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> ps --> stop
    q2 -- No --> neither --> stop

    style start fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style es fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style ps fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style neither fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
    style stop fill:#455a64,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Signal Pipeline in a Robotic System

```mermaid
graph LR
    wld["Physical World Phenomenon"]
    snsr["Sensor Transducer"]
    cond["Signal Conditioning Amplify Filter"]
    adc["ADC Sampler Quantizer"]
    dsp["DSP Unit PCCPT402 Scope"]
    dec["Decision or Actuator Output"]

    wld -->|"Continuous analog signal"| snsr
    snsr -->|"Raw CT signal with noise"| cond
    cond -->|"Cleaned CT signal"| adc
    adc -->|"Discrete digital signal x n"| dsp
    dsp -->|"Processed features / classification"| dec

    style wld fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff
    style snsr fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff
    style cond fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style adc fill:#ef6c00,color:#ffffff
    style dsp fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff
    style dec fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** At each block boundary, the signal changes its class. The ADC boundary is critical — it transforms a **continuous deterministic + noise** mixture into a **discrete digital** signal. This is exactly the chain students must understand to answer Module 1 classification questions in context.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**
**Define an energy signal and a power signal. Show with a mathematical example that a signal cannot be both.**

**Model Answer:**

An **energy signal** $x(t)$ is one whose total normalized energy is finite and non-zero:
$$ E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt \quad \text{with} \quad 0 < E < \infty $$

A **power signal** $x(t)$ is one whose average power is finite and non-zero:
$$ P = \lim_{T \to \infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt \quad \text{with} \quad 0 < P < \infty $$

**Proof of mutual exclusivity (3 lines):**

> For any signal, if $E < \infty$, then $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{E}{2T} = 0$ (energy spread over infinite time gives zero average power). Conversely, if $0 < P < \infty$, then $E = \lim_{T \to \infty} 2T \cdot P = \infty$. Therefore a signal cannot be both an energy and a power signal. $\blacksquare$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**State the conditions for a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ to be (a) periodic, (b) even, (c) odd. Illustrate each with one example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Condition | Example |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| (a) Periodic | $\exists\, N_0 \in \mathbb{Z}^+ : x[n + N_0] = x[n] \, \forall n$ | $x[n] = \cos(0.2\pi n)$ with $N_0 = 10$ |
| (b) Even | $x[-n] = x[n] \, \forall n$ | $x[n] = \cos(n)$ since $\cos(-n) = \cos(n)$ |
| (c) Odd | $x[-n] = -x[n] \, \forall n$ | $x[n] = \sin(n)$ since $\sin(-n) = -\sin(n)$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Question (with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Define and explain with examples the classification of continuous-time signals based on:
**(i)** Periodicity **(ii)** Symmetry **(iii)** Determinism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t)$:
**(i)** Determine if it is energy or power signal by computing $E$.
**(ii)** Decompose it into its even and odd components. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(i) Periodicity:**
A CT signal $x(t)$ is periodic if $\exists\, T_0 > 0$ such that $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$ for all $t$. $T_0$ is the fundamental period.
- *Example of periodic signal:* $x(t) = \cos(100\pi t)$, $T_0 = 20$ ms.
- *Example of aperiodic signal:* $x(t) = e^{-5t}u(t)$.

**(ii) Symmetry:**
- *Even:* $x(t) = x(-t)$. Example: $\cos(t)$.
- *Odd:* $x(t) = -x(-t)$. Example: $\sin(t)$.
- Signals with neither property are *mixed* and must be decomposed.

**(iii) Determinism:**
- *Deterministic:* completely described by an explicit mathematical function. Example: $x(t) = 5\cos(2t)$.
- *Random (stochastic):* described only by statistical measures. Example: thermal noise $n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Energy calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\vert x(t)\vert^2 dt \\
&= \int_{-\infty}^{0} e^{4t}\,dt + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t}\,dt \\
&= \left[ \frac{e^{4t}}{4} \right]_{-\infty}^{0} + \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= \left(\frac{1}{4} - 0\right) + \left(0 - \left(-\frac{1}{4}\right)\right) = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0 < E = 1/2 < \infty$, the signal is an **energy signal**.

**(ii) Even-Odd decomposition:**

From Section 3.1 derivation, we already found:

$$ x_e(t) = e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t) = e^{-2\vert t \vert} $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(t) - x(-t) &= \left[ e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{2t}u(-t) \right] - \left[ e^{2t}u(-t) + e^{-2t}u(t) \right] = 0 \\
x_o(t) &= \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The signal is **purely even**, with even part $x_e(t) = e^{-2\vert t \vert}$ and odd part $x_o(t) = 0$.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Compute the energy and average power of the following signals and classify each:
**(i)** $x_1(t) = A e^{-3t} u(t)$
**(ii)** $x_2(t) = 4 \cos(50\pi t + \pi/4)$ **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time signal is given by $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ (defined for $n = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$).
**(i)** Test whether $x[n]$ is even, odd, or neither.
**(ii)** Find its even and odd parts and verify the decomposition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(i) For $x_1(t) = Ae^{-3t}u(t)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \int_{0}^{\infty} A^2 e^{-6t}\,dt = A^2 \left[ \frac{e^{-6t}}{-6} \right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{A^2}{6} \\
P_1 &= \lim_{T \to \infty}\frac{1}{2T} \cdot \frac{A^2}{6} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Class:** **Energy signal** (finite $E_1 = A^2/6$, zero $P_1$).

**(ii) For $x_2(t) = 4\cos(50\pi t + \pi/4)$:**

This is a sinusoid with $A = 4$. By the standard result (Section 3.3):

$$ P_2 = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8\ \text{W} $$

$$ E_2 = \infty $$

**Class:** **Power signal** with $P_2 = 8$ W.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Symmetry test:**

The signal: $x[-2]=1, x[-1]=2, x[0]=3, x[1]=2, x[2]=1$.

Check: $x[-2] = 1 = x[2]$ ✓, $x[-1] = 2 = x[1]$ ✓, $x[0] = 3 = -x[0]$? No, $3 \neq -3$.

So the signal is **neither even nor odd** (it is a *mixed* signal).

**(ii) Decomposition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_e[n] &= \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2} = \{1, 2, 1.5, 2, 1\} \quad \text{for } n = -2,-1,0,1,2 \\
x_o[n] &= \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2} = \{0, 0, 1.5, 0, 0\} \quad \text{for } n = -2,-1,0,1,2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $x_e[n] + x_o[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\} = x[n]$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Top 3 Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value / square in energy formula.** Writing $E = \int x(t)\,dt$ instead of $\int \vert x(t)\vert^2 dt$ will cost **2 of 7 marks** in part (a).
> 2. **Misclassifying rectangular pulses as power signals.** A finite-duration signal is almost always an energy signal. State this explicitly with $P = 0$.
> 3. **In even-odd decomposition, computing $x(-t)$ incorrectly.** Substitute $-t$ carefully inside unit step functions. A common error is to write $u(-t) = u(t)$, which is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Characterization & Classification of Signals**

- **Signal = function of independent variable carrying information.** Time is the canonical independent variable in robotics.
- **Three domain types:** Continuous (analog), Discrete-time (sampled), Digital (sampled + quantized).
- **Periodicity Test (CT):** $x(t + T_0) = x(t)$. **Periodicity Test (DT):** $x[n + N_0] = x[n]$.
- **Symmetry Tests:** Even → $x(-t) = x(t)$. Odd → $x(-t) = -x(t)$.
- **Decomposition Theorem:** Any signal = Even part + Odd part, with formulas $x_e = (x+x(-))/2$ and $x_o = (x-x(-))/2$.
- **Energy Signal:** $0 < E < \infty$, hence $P = 0$. Use the squared absolute integral/sum.
- **Power Signal:** $0 < P < \infty$, hence $E = \infty$. For periodic signals, average over one period.
- **Sinusoid Power = $A^2/2$** — memorize this cold; it appears in nearly every KTU paper.
- **Mutual Exclusivity:** A signal cannot be both energy AND power (proved using the limit relationship $P = E/(2T)$ as $T \to \infty$).
- **Deterministic vs Random:** Deterministic = exact formula. Random = statistical description (mean, variance, PSD).
- **Robotic context (must mention in long answers for full marks):** Encoder signals are periodic power signals; LiDAR pulses are aperiodic energy signals; sensor noise is random and treated statistically.
- **Top mark-scoring tricks:** Always state the **class** after computing $E$ or $P$. Always verify the decomposition $x = x_e + x_o$. Always show the $T \to \infty$ limit when classifying by power.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Characterization and Classification of Signals**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Signal Processing Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Signal Processing Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Typical Signal Processing Operations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Robotics and Embedded Systems**, a *Signal* is defined as any physical quantity that varies with time, space, or any other independent variable and carries information. **Typical Signal Processing Operations** refer to the standard, sequential, and mathematical procedures applied to raw sensor data to extract, enhance, transform, or compress meaningful information so that a robotic system can perceive its environment, make decisions, and execute control actions.

The canonical signal processing chain in robotics consists of:
1. **Signal Acquisition** (Sensing)
2. **Signal Conditioning** (Amplification, Filtering)
3. **Signal Conversion** (Sampling and Quantization)
4. **Signal Transformation** (Fourier, Wavelet, Z-transform)
5. **Signal Analysis** (Feature extraction, Correlation, Convolution)
6. **Signal Reconstruction** (DAC, Interpolation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course PCCPT402 emphasizes operations that bridge the gap between *continuous-time analog sensor inputs* (e.g., LiDAR, IMU, microphones) and *discrete-time digital commands* that actuate motors, servos, and decision algorithms in a robot.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a noisy factory, trying to listen to a colleague give you a specific instruction (e.g., "move 2 meters forward"). Your **ears** are the *sensors*, the **factory noise** is the *interference*, and your friend's voice is the *desired signal*. Your brain instinctively performs these steps:
- **Filtering**: You tune your attention to ignore the noise (analogous to a band-pass filter).
- **Amplification**: You lean closer to hear the weak voice (analogous to an amplifier).
- **Sampling**: You capture discrete words like "two", "meters", "forward" (analogous to analog-to-digital conversion).
- **Feature Extraction**: You interpret the meaning of the words (analogous to FFT or pattern recognition).

A robot does the exact same thing, but mathematically and deterministically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The fidelity of any signal processing pipeline is judged using **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**, measured in **decibels (dB)**, and the system's ability to preserve information is measured by its **bandwidth (Hz)** and **bit-depth (bits)**.

### Classification of Signals in Robotics

| Signal Class | Mathematical Notation | Robotics Example |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------|
| Continuous-Time, Continuous-Amplitude (Analog) | $x(t), \ t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Output of a thermistor, microphone voltage |
| Discrete-Time, Continuous-Amplitude (Sampled) | $x[nT_s], \ n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Pre-quantization ADC samples |
| Discrete-Time, Discrete-Amplitude (Digital) | $x_Q[n] \in \{0, 1, ..., 2^B - 1\}$ | Stored image pixels, IMU digital packets |
| Deterministic Signal | Predictable mathematical form | Sine wave from a motor encoder |
| Random Signal | Described statistically | Thermal noise, sensor jitter |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Signal Processing Pipeline

The processing of a robotic sensor signal follows a structured six-stage pipeline. Each stage is critical, and any failure at one stage propagates downstream.

### Stage 1: Signal Acquisition (Transduction)
A physical phenomenon (light, sound, force, temperature) is converted into a measurable electrical signal by a *transducer*. Common robotic transducers include photodiodes (vision), MEMS accelerometers (IMU), and Hall-effect sensors (motor speed).

### Stage 2: Signal Conditioning
Raw transducer outputs are typically weak (in the **millivolt (mV)** range) and buried in noise. Conditioning prepares the signal for digitization:
- **Amplification**: Boosts amplitude using Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amps). The voltage gain is:
$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$$
- **Isolation**: Protects the digital system from high-voltage transients.
- **Linearization**: Corrects non-linear transducer responses (e.g., thermistor NTC curves).

### Stage 3: Filtering
Filters selectively pass or reject specific frequency components. They are classified into four fundamental types:
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)**: Passes frequencies below a cutoff $\omega_c$ (used to remove high-frequency sensor noise).
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF)**: Passes frequencies above $\omega_c$ (used to remove DC offset or drift).
- **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)**: Passes a specific band $ [\omega_{c1}, \omega_{c2}] $ (used in audio-based human-robot interaction).
- **Band-Stop / Notch Filter**: Rejects a specific band (used to remove 50/60 Hz power-line interference).

### Stage 4: Sampling (Analog → Discrete-Time)
The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** dictates that to perfectly reconstruct a band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy:
$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
The minimum allowable rate $2f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. Failure to satisfy this causes **aliasing**, where high frequencies masquerade as low frequencies.

### Stage 5: Quantization (Discrete-Time → Digital)
Each sample is mapped to the nearest level of a finite set of $2^B$ discrete values, where $B$ is the bit-depth. The **quantization step size** is:
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^B - 1}$$
where $V_{FS}$ is the full-scale input voltage. The maximum theoretical **Quantization SNR** is:
$$\text{SNR}_Q \approx 6.02B + 1.76 \ \text{dB}$$

### Stage 6: Digital Processing and Reconstruction
Once digital, signals are processed by **DSPs (Digital Signal Processors)** or **MCUs** using operations like FFT, convolution, and digital filtering. The output can be:
- Used directly as a control input (e.g., velocity command).
- Converted back to analog via a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** for legacy actuators.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield equation you need to solve Module 1 numericals. Master these before attempting the KTU University Exam.

| Operation | Governing Equation | Boundary Condition / Unit | Robotics Application |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ in **Hz** | Sampling LiDAR / camera frames |
| Quantization Step | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^B - 1}$ | $V_{FS}$ in **Volts**, $B$ in **bits** | ADC resolution planning |
| Quantization SNR | $\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02B + 1.76$ | Result in **dB** | ADC selection for sensors |
| RC Low-Pass Cutoff | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in F | Filtering PWM motor noise |
| Convolution Sum | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$ | $n, k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | FIR filter implementation |
| Linear Correlation | $r_{xy}[l] = \sum_{n} x[n] \cdot y[n+l]$ | Lag $l$ in samples | Template matching in vision |
| DFT Definition | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | $N$ samples, $k \in [0, N-1]$ | Spectral analysis of vibration |
| Parseval's Theorem | $\sum_{n} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k} \vert X[k] \vert^2$ | Power equivalence | Energy-preserving transforms |
| dB Conversion | $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \right)$ | Voltage ratio → dB | Amplifier gain spec |
| Aliasing Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f - m f_s \vert$ | $m$ nearest integer | Identifying fold-over errors |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

In a humanoid robot, the IMU outputs a continuous analog voltage proportional to acceleration. Without proper signal processing, the robot's balance controller would receive a noisy, aliased signal and fall over. The canonical pipeline filters the noise, samples it at 1 kHz, quantizes it to 16 bits, and feeds the digital stream to a complementary filter or Kalman filter for pose estimation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Quantization SNR Formula

We model the quantization error $e_Q[n]$ as an additive, uniformly distributed, zero-mean random variable independent of the input signal $x[n]$.

**Step 1**: Define the quantization error as the difference between the actual sample and its quantized counterpart:
$$e_Q[n] = x[n] - x_Q[n]$$
where $x_Q[n]$ is the quantized value.

**Step 2**: The error $e_Q[n]$ is bounded within the interval $\left[ -\Delta/2, \ \Delta/2 \right]$, where $\Delta$ is the quantization step size derived from a mid-tread quantizer.

**Step 3**: Assume a uniform probability density function (PDF) for $e_Q[n]$ over this interval. The PDF is:
$$p(e_Q) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad \text{for} \quad e_Q \in \left[ -\frac{\Delta}{2}, \frac{\Delta}{2} \right]$$

**Step 4**: Compute the mean square value (variance) of the quantization noise. For a zero-mean uniform distribution:
$$\sigma_e^2 = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_Q^2 \cdot p(e_Q) \ de_Q$$
$$= \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_Q^2 \ de_Q$$

**Step 5**: Evaluate the integral. The antiderivative of $e_Q^2$ is $e_Q^3 / 3$:
$$\sigma_e^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_Q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2}$$
$$= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} \right)$$
$$= \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{2 \Delta^3}{24} \right)$$
$$= \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 6**: Express the signal power. Assume a full-scale sinusoid of peak amplitude $V_{FS}/2$. Its RMS power is:
$$P_{signal} = \left( \frac{V_{FS}}{2} \right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

**Step 7**: Relate $\Delta$ to $V_{FS}$ and bit-depth $B$. The total dynamic range $V_{FS}$ is divided into $2^B$ levels:
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^B}$$
Therefore:
$$\Delta^2 = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{2^{2B}}$$

**Step 8**: Substitute the noise power:
$$P_{noise} = \sigma_e^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}$$

**Step 9**: Compute the SNR in linear scale:
$$\text{SNR}_{linear} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{V_{FS}^2 / (12 \cdot 2^{2B})} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2B}}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2B}$$

**Step 10**: Convert to decibels using $10 \log_{10}$:
$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2B} \right)$$
$$= 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20B \log_{10}(2)$$
$$= 1.76 + 20B \cdot 0.30103$$
$$\approx 6.02B + 1.76 \ \text{dB}$$

This is the famous "6 dB per bit" rule. **Each additional bit of resolution improves the SNR by approximately 6.02 dB**, which corresponds to a doubling of the effective number of distinguishable signal levels.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Sampling Theorem

A robotic ultrasonic sensor emits and receives acoustic waves. The maximum useful frequency component in the received echo is **40 kHz**. The onboard ADC samples at **75 kHz**.

**Step 1**: Identify the maximum signal frequency:
$$f_{max} = 40 \ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the Nyquist Rate:
$$f_{nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 40 = 80 \ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 3**: Compare with actual sampling frequency:
$$f_s = 75 \ \text{kHz} < f_{nyquist} = 80 \ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 4**: Conclude the diagnosis:
Since $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, aliasing will occur. Specifically, the true signal at 40 kHz will fold over to a perceived frequency of:
$$f_{alias} = \vert 40 - 1 \cdot 75 \vert = 35 \ \text{kHz}$$

**Step 5**: Remediation: The sampling rate must be increased to at least 80 kHz, or an anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter with a cutoff at 37.5 kHz must precede the ADC to band-limit the input.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Robotic Signal Processing Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow of a typical signal in a robotics application, from physical phenomenon to control decision.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phyWorld[Physical World Phenomenon] --> sensor[Sensor Transducer]
    sensor --> cond[Signal Conditioning Amplification and Linearization]
    cond --> aaf[Anti Aliasing Analog LPF]
    aaf --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter Sampling and Quantization]
    adc --> dsp[Digital Signal Processor FFT Filter Convolution]
    dsp --> logic[Decision Logic Feature Extraction Classification]
    logic --> ctrl[Control Command Output]
    ctrl >>>
    ctrl --> actuator[Robot Actuator Motor Servo]
    actuator --> phyWorld

    noise[Environmental Noise and EMI] -. interferes .-> sensor
    noise -. interferes .-> cond

    classDef ana fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dig fill:#E0F2F1,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef phy fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class sensor,cond,aaf,adc,actuator ana
    class dsp,logic,ctrl dig
    class phyWorld,noise phy
```

## 4.2 Frequency Domain Operations Map

This flowchart shows how time-domain signals are transformed, filtered, and analyzed in the frequency domain for robotic perception tasks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    xn[Time Domain Signal x of n] --> fft[FFT Operation]
    fft --> Xk[Frequency Domain X of k]
    Xk --> win[Apply Windowing Function]
    win --> filt[Digital Filter Design FIR or IIR]
    filt --> ifft[Inverse FFT]
    ifft --> yn[Filtered Time Domain y of n]

    Xk --> spec[Spectral Feature Extraction]
    spec --> peaks[Peak Frequency and Magnitude]
    peaks --> dec[Robot Decision Making]

    classDef nodeA fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef nodeB fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef nodeC fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#000

    class xn,yn nodeA
    class Xk,win,filt nodeB
    class spec,peaks,dec,fft,ifft nodeC
```

## 4.3 Classification of Typical Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ops[Typical Signal Processing Operations] --> cont[Continuous Domain]
    ops --> disc[Discrete Domain]
    ops --> tran[Transform Domain]

    cont --> integ[Integration]
    cont --> diff[Differentiation]
    cont --> filtA[Analog Filtering RC Active]

    disc --> convOp[Convolution]
    disc --> corrOp[Correlation]
    disc --> filtD[Digital Filtering FIR IIR]

    tran --> fftOp[FFT DFT]
    tran --> zOp[Z Transform]
    tran --> lap[Laplace Transform]

    classDef root fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef sub fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#F9FBE7,stroke:#827717,color:#000

    class ops root
    class cont,disc,tran sub
    class integ,diff,filtA,convOp,corrOp,filtD,fftOp,zOp,lap leaf
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Sampling Theorem Definition `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_{max}$ Hz can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples $x[n]$ if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies the condition $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum sampling rate of $2 f_{max}$ Hz is called the **Nyquist Rate**. Sampling below this rate causes **aliasing**, in which high-frequency components are incorrectly mapped to lower frequencies, causing irreversible information loss. In robotics, this principle governs the design of ADC sampling rates for sensors like microphones, IMUs, and cameras.

### Question 2: Quantization Error `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Quantization error $e_Q$ is the difference between the actual analog sample value and its nearest representable digital value:
$$e_Q = x[n] - x_Q[n]$$
The maximum magnitude of the error is bounded by half the quantization step size:
$$\vert e_Q \vert \leq \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{B+1}}$$
It is modeled as an additive white noise source uniformly distributed over $\left[ -\Delta/2, \Delta/2 \right]$, and its variance is $\sigma_e^2 = \Delta^2 / 12$. This error directly limits the dynamic range of a digital system.

---

## Part B Question A (14 Marks Total)

### `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` 

### (a) Describe the typical signal processing operations performed in a robotics system with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A robotic system acquires information from the physical world through a structured pipeline of signal processing operations:

**1. Signal Acquisition (Transduction):** A physical quantity such as light intensity, acoustic pressure, or acceleration is converted into a proportional electrical signal by a sensor. Examples include photodiodes for vision, MEMS accelerometers for IMUs, and encoders for motor position.

**2. Signal Conditioning:** The raw transducer signal is typically low in amplitude (mV range) and contaminated with noise. Operational amplifiers raise the signal level to the input range of the ADC. Isolation circuits protect downstream electronics.

**3. Anti-Aliasing Filtering:** A continuous-time analog low-pass filter with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s/2$ is applied. This prevents high-frequency noise from folding back into the baseband due to aliasing.

**4. Sampling and Quantization (ADC):** The filtered analog signal is sampled at frequency $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. Each sample is mapped to one of $2^B$ discrete digital codes, producing the final digital signal $x_Q[n]$.

**5. Digital Processing:** A DSP or MCU applies discrete operations such as digital filtering, FFT, correlation, and feature extraction to extract meaningful information.

**6. Decision and Control:** The processed information is fed to a control algorithm (PID, state machine, neural network) which generates command signals.

**7. Actuation and Reconstruction:** The digital commands drive actuators (motors, servos). If a continuous actuator is used, a DAC reconstructs the analog waveform from the digital stream.

```
Physical World -> Sensor -> Conditioner -> Anti-Alias LPF -> ADC -> DSP -> Controller -> DAC -> Actuator -> Physical World
```

### (b) A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize a sensor signal with a full-scale voltage of 5 V. Calculate (i) the quantization step size and (ii) the theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Bit-depth $B = 12$ bits, Full-scale voltage $V_{FS} = 5$ V.

**(i) Quantization Step Size:**
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^B - 1} = \frac{5}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{5}{4095}$$
$$\Delta \approx 1.221 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{V} = 1.221 \ \text{mV}$$

**(ii) Theoretical SNR in dB:**
$$\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02B + 1.76 \ \text{dB}$$
$$\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76$$
$$\text{SNR}_Q = 72.24 + 1.76$$
$$\text{SNR}_Q = 74.0 \ \text{dB}$$

This means the digitizer provides approximately **74 dB** of dynamic range, sufficient for most robotic sensing tasks such as IMU and ultrasonic ranging.

---

## Part B Question B (14 Marks Total)

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### (a) With suitable mathematical expressions, explain the operations of sampling, quantization, and reconstruction in a signal processing system. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Sampling:** Converts a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by measuring the amplitude at uniform intervals $T_s = 1/f_s$:
$$x[n] = x(n T_s), \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$
The sampling theorem requires $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to prevent aliasing. The sampled signal in impulse-train form is:
$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(n T_s) \delta(t - n T_s)$$

**Quantization:** Maps each sample to the nearest of $2^B$ discrete levels:
$$x_Q[n] = \Delta \cdot \text{round}\left( \frac{x[n]}{\Delta} \right)$$
The quantization step is $\Delta = V_{FS} / 2^B$, and the introduced error $e_Q = x[n] - x_Q[n]$ is bounded by $\vert e_Q \vert \leq \Delta / 2$.

**Reconstruction:** A DAC converts the digital stream back to analog by holding each sample value for one sampling period (zero-order hold). Ideal reconstruction via a low-pass filter recovers $x(t)$ using the Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula:
$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \cdot \text{sinc}\left( \frac{t - n T_s}{T_s} \right)$$
where $\text{sinc}(u) = \sin(\pi u) / (\pi u)$.

### (b) A robotic audio sensor captures speech signals with a maximum frequency of 4 kHz. The ADC operates at 10 kHz with 8-bit resolution and a 3.3 V full-scale range. Determine if the system is properly designed. If not, suggest corrections. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f_{max} = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 10$ kHz, $B = 8$ bits, $V_{FS} = 3.3$ V.

**Step 1: Check Sampling Rate (Nyquist Criterion):**
$$f_{nyquist} = 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \ \text{kHz}$$
Since $f_s = 10 \ \text{kHz} \geq 8 \ \text{kHz}$, the sampling criterion is satisfied. **Sampling is adequate.**

**Step 2: Check Resolution and SNR:**
$$\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \ \text{dB}$$
$$\Delta = \frac{3.3}{2^8 - 1} = \frac{3.3}{255} \approx 0.01294 \ \text{V} = 12.94 \ \text{mV}$$

**Step 3: Practical Verdict:**
While the sampling rate is theoretically sufficient, 8-bit resolution yields only ~50 dB SNR, which is **borderline for clean speech recognition** in robotics. Most modern voice-command systems require at least 12 bits (74 dB SNR).

**Step 4: Suggested Corrections:**
1. Increase ADC resolution to **12 bits** to raise SNR to 74 dB.
2. Increase sampling frequency to **at least 16 kHz** (4 times oversampling) to provide a guard band and relax the anti-aliasing filter design.
3. Insert an analog anti-aliasing low-pass filter with cutoff at 4 kHz before the ADC.
4. Consider a Sigma-Delta audio codec for superior noise performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the $2^B - 1$ denominator** in the quantization step formula (it should be $2^B - 1$, not $2^B$).
> - **Confusing $\text{SNR}_Q = 6.02B + 1.76$ dB** (signal power) with the alternate form $1.76 + 6.02B$ — both are correct, but writing $1.76B$ will cost you full marks.
> - **Mixing up Hz and rad/s** in filter cutoff formulas; KTU expects $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ in **Hz**.
> - **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter** when justifying sampling choices in Part B derivations.
> - **Skipping units in the final answer** (always write kHz, V, dB).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Signal**: A time-varying quantity carrying information; classified as analog, discrete, or digital.
- **Typical Pipeline**: Sensor → Amplifier → Anti-Alias Filter → ADC → DSP → Controller → DAC → Actuator.
- **Nyquist Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ is the fundamental rule. Violating it causes **aliasing**.
- **Quantization Step**: $\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^B - 1)$; smaller $\Delta$ = higher fidelity.
- **6 dB Rule**: Each extra bit of ADC resolution improves SNR by **6.02 dB**.
- **Filtering Classes**: LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF — implemented using analog RC circuits or digital FIR/IIR structures.
- **Convolution**: $y[n] = \sum_k x[k] h[n-k]$ is the core of linear digital filtering.
- **Correlation**: $r_{xy}[l] = \sum_n x[n] y[n+l]$ measures similarity; used in template matching for robot vision.
- **DFT/FFT**: Converts time-domain to frequency-domain; $X[k] = \sum_n x[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$.
- **Parseval's Theorem**: Energy is conserved between time and frequency domains.
- **Decibels**: Always $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage ratios, $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios.
- **Anti-Aliasing**: Always place an analog LPF with $f_c = f_s/2$ before the ADC.
- **Reconstruction**: A DAC plus low-pass filter recovers the analog signal from digital samples.
- **Robotic Context**: This pipeline is the foundation of sensor fusion, computer vision, speech recognition, and motor control in modern robots.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Example of Typical Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Example of Typical Signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Typical Signal"?

A **signal** is a mathematical function that conveys information about the state or behavior of a physical system, typically as a function of one or more independent variables (most commonly time `t` or spatial coordinates). In the context of **Signal Processing for Robotics**, signals are the fundamental language through which sensors (LIDAR, IMU, encoders, cameras, microphones) communicate with controllers and processors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCPT402 - Module 1):**
> A **typical (or standard) signal** is a deterministic, mathematically well-defined waveform — such as the unit impulse, unit step, ramp, exponential, sinusoidal, complex exponential, signum, sinc, rectangular, or triangular pulse — that is used as a *building block* to represent, model, and analyze more complex real-world signals encountered in robotic systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The LEGO Blocks of Robotics"

Imagine a robotics engineer trying to describe the jolt produced when a robotic arm suddenly halts (emergency stop), the steady push of a conveyor belt motor, the smooth sinusoidal sweep of a servo motor's reference trajectory, or the exponentially decaying vibration of a flexible manipulator arm. None of these are "random" — they can each be described by a **standard mathematical function**. Just as **LEGO bricks** combine to form intricate structures, these standard signals (impulse, step, ramp, exponential, sinusoid, etc.) combine via operations like scaling, shifting, convolution, and superposition to construct *any* real-world robotic signal.

### 1.3 Classification of Typical Signals

| Category | Examples | Robotic Use Case |
|----------|----------|------------------|
| **Singularity-type** | Unit Impulse δ(t) | Sudden impact (collision detection) |
| **Singularity-free** | Step u(t), Ramp r(t) | Motor ON/OFF, constant acceleration profile |
| **Decaying/Growing** | Exponential e^(at) | Battery discharge, thermal sensor drift |
| **Periodic** | Sinusoid sin(ωt) | Joint oscillation, AC motor reference |
| **Rotational/Phasor** | Complex exponential e^(jωt) | Frequency-domain analysis of gait |
| **Piecewise** | Signum, Rectangular, Triangular | PWM motor control pulses |
| **Band-limited** | Sinc function | Digital reconstruction of LIDAR scan |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Robotics:** Every actuator command, every sensor reading, and every control error signal in a robot can ultimately be decomposed into a weighted sum (or integral) of these elementary typical signals. Mastering them is equivalent to learning the alphabet before writing sentences.

### 1.4 The Standard Library of Typical Signals (Formal Definitions)

Let `t ∈ ℝ` denote continuous time. We define the following canonical signals:

**1. Unit Impulse Signal δ(t):**
- δ(t) = 0 for t ≠ 0
- ∫₋∞^{+∞} δ(t) dt = 1
- It is *not* a conventional function — it is a **distribution** (generalized function).

**2. Unit Step Signal u(t):**
- u(t) = 0 for t < 0
- u(t) = 1 for t > 0
- At t = 0, conventionally u(0) = 1/2 (for continuous-time LTI analysis).

**3. Unit Ramp Signal r(t):**
- r(t) = t for t ≥ 0
- r(t) = 0 for t < 0

**4. Exponential Signal:**
- x(t) = A · e^(at), with A, a ∈ ℝ (or ℂ)
- If a < 0 → decaying (stable, e.g., damped vibration)
- If a > 0 → growing (unstable, e.g., thermal runaway)

**5. Sinusoidal Signal:**
- x(t) = A cos(ω₀t + φ)  *or*  A sin(ω₀t + φ)
- A = amplitude, ω₀ = angular frequency (rad/s), φ = phase (rad)

**6. Complex Exponential Signal:**
- x(t) = e^(σt) · e^(jω₀t) = e^((σ + jω₀)t)

**7. Signum Function sgn(t):**
- sgn(t) = -1 for t < 0
- sgn(t) = 0 for t = 0
- sgn(t) = +1 for t > 0

**8. Rectangular Pulse rect(t/T):**
- rect(t/T) = 1 for \vert t \vert < T/2
- rect(t/T) = 0 for \vert t \vert > T/2
- rect(t/T) = 1/2 at \vert t \vert = T/2

**9. Triangular Pulse tri(t/T):**
- tri(t/T) = 1 − \vert t \vert/T for \vert t \vert ≤ T
- tri(t/T) = 0 for \vert t \vert > T

**10. Sinc Function:**
- sinc(t) = sin(πt) / (πt) for t ≠ 0
- sinc(0) = 1

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why These Signals Are "Typical" — The Engineering Rationale

A signal earns "typical" status if it satisfies at least one of three properties:
1. **It is an eigenfunction of LTI systems** (sinusoid in, sinusoid out — only amplitude/phase change).
2. **It serves as a basis function** (impulse, complex exponential) for representing arbitrary signals.
3. **It physically models a ubiquitous real-world phenomenon** (step ≈ switch closure, ramp ≈ constant force, exponential ≈ RC/RL discharge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Concept:** The complex exponential `e^(jω₀t)` is the *most important* typical signal in robotics signal processing. Any periodic or aperiodic signal can be represented as a continuous superposition (Fourier Integral) of complex exponentials of different frequencies. This is the gateway to Fourier/Laplace/Z-transforms covered later in Module 2/3.

## 2.2 Mathematical & Physical Properties

### 2.2.1 Unit Impulse δ(t) — Properties

- **Sifting property:** ∫₋∞^{+∞} x(t) δ(t − t₀) dt = x(t₀)
- **Sampling property:** x(t) δ(t − t₀) = x(t₀) δ(t − t₀)
- **Time-scaling:** δ(at) = (1/\vert a \vert) δ(t)
- **Even symmetry:** δ(−t) = δ(t)
- **Derivative relationship with step:** δ(t) = du(t)/dt (in the distributional sense)
- **Integral relationship with step:** u(t) = ∫₋∞^{t} δ(τ) dτ

### 2.2.2 Unit Step u(t) — Properties

- **Time-shifted step:** u(t − t₀) = 1 for t > t₀
- **Ramp from step:** r(t) = ∫₋∞^{t} u(τ) dτ = t · u(t)
- **Signum from steps:** sgn(t) = 2u(t) − 1
- **Rectangular pulse from steps:** rect(t/T) = u(t + T/2) − u(t − T/2)

### 2.2.3 Sinusoidal & Complex Exponential — Properties

- **Euler's formula:** e^(jω₀t) = cos(ω₀t) + j sin(ω₀t)
- **Real sinusoid from complex exponentials:** cos(ω₀t) = (e^(jω₀t) + e^(−jω₀t)) / 2
- **Periodicity:** A signal is periodic with period T₀ if x(t + T₀) = x(t) ∀t; fundamental frequency f₀ = 1/T₀; angular frequency ω₀ = 2π/T₀.
- **Average power:** P = (1/T₀) ∫_{T₀} \vert x(t) \vert² dt

### 2.2.4 Exponential Signal — Properties

- **Time constant τ = 1/\vert a \vert** (for a < 0); signal decays to 36.8% of its initial value after one time constant.
- **Relationship to first-order system response:** x(t) = X₀ e^(−t/τ) for RC or RL circuits in robotics power supplies.

### 2.2.5 Energy vs. Power Signals

- **Energy signal:** 0 < E = ∫₋∞^{+∞} \vert x(t) \vert² dt < ∞ (e.g., decaying exponential, single pulse)
- **Power signal:** 0 < P = lim_{T→∞} (1/2T) ∫_{−T}^{+T} \vert x(t) \vert² dt < ∞ (e.g., sinusoid, step)
- **Neither:** δ(t) — infinite amplitude but zero energy; theoretically classified as neither energy nor power signal in the strict sense.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Signal | Mathematical Definition | Energy (E) or Power (P) | Robotic Application |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Unit Impulse δ(t) | δ(t) = 0, t ≠ 0; ∫δ dt = 1 | Neither (distribution) | Impulse collision, sampling |
| Unit Step u(t) | u(t) = 0 (t<0); 1 (t>0) | P = 1/2 (avg) | Relay switching, motor ON |
| Unit Ramp r(t) | r(t) = t · u(t) | Power signal | Constant acceleration profile |
| Exponential e^(at) | x(t) = e^(at) u(t) | E = 1/(2\vert a \vert) if a<0 | RC discharge, vibration decay |
| Sinusoid A cos(ω₀t) | A cos(ω₀t + φ) | P = A²/2 | Joint oscillation, AC reference |
| Complex Exp. e^(jω₀t) | e^(jω₀t) | P = 1 | Phasor, eigenfunction |
| Signum sgn(t) | −1 (t<0); +1 (t>0) | Power (P = 1) | Thresholded direction signal |
| Rectangular rect(t/T) | 1 for \vert t \vert < T/2 | E = T | PWM pulse, gating window |
| Triangular tri(t/T) | 1 − \vert t \vert/T for \vert t \vert ≤ T | E = 2T/3 | Trapezoidal velocity profile |
| Sinc sinc(t) | sin(πt)/(πt) | Energy signal | Ideal interpolation kernel |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always write the **time support** (e.g., "for t ≥ 0", "for \vert t \vert ≤ T/2") explicitly in your definitions. KTU examiners award a half-mark for omitting the domain of definition.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Robotics

| Robotic Subsystem | Typical Signal Used | Purpose |
|-------------------|---------------------|---------|
| Wheeled mobile robot (encoder) | Impulse train | Counting wheel revolutions |
| Robotic gripper (force sensor) | Exponential decay | Impact force dissipation model |
| Servo motor controller | PWM rectangular pulse | Speed/torque command |
| Quadruped robot (gait planner) | Sinusoidal reference | Smooth leg trajectory |
| IMU (gyroscope) | Sum of sinusoids | Vibration analysis via FFT |
| LIDAR return | Modulated sinc pulse | Range measurement |
| PID controller step response | u(t) and δ(t) | Stability/tuning analysis |
| Battery voltage profile | Decaying exponential | State-of-charge estimation |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship between Unit Impulse and Unit Step

We begin with the rectangular pulse approximation of the unit impulse. Consider a rectangular pulse of width ε and height 1/ε centered at t = 0:

$$
p_\varepsilon(t) = \begin{cases} 1/\varepsilon, & \vert t \vert \leq \varepsilon/2 \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > \varepsilon/2 \end{cases}
$$

The area of this pulse is always unity:

$$
A = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p_\varepsilon(t) \, dt = \int_{-\varepsilon/2}^{+\varepsilon/2} \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \, dt = \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \cdot \varepsilon = 1
$$

As ε → 0, the pulse becomes infinitesimally narrow and infinitely tall, while its area remains 1. In the limit, we obtain the unit impulse:

$$
\delta(t) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} p_\varepsilon(t)
$$

The corresponding step approximation is the integral of the rectangular pulse. For t < −ε/2, the integral is 0. For t > ε/2, the integral saturates at 1. In the transition region −ε/2 < t < ε/2, the integral rises linearly from 0 to 1. Taking the limit ε → 0 yields the discontinuous unit step:

$$
u(t) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} \int_{-\infty}^{t} p_\varepsilon(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

Differentiating this in the distributional sense (i.e., ignoring the measure-zero set at t = 0) gives:

$$
\frac{du(t)}{dt} = \delta(t)
$$

Conversely, integrating δ(t) from −∞ up to an arbitrary time t reproduces the step:

$$
u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

> **Interpretation:** The unit step is the *cumulative* (running integral) of the unit impulse, while the unit impulse is the *instantaneous rate of change* (derivative) of the unit step. This duality is the cornerstone of LTI system analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Euler's Identity and the Phasor Representation

Start with the Taylor series of e^(jω₀t) about t = 0:

$$
e^{j\omega_0 t} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(j\omega_0 t)^n}{n!}
$$

Separating even and odd powers of n:

$$
e^{j\omega_0 t} = \sum_{n \text{ even}} \frac{(j\omega_0 t)^n}{n!} + \sum_{n \text{ odd}} \frac{(j\omega_0 t)^n}{n!}
$$

Note that j² = −1, j⁴ = 1, etc., so even powers of j alternate between +1 and −1 with period 4, yielding the real cosine series. Odd powers carry one extra factor of j, yielding the imaginary sine series. Combining:

$$
e^{j\omega_0 t} = \left( 1 - \frac{\omega_0^2 t^2}{2!} + \frac{\omega_0^4 t^4}{4!} - \cdots \right) + j\left( \omega_0 t - \frac{\omega_0^3 t^3}{3!} + \cdots \right)
$$

Recognising the two bracketed series as cos(ω₀t) and sin(ω₀t):

$$
e^{j\omega_0 t} = \cos(\omega_0 t) + j \sin(\omega_0 t)
$$

This is **Euler's formula**. Replacing t by −t gives e^(−jω₀t) = cos(ω₀t) − j sin(ω₀t). Adding and subtracting the two:

$$
\cos(\omega_0 t) = \frac{e^{j\omega_0 t} + e^{-j\omega_0 t}}{2}, \qquad \sin(\omega_0 t) = \frac{e^{j\omega_0 t} - e^{-j\omega_0 t}}{2j}
$$

> **Robotic Implication:** A cosine trajectory (e.g., smooth joint motion) is mathematically a sum of two *counter-rotating phasors* in the complex frequency plane. This viewpoint is the entry ticket to Fourier and Laplace transforms.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy of a Decaying Exponential

Consider x(t) = e^(−at) u(t) with a > 0. By definition of energy:

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{+\infty} e^{-2at} \, dt
$$

Evaluating the improper integral:

$$
E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \int_{0}^{T} e^{-2at} \, dt = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left[ \frac{e^{-2at}}{-2a} \right]_{0}^{T}
$$

Substituting the limits:

$$
E = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( \frac{e^{-2aT}}{-2a} - \frac{1}{-2a} \right) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( -\frac{e^{-2aT}}{2a} + \frac{1}{2a} \right)
$$

Since a > 0, e^(−2aT) → 0 as T → ∞, so:

$$
E = 0 + \frac{1}{2a} = \frac{1}{2a}
$$

Generalising to amplitude A: if x(t) = A e^(−at) u(t), then E = A² / (2a).

> **Physical meaning:** A *faster* decay (larger a) means the signal is concentrated near t = 0, hence carries less total energy. A *slower* decay (smaller a) means the signal lingers, accumulating more energy.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Time-Scaling Property of the Unit Impulse

Let δ_a(t) = δ(at) with a ≠ 0. We seek the constant k such that:

$$
\delta(at) = k \cdot \delta(t)
$$

Apply the sifting property to an arbitrary test function x(t):

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, \delta(at) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau/a) \, \delta(\tau) \, \frac{d\tau}{|a|} = \frac{x(0)}{|a|}
$$

(Here we substituted τ = at, so dt = dτ / \vert a \vert, and used the sifting property.) The last expression equals (1 / \vert a \vert) times the result of sifting x(t) with δ(t). Therefore:

$$
\delta(at) = \frac{1}{|a|} \delta(t)
$$

> **Special case a = −1:** δ(−t) = δ(t) — the impulse is an **even** function of time, a result that the KTU examiner expects students to recall instantly.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Average Power of a Sinusoid

Let x(t) = A cos(ω₀t + φ). Average power over one period T₀ = 2π / ω₀:

$$
P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt
$$

Using the trigonometric identity cos²θ = (1 + cos 2θ) / 2:

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt
$$

The integral of cos(2ω₀t + 2φ) over a full period T₀ = 2π / ω₀ is exactly zero (it completes two full cycles). So:

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{2 T_0} \cdot T_0 = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

> **Result:** The average power of a sinusoid depends *only* on its amplitude, not on its frequency or phase. This is why RMS (root mean square) amplitude A / √2 is the standard figure-of-merit in AC servomotor specifications.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| # | Property/Identity | Statement | Use in Robotics |
|---|-------------------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | δ(−t) = δ(t) | Impulse is even | Symmetric collision analysis |
| 2 | δ(at) = (1/\vert a \vert) δ(t) | Time-scaling | Wheel-encoder scaling |
| 3 | x(t)δ(t − t₀) = x(t₀) δ(t − t₀) | Sampling | Sample-and-hold |
| 4 | u(t) = ∫δ dτ | Step from impulse | Switching transients |
| 5 | e^(jω₀t) = cos ω₀t + j sin ω₀t | Euler's identity | Phasor/frequency analysis |
| 6 | cos ω₀t = (e^(jω₀t) + e^(−jω₀t))/2 | Real from complex | Fourier series |
| 7 | Energy of e^(−at)u(t) = 1/(2a) | Energy formula | Vibration damping |
| 8 | Power of A cos(ω₀t) = A²/2 | Sinusoid power | Servo current rating |
| 9 | r(t) = t · u(t) | Ramp from step | Constant-accel motion |
| 10 | sgn(t) = 2u(t) − 1 | Signum from step | Direction control logic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — "How Typical Signals Enter a Robot"

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the *information flow* by which typical signals are generated, sensed, and processed within a typical robotic system (autonomous mobile robot). It is used in lieu of a literal waveform plot since Mermaid's flow notation is more precise and accessible than hand-drawn sketches for educational reproduction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[actuatorCommand]:::src --> B[signalGenerator]:::gen
    B --> C1[unitStep u of t]:::typ
    B --> C2[unitImpulse delta of t]:::typ
    B --> C3[ramp r of t]:::typ
    B --> C4[exponential e to power at]:::typ
    B --> C5[sinusoid A cos omega t]:::typ
    B --> C6[complexExp e to power j omega t]:::typ
    C1 --> D[transmissionChannel]:::chan
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E1[sensorEncoder]:::sns
    D --> E2[IMUaccelerometer]:::sns
    D --> E3[forceTorqueSensor]:::sns
    D --> E4[LIDARreturn]:::sns
    E1 --> F[signalConditioningBlock]:::cond
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[analogToDigitalConverter]:::adc
    G --> H[robotProcessor]:::proc
    H --> I1[controlAlgorithm]:::ctrl
    H --> I2[pathPlanner]:::plan
    H --> I3[machineLearner]:::ml
    I1 --> J[motionActuator]:::act
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[physicalEnvironment]:::env
    K -->|feedback| A

    classDef src fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef typ fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    classDef chan fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    classDef sns fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#a04000,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
    classDef adc fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#9a7d0a,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5276,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef plan fill:#a3e4d7,stroke:#117864,color:#000
    classDef ml fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
    classDef env fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
```

> **How to read this diagram:** Starting from the *actuator command* (left), a signal generator produces one or more typical waveforms (green box). These traverse a *channel* (which may add noise), are picked up by *sensors* (orange), conditioned (filtering/amplification), digitised by an ADC, and finally consumed by the robot's processor for *control, planning, or learning*. The closed loop returns to the actuator via the physical environment.

## 4.2 Signal Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[typicalSignals]:::root --> B[continuousTime]:::ct
    A --> C[discreteTime]:::dt
    B --> D[energySignals]:::en
    B --> E[powerSignals]:::pw
    B --> F[neitherEnergyNorPower]:::np
    D --> D1[decayingExponential]:::en
    D --> D2[rectangularPulse]:::en
    D --> D3[sincFunction]:::en
    D --> D4[triangularPulse]:::en
    E --> E1[unitStep]:::pw
    E --> E2[sinusoid]:::pw
    E --> E3[unitRamp]:::pw
    E --> E4[signumFunction]:::pw
    F --> F1[unitImpulse]:::np
    C --> G[discreteImpulse delta n]:::dt
    C --> H[discreteStep u of n]:::dt
    C --> I[discreteExponential a to power n]:::dt
    C --> J[discreteSinusoid cos omega n]:::dt

    classDef root fill:#34495e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ct fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5276,color:#000
    classDef dt fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    classDef en fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    classDef pw fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    classDef np fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
```

> **Pedagogical note:** This classification tree aligns with the KTU Module 1 learning outcome *"Classify continuous-time signals based on energy and power criteria."* The student should be able to draw this tree from memory.

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping — Typical Signal → Robotic Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[unitImpulse]:::typ --> B1[collisionDetection]:::app
    A2[unitStep]:::typ --> B2[motorEngagement]:::app
    A3[unitRamp]:::typ --> B3[constantAccelTrajectory]:::app
    A4[exponentialDecay]:::typ --> B4[batteryDischarge]:::app
    A5[sinusoid]:::typ --> B5[jointOscillation]:::app
    A6[complexExp]:::typ --> B6[frequencyDomainAnalysis]:::app
    A7[rectangularPulse]:::typ --> B7[PWMCommand]:::app
    A8[triangularPulse]:::typ --> B8[trapezoidalVelocityProfile]:::app
    A9[sincFunction]:::typ --> B9[LIDARRangefinding]:::app
    A10[signumFunction]:::typ --> B10[directionalControl]:::app

    classDef typ fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
```

> **Reading aid:** Each typical signal (left, green) is mapped to its most direct robotic application (right, blue). This is the "cheat-sheet" mental image to recall during the exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper, PCCPT402]**
*Define the unit impulse signal δ(t). State and prove its sifting property.*

**Model Answer:**

The unit impulse signal δ(t), also called the Dirac delta function, is a generalized function (distribution) defined by two properties:

- (i) δ(t) = 0 for all t ≠ 0
- (ii) ∫₋∞^{+∞} δ(t) dt = 1

It can be viewed as the limit of a rectangular pulse of width ε → 0 and height 1/ε, while preserving unit area.

**Sifting Property (Proof):** Consider ∫₋∞^{+∞} x(t) δ(t − t₀) dt. Since δ(t − t₀) is non-zero only at t = t₀, and assuming x(t) is continuous at t₀, we can replace x(t) by its value at t₀ within the integral:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) \, \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0) \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - t_0) \, dt = x(t_0) \cdot 1 = x(t_0)
$$

This "sifts out" the value of x(t) at t = t₀, hence the name. In robotic signal processing, this property underlies the impulse-sampling of continuous signals into discrete-time sequences for digital control. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam, PCCPT402]**
*Differentiate between energy and power signals. Classify the following signals: (a) x(t) = e^(−2t) u(t), (b) x(t) = 5 cos(10t + π/3).*

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Energy Signal | Power Signal |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| Energy E | 0 < E < ∞ (finite, non-zero) | E = ∞ |
| Power P | P = 0 | 0 < P < ∞ (finite, non-zero) |
| Typical examples | Decaying pulse, sinc | Sinusoid, step |
| Physical meaning | Transient, decays fully | Persistent, average power exists |

**Classification:**

**(a) x(t) = e^(−2t) u(t):**
$$
E = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt = \frac{1}{4} \quad \text{(finite)}
$$
Since E is finite and non-zero, P = 0. Hence it is an **energy signal**. **(1.5 marks)**

**(b) x(t) = 5 cos(10t + π/3):**
$$
P = \frac{A^2}{2} = \frac{5^2}{2} = 12.5 \quad \text{(finite)}
$$
Since 0 < P < ∞, it is a **power signal**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 1, PCCPT402]**

**(a)** Define the unit step signal u(t). Show that the unit ramp signal r(t) can be obtained by integrating u(t). Sketch both signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the unit impulse δ(t) and the unit step u(t). Hence obtain the energy of the signal x(t) = e^(−3t) u(t) and state whether it is an energy or power signal. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Unit Step and Unit Ramp — Definition, Integration, and Sketch**

The unit step signal u(t) is defined as:

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t > 0 \end{cases}
$$

For continuous-time LTI analysis, the value at t = 0 is taken as u(0) = 1/2. The step represents an instantaneous transition from 0 to 1 at t = 0, modelling a sudden event such as a relay closing in a robot's power circuit.

The unit ramp signal r(t) is defined as:

$$
r(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ t, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Deriving r(t) by integrating u(t):** Integrate the step from −∞ to an arbitrary time t:

$$
r(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} u(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

For t < 0, u(τ) = 0 throughout the integration range, so the integral is 0. For t ≥ 0, u(τ) jumps to 1 at τ = 0 and stays there. The integration from −∞ to 0 contributes 0, and from 0 to t contributes (1 · t) = t. Thus:

$$
r(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ t, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

which matches the definition. In compact notation, r(t) = t · u(t). **(5 marks for derivation + definition)**

**Sketch (textual description for the student to draw):**
- u(t): horizontal line at 0 for t < 0, vertical jump at t = 0, horizontal line at 1 for t > 0.
- r(t): horizontal line at 0 for t < 0, straight line of slope 1 through the origin for t > 0. **(2 marks for sketch)**

---

**(b) Impulse–Step Duality and Energy of an Exponential**

We have already established (Section 3.1) that:

$$
u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau) \, d\tau \quad \text{and} \quad \delta(t) = \frac{du(t)}{dt}
$$

in the distributional sense. **(2 marks)**

**Energy of x(t) = e^(−3t) u(t):** Apply the energy formula:

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-6t} \, dt
$$

Evaluating:

$$
E = \left[ \frac{e^{-6t}}{-6} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \left( -\frac{e^{-6T}}{6} + \frac{1}{6} \right) = 0 + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{1}{6} \text{ Joules}
$$

Since 0 < E = 1/6 < ∞, the signal is an **energy signal**. **(5 marks for the energy calculation + classification)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam, Module 1, PCCPT402]**

**(a)** Using Euler's formula, express the sinusoid A cos(ω₀t + φ) as a sum of complex exponentials. Hence compute its average power. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the signum function sgn(t) and express it in terms of unit step signals. A rectangular pulse of unit amplitude extends from t = −2 to t = 2 seconds. Express it using step functions and compute its total energy. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Sinusoid as Complex Exponentials and its Average Power**

Euler's formula states:

$$
e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta}}{2}
$$

Applying this with θ = ω₀t + φ:

$$
A \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) = \frac{A}{2} \left[ e^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi)} + e^{-j(\omega_0 t + \phi)} \right] = \frac{A}{2} e^{j\phi} e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2} e^{-j\phi} e^{-j\omega_0 t}
$$

This expresses the real sinusoid as the sum of two counter-rotating complex exponentials of amplitudes (A/2)e^(jφ) and (A/2)e^(−jφ). **(3 marks)**

**Average Power:** The average power of a periodic signal with period T₀ = 2π / ω₀ is:

$$
P = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} A^2 \cos^2(\omega_0 t + \phi) \, dt
$$

Using cos²(θ) = (1 + cos 2θ) / 2:

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \frac{1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi)}{2} \, dt = \frac{A^2}{2 T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} \left( 1 + \cos(2\omega_0 t + 2\phi) \right) dt
$$

The cosine term integrates to 0 over a complete period (it completes two full cycles), leaving:

$$
P = \frac{A^2}{2 T_0} \cdot T_0 = \frac{A^2}{2}
$$

The average power is A²/2, independent of ω₀ and φ. **(4 marks)**

---

**(b) Signum Function, Rectangular Pulse, and its Energy**

The signum (sign) function is defined as:

$$
\operatorname{sgn}(t) = \begin{cases} -1, & t < 0 \\ 0, & t = 0 \\ +1, & t > 0 \end{cases}
$$

In terms of unit step u(t), observe that 2u(t) − 1 equals −1 for t < 0 (since u = 0) and equals +1 for t > 0 (since u = 1). Thus:

$$
\operatorname{sgn}(t) = 2u(t) - 1
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Rectangular pulse from t = −2 to t = 2:** A unit-amplitude pulse active on the interval [−2, 2] is the difference of two step functions — one "turning on" at t = −2 and one "turning off" at t = 2:

$$
x(t) = u(t + 2) - u(t - 2)
$$

To verify: for t < −2, both steps are 0, so x = 0. For −2 ≤ t < 2, the first step is 1 and the second is 0, so x = 1. For t > 2, both steps are 1, so x = 0. This is the desired pulse. **(2 marks)**

**Energy:** The pulse is non-zero only on [−2, 2] with unit amplitude, so:

$$
E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-2}^{2} 1^2 \, dt = [t]_{-2}^{2} = 2 - (-2) = 4 \text{ Joules}
$$

**(3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Signal Definition Module):**
> 1. **Forgetting the time support.** Writing "x(t) = e^(−3t)" without the factor u(t) is incorrect — the signal must be explicitly defined for all t ∈ ℝ. *Loss: 0.5 to 1 mark per instance.*
> 2. **Confusing continuous-time and discrete-time notation.** δ(t) and u(t) are continuous; δ[n] and u[n] are discrete. Mixing them is a frequent error. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 3. **Writing ∣t∣ instead of \vert t \vert in the exam sheet.** Use the modulus bars (with proper LaTeX) and never forget the absolute-value sign for even functions. *Loss: 0.5 mark.*
> 4. **Omitting the sifting property of δ(t).** It is a "favourite" 3-mark question; the integral must be evaluated explicitly. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 5. **Sign errors in Euler's formula.** The conversion `cos θ = (e^{jθ} + e^{−jθ}) / 2` is correct; the conversion for `sin θ` has `2j` in the denominator (not `2`). *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 6. **Declaring δ(t) as an "energy signal" or "power signal".** It is neither (it is a distribution). *Loss: 1 mark.*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Impulse δ(t):** zero everywhere except t = 0; integral = 1; *even function* δ(−t) = δ(t); *sifting property* picks out the value of any continuous function at t₀; *derivative of step* and *integral of step*; time-scaling δ(at) = (1/\vert a \vert) δ(t).
- **Step u(t):** 0 for t < 0, 1 for t > 0; the *integral of impulse*; shifting u(t − t₀) "switches on" at t₀; the *building block* of rectangular and other piecewise signals.
- **Ramp r(t):** t · u(t); slope 1 for t ≥ 0; *integral of step*; used for constant-acceleration motion profiles.
- **Exponential e^(at):** decaying if a < 0 (energy signal, E = 1/(2\vert a \vert) for unit amplitude), growing if a > 0 (neither), used in RC discharge, vibration decay, battery models.
- **Sinusoid A cos(ω₀t + φ):** periodic with period T₀ = 2π/ω₀; average power A²/2; expressible as (A/2)[e^{j(ω₀t+φ)} + e^{−j(ω₀t+φ)}] via Euler's formula; eigenfunction of LTI systems.
- **Complex exponential e^(jω₀t):** unit magnitude; *the* eigenfunction of LTI systems; basis for Fourier/Laplace transforms; rotates in the complex plane at angular speed ω₀.
- **Signum sgn(t):** 2u(t) − 1; represents direction; used in thresholding, bang-bang controllers.
- **Rectangular rect(t/T):** 1 on \vert t \vert < T/2, 0 elsewhere; energy = T; constructed as u(t + T/2) − u(t − T/2).
- **Triangular tri(t/T):** 1 − \vert t \vert/T on \vert t \vert ≤ T; energy = 2T/3; used for trapezoidal velocity profiles in motion control.
