**Step 4: Final answer table for $y[n]$.**

| $n$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ | $3$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $y[n]$ | $4$ | $3$ | $2$ | $1$ |

**Step 5: Interpretation.** The original sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$) becomes $y[n] = \{4, 3, 2, 1\}$ — the sequence is **time-reversed** within its support window. The operation $y[n] = x[3 - n]$ is equivalent to first folding $x[n]$ to get $x[-n]$ (defined for $n \in \{-3, -2, -1, 0\}$) and then shifting right by $3$ to bring it back to the range $n \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$.

**Step 6: Stem plot sketch description.** Plot stems at $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$ with decreasing heights $4, 3, 2, 1$ — a descending staircase from left to right.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1: Apply the energy formula for a finite-length sequence.**

$$E_x = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2$$

**Step 2: Substitute the non-zero values.**

$$E_x = \sum_{n=0}^{3} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \vert 1 \vert^2 + \vert 2 \vert^2 + \vert 3 \vert^2 + \vert 4 \vert^2$$

**Step 3: Compute each term.**

$$E_x = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16$$

**Step 4: Final energy.**

$$E_x = 30 \text{ units (energy)}$$

**Step 5: Compute the average power $P_x$.**

For a finite-duration sequence, the average power over a large window $N$ approaches zero as $N \to \infty$ because the sequence has zero amplitude outside $[0, 3]$:

$$P_x = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{30}{2N+1} = 0$$

**Step 6: Conclusion.** Since $0 < E_x = 30 < \infty$ and $P_x = 0$, the sequence $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ is classified as a **finite-energy signal** (also called an **energy signal**). It is not a power signal because its average power is zero.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Index Direction Errors:** The most common mistake is confusing the direction of the time shift. Always remember: $x[n - k]$ with $k > 0$ shifts **right** (delays the signal). A wrong sign can cost you up to 4 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Domain-Range Mismatches:** When adding or multiplying two sequences, students often forget to extend both sequences to the union of their supports. The result must be tabulated over the combined range, not just the overlap.
> 3. **Even-Odd Verification:** The examiner may deduct $1$ mark if you do not explicitly verify that $x_e[-n] = x_e[n]$ and $x_o[-n] = -x_o[n]$ in your final answer.
> 4. **Stem Plot Convention:** Always use **filled circles (●)** for the actual sample values and **hollow circles (○)** for zero values. Drawing a continuous line instead of stems costs at least $1$ mark.
> 5. **Energy vs. Power Confusion:** A common trap is to label every finite-length sequence as an "energy signal" without checking that the energy is finite (which it always is for finite sequences) AND that the power is zero. State both conditions explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **discrete-time sequence** $x[n]$ is defined only for integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, in contrast to continuous-time signals $x(t)$ defined for all real $t$.
- **Amplitude operations** (addition, multiplication, scalar scaling) are pointwise; they preserve the index structure.
- **Index operations** (shifting, folding, scaling) modify the relationship between index and amplitude.
- **Time shifting** $x[n - k]$: positive $k$ = right shift (delay), negative $k$ = left shift (advance).
- **Time reversal** $x[-n]$: reflects the sequence about the vertical axis at $n = 0$.
- **Combined operations** $x[-n + k]$ or $x[an + b]$: always identify the order of operations (fold vs. shift vs. scale).
- **Even-Odd decomposition** is unique for real sequences: $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] + x[-n]\}$ and $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] - x[-n]\}$.
- **Energy signal:** $0 < E_x < \infty$ and $P_x = 0$.
- **Power signal:** $0 < P_x < \infty$ and $E_x = \infty$ (or undefined).
- **Stem plot convention:** filled circles for actual samples, hollow circles for zeros; stems are vertical lines, never curves.
- **Support of a sequence** is the range of $n$ for which $x[n] \neq 0$. When combining sequences, the new support is the union of the individual supports.
- **For the KTU exam**, always: (i) tabulate the input sequence, (ii) apply the operation symbolically, (iii) tabulate the output, (iv) sketch the stem plot, and (v) verify your answer with a sample substitution.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Sampling Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Sampling Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Sampling** is the process of converting a continuous-time (analog) signal $x(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ by capturing the amplitude of the signal at uniformly spaced time intervals called the **sampling period** $T_s$. The reciprocal of this period is the **sampling frequency** $f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$ (in Hz).

In the context of **Signal Processing for Robotics**, the sampling process is the foundational gateway through which real-world analog phenomena (motor currents, LiDAR reflections, force-torque sensor outputs, joint encoder voltages, and camera pixel intensities) enter the digital domain for algorithmic processing, control law computation, and machine learning inference.

Mathematically, the ideal continuous-to-discrete conversion can be expressed as:

$$
x[n] = x(t)\Big\vert_{t = nT_s} = x(nT_s), \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}
$$

where $n$ is the integer sample index, $T_s$ is the sampling period in **seconds**, and $f_s$ is expressed in **Hertz (Hz)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy — The Stroboscopic Camera:** Imagine filming a spinning fan blade with a video camera. If the camera takes **many frames per second** (high $f_s$), you can clearly see the blade rotating in one direction. If the camera takes frames **too slowly** (low $f_s$), the blade appears to rotate *backward* or stand still — this is **aliasing**. The sampling process is exactly this stroboscopic capture, but for **electrical signals** rather than light. The faster you sample, the more faithfully you can reconstruct the original motion (signal).

In a robotic arm, if you sample the encoder position at $f_s = 10$ Hz but the motor oscillates at $50$ Hz, the controller will "see" a phantom slow drift instead of the true high-frequency vibration — a classic aliasing failure that can destabilize a closed-loop system.

### 1.3 Physical and Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2 f_m$, where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component in the signal.
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N/2 = f_m$ (the maximum representable frequency).
- **Standard Audio Sampling:** **44.1 kHz** (CD), **48 kHz** (professional audio), used because human hearing extends to ~**20 kHz**.
- **Typical Robotics Sampling Rates:** IMU at **1 kHz–8 kHz**, motor torque loops at **10 kHz–20 kHz**, LiDAR at **10 Hz–20 Hz**, cameras at **30 fps–120 fps**.

> [!TIP]  
> Always remember: **Sampling is information preservation, not data preservation.** The art lies in choosing $f_s$ such that the original signal's essential information survives the discrete conversion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ideal Sampling Mechanism — Mathematical Idealization

The ideal sampler is modeled by multiplying the continuous signal $x(t)$ with an **impulse train** (Dirac comb) $p(t)$:

$$
p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

The sampled (impulse-modulated) signal is:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)
$$

**Why multiply by impulses?** Because the Dirac delta $\delta(t-nT_s)$ acts as a mathematical "sampling probe" — it siphons the value $x(nT_s)$ out of the continuous waveform at exactly $t = nT_s$ and represents it as an impulse whose area equals that amplitude.

### 2.2 The Fourier Transform of the Sampled Signal

The impulse train $p(t)$ has a Fourier Series representation:

$$
p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}
$$

Using the **multiplication property** of the Fourier Transform (convolution in frequency):

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

This is the **spectral replica theorem**: the spectrum of the sampled signal consists of infinitely many scaled, shifted copies of the original spectrum $X(f)$, centered at integer multiples of $f_s$.

### 2.3 The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Theorem (Nyquist-Shannon, 1949):** A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be **uniquely and perfectly reconstructed** from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies:  
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$

The minimum acceptable rate, $f_s = 2f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**. The boundary $f_s/2$ is the **folding frequency**.

### 2.4 Aliasing — The Failure Mode

When $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas **overlap**, causing high-frequency components to masquerade as lower frequencies. A sinusoid at frequency $f_a$ appears to have frequency:

$$
f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_a - k f_s \vert, \quad \text{for the integer } k \text{ that minimizes the result within } [0, f_s/2]
$$

**Engineering utility:** Anti-aliasing **low-pass filters** (AAF) with cutoff $f_c \leq f_s/2$ are placed *before* the sampler in every robotic data-acquisition chain (DSP boards, microcontrollers, FPGAs).

### 2.5 Reconstruction via the Low-Pass Filter

If the Nyquist condition is met, the original spectrum $X(f)$ can be extracted by passing $X_s(f)$ through an **ideal low-pass filter** of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_s/2$. The time-domain interpolation is the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula**:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)
$$

where $\mathrm{sinc}(u) = \frac{\sin(\pi u)}{\pi u}$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Sample index conversion | $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ | Dimensionless index $n$ |
| Sampling frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| Sampling period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | **Seconds (s)** |
| Nyquist rate | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | Hz, lower bound on $f_s$ |
| Folding frequency | $f_s / 2$ | Hz, mirror axis |
| Ideal sampled signal | $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\delta(t-nT_s)$ | Impulse train modulation |
| Sampled spectrum | $X_s(f) = \tfrac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(f-kf_s)$ | Periodic replication |
| Reconstruction (sinc interp.) | $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}[(t-nT_s)/T_s]$ | Requires $f_s \geq 2f_m$ |
| Alias frequency | $f_a = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | Smallest non-negative value |
| ZOH transfer function | $H_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = \tfrac{1 - e^{-sT_s}}{s}$ | Practical D/A model |
| Quantization SNR | $\text{SNR}_q \approx 6.02B + 1.76$ dB | $B$ = bit resolution |

> [!TIP]  
> The relationship $\text{SNR}_q \approx 6.02B + 1.76$ dB is the classic rule for an ideal uniform quantizer with $B$-bit resolution. For a 16-bit ADC (common in robotic audio/sensor front-ends), $\text{SNR} \approx 98$ dB.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Codecs (MP3, AAC):** Use $f_s = 44.1$ kHz because the human auditory range is bounded near $f_m = 20$ kHz.
- **Robotic Control Loops:** Sample at least 10× the closed-loop bandwidth for stable discrete-time control; $T_s$ enters directly into the discrete PID gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$.
- **Telecommunications:** Nyquist criterion defines the maximum symbol rate per Hertz of bandwidth.
- **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT):** Sampling in $k$-space and image-domain must both satisfy Nyquist to avoid image-domain aliasing artifacts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Transform of the Impulse-Train Sampler

**Step 1 — Start with the impulse train model.**

We model ideal sampling as multiplication of $x(t)$ by a periodic train of Dirac impulses spaced $T_s$ apart:

$$
p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)
$$

**Step 2 — Express the impulse train in its complex exponential Fourier series.**

Because $p(t)$ is periodic with period $T_s$, its fundamental frequency is $f_0 = 1/T_s$. The Fourier series coefficient is:

$$
C_k = \frac{1}{T_s}\int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} \delta(t)\,e^{-j 2\pi k t / T_s}\,dt
$$

Since the delta at $t=0$ lies inside the integration window, $e^{-j 2\pi k \cdot 0 / T_s} = 1$, and:

$$
C_k = \frac{1}{T_s}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the Fourier series.**

$$
p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} C_k\, e^{j 2\pi k f_s t} = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the sampled signal.**

$$
x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\,x(t)\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the modulation theorem (multiplication in time $\Leftrightarrow$ convolution in frequency, but here we use the shift property of the Fourier transform).**

Recall the shift property: if $\mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = X(f)$, then:

$$
\mathcal{F}\{x(t)\,e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}\} = X(f - k f_s)
$$

Applying this term-by-term to the sum:

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**

The spectrum of the sampled signal is a periodic, scaled replication of the original spectrum at intervals of $f_s$. Each replica is scaled by $1/T_s$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Sampling Rate from the No-Overlap Condition

**Step 1 — Bandlimiting assumption.**

Assume $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$ (i.e., the signal is strictly bandlimited to $[-f_m, f_m]$).

**Step 2 — Locate the replicas.**

The replicas $X(f - k f_s)$ are centered at $f = k f_s$. The right edge of the $k=0$ replica is at $+f_m$. The left edge of the $k=1$ replica is at $f_s - f_m$.

**Step 3 — Impose the no-overlap condition.**

For no spectral overlap (so the original can be recovered with an LPF), we require:

$$
f_s - f_m \;\geq\; f_m
$$

**Step 4 — Solve.**

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_m
$$

This is the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling criterion**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Aliasing Computation

**Problem:** A signal contains a frequency component at $f_a = 370$ Hz. It is sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz. Determine the apparent (aliased) frequency.

**Step 1 — Compute the difference $f_a - f_s$.**

$$
f_a - f_s = 370 - 200 = 170 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Check whether the result lies within the baseband $[0, f_s/2] = [0, 100]$ Hz.**

Since $170 > 100$, we subtract $f_s$ again:

$$
170 - 200 = -30 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the absolute value and reduce to the principal Nyquist interval.**

$$
f_{\text{alias}} = \vert -30 \vert = 30 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

A genuine 370 Hz oscillation, sampled at 200 Hz, appears to the system as a **30 Hz** signal. This is a real-world example of how high-frequency vibration in a robot joint (e.g., bearing defect) can be misread as a slow oscillation by an undersampled controller. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) Transfer Function

The ZOH is the most common practical reconstruction filter (used in every DAC, PWM motor driver, and digital-to-analog output stage).

**Step 1 — Impulse response of the ZOH.**

A ZOH holds each sample value constant for one period $T_s$. Its impulse response is a rectangular pulse of width $T_s$ and height 1:

$$
h_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T_s)
$$

**Step 2 — Laplace transform of the rectangular pulse.**

$$
H_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = \int_0^{T_s} e^{-st}\,dt = \frac{1 - e^{-sT_s}}{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency response.**

Substitute $s = j 2\pi f$:

$$
H_{\text{ZOH}}(j 2\pi f) = T_s \cdot e^{-j\pi f T_s} \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(f T_s)
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude (sinc envelope).**

$$
\vert H_{\text{ZOH}}(f) \vert = T_s \left\vert \mathrm{sinc}(f T_s) \right\vert = T_s \left\vert \frac{\sin(\pi f T_s)}{\pi f T_s} \right\vert
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering insight.**

The ZOH has a **sinc-shaped droop** — at $f = f_s/2$, $\vert H_{\text{ZOH}} \vert \approx 0.637\,T_s$ (i.e., a 4 dB loss). To compensate, robotic controllers often pre-emphasize high frequencies or insert a **post-filter** to flatten the response. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Sampling Rate vs Application

| Application | Typical $f_m$ | Recommended $f_s$ | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human speech (telephony) | 4 kHz | 8 kHz | Nyquist minimum for intelligibility |
| CD-quality audio | 20 kHz | 44.1 kHz | Cover full human auditory range |
| Robotic force/torque loop | 50–500 Hz | 1 kHz–5 kHz | 10× control bandwidth rule |
| MEMS gyroscope | 100–500 Hz | 1 kHz–8 kHz | Anti-aliasing before AHRS filter |
| Industrial motor drive | 1 kHz | 10 kHz–20 kHz | PWM and current-loop stability |
| Vision / LiDAR | Spatial frequency bound | 30 Hz–120 fps frame | Per-pixel Nyquist in image plane |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Sampling Process

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete signal flow from the physical analog world to the digital processing core, and back to the analog actuator — the canonical pipeline in any robotic system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Analog World Signal xt] --> aafB[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    aafB --> sampC[Ideal Sampler Impulse Train Modulation]
    sampC --> adcD[ADC Quantization B bits]
    adcD --> dspE[Digital Signal Processor or MCU]
    dspE --> dacF[DAC with Zero Order Hold]
    dacF --> recG[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter]
    recG --> outH[Reconstructed Analog Signal y t]
    clkI[Clock f s] -.-> sampC
    clkI -.-> adcD
    clkI -.-> dacF
    aafB -.->|Cutoff f s over 2| aafB
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. **Analog World Signal** — Physical sensor output (joint angle, force, current).
2. **Anti-Aliasing LPF** — Hard-limiting band to $[0, f_s/2]$.
3. **Ideal Sampler** — Multiplication by impulse train at $f_s$.
4. **ADC / Quantizer** — Maps continuous amplitude to $B$-bit digital code.
5. **DSP / MCU** — Runs the robotic algorithm (PID, EKF, CNN).
6. **DAC + ZOH** — Converts digital codes back to staircase analog.
7. **Reconstruction LPF** — Smooths staircase to a continuous waveform.
8. **Clock $f_s$** — Synchronizes the sampler, ADC, and DAC.

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain Visualization (Conceptual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spA["Original Spectrum X f\nbandlimited to plus or minus f m"] --> spB["Multiply by Impulse Train"]
    spB --> spC["Spectrum Xs f\nInfinite Replicas at k f s"]
    spC --> spD["Low Pass Filter Cutoff f s over 2"]
    spD --> spE["Recovered X f\nScaled by T s"]
    note1["If f s greater than 2 f m\nReplicas do NOT overlap\nRECOVERY POSSIBLE"] -.-> spC
    note2["If f s less than 2 f m\nReplicas OVERLAP\nALIASING - LOSS OF INFO"] -.-> spC
```

### 4.3 Reconstruction via Sinc Interpolation (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    in1["Discrete Samples x 0 x 1 x 2 x 3"] --> pr1["Shifted Sinc Kernel 1"]
    in1 --> pr2["Shifted Sinc Kernel 2"]
    in1 --> pr3["Shifted Sinc Kernel 3"]
    in1 --> prN["Shifted Sinc Kernel N"]
    pr1 --> sum["Weighted Summation"]
    pr2 --> sum
    pr3 --> sum
    prN --> sum
    sum --> outR["Reconstructed Continuous Signal x t"]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> The above is the visual analog of the Whittaker-Shannon formula: each sample $x[n]$ is convolved with a shifted sinc function, and the result is summed. In practice, this is implemented as a **FIR low-pass filter** with $T_s$ gain and a rectangular window on the impulse response.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1, 3 marks]**

**Q1.** Define the **Nyquist Rate** and **Nyquist Frequency**. How are they related to the sampling frequency $f_s$ of a bandlimited signal?

**Model Answer:**

> The **Nyquist Rate** is the minimum sampling frequency required to avoid aliasing for a signal with maximum frequency $f_m$. It is given by $f_N = 2 f_m$. The **Nyquist Frequency** (also called the folding frequency) is the highest frequency that can be unambiguously represented and equals $f_N/2 = f_s/2$. The relationship is $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ for lossless reconstruction.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2, 3 marks]**

**Q2.** What is **aliasing**? State one practical method to prevent it in a robotic data acquisition system.

**Model Answer:**

> Aliasing is the phenomenon in which two or more distinct frequency components become indistinguishable (i.e., they map to the same value) when a signal is undersampled. It occurs when $f_s < 2 f_m$.  
> **Prevention:** Insert a **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** (AAF) with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s/2$ immediately before the ADC. This is standard practice in IMU, encoder, and LiDAR front-ends.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, 14 marks total]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **sampled spectrum** $X_s(f)$ in terms of the original spectrum $X(f)$ when a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ is sampled by an impulse train of period $T_s$. Clearly state the conditions under which the original signal can be recovered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the impulse train sampler.**

$$
p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s), \quad x_s(t) = x(t)\,p(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Write the Fourier Series of $p(t)$.**

$$
p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}, \quad f_s = 1/T_s
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $x_s(t)$.**

$$
x_s(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the frequency-shift property of the Fourier Transform.**

$$
X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)
$$

**Step 5 — Recovery condition.**

For bandlimited $X(f)$ with cutoff $f_m$, the replicas do not overlap iff $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. Under this condition, applying an ideal LPF of gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_s/2$ recovers $X(f)$ exactly.

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \cdot 600\,t) + 3\sin(2\pi \cdot 1500\,t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz.  
**(i)** Check whether the Nyquist criterion is satisfied. **(2 marks)**  
**(ii)** Determine the apparent frequencies if the criterion is violated. **(3 marks)**  
**(iii)** Recommend a minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i)** Highest frequency: $f_m = 1500$ Hz. Nyquist criterion requires $f_s \geq 2(1500) = 3000$ Hz. Since $f_s = 1000$ Hz $\ll 3000$ Hz, the criterion is **NOT satisfied**.

**(ii) Aliasing computation:**

- For the 600 Hz component: $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert 600 - 1000 \vert = 400$ Hz (and $400 \leq 500$, so it stays).
- For the 1500 Hz component: $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert 1500 - 1000 \vert = 500$ Hz.

So the sampled signal will appear to contain **400 Hz** and **500 Hz** components — not the original 600 Hz and 1500 Hz.

**(iii)** Minimum rate: $f_s \geq 2 \times 1500 = 3000$ Hz. A safe engineering choice is $f_s = 4000$ Hz (or 4 kHz) to allow for practical filter roll-off.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat **block diagram**, explain the complete process of **sampling, quantization, and reconstruction** of a continuous-time signal. Mention the role of the anti-aliasing filter and the zero-order hold. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (textual):**

$$
x(t) \;\to\; [\text{Anti-Alias LPF}] \;\to\; [\text{Sampler}] \;\to\; [\text{Quantizer (ADC)}] \;\to\; [\text{Digital Processor}] \;\to\; [\text{ZOH (DAC)}] \;\to\; [\text{Reconstruction LPF}] \;\to\; y(t)
$$

**Explanation:**

1. **Anti-Aliasing LPF:** Limits the input bandwidth to $f_s/2$ to prevent spectral overlap before sampling.
2. **Sampler:** Captures instantaneous amplitudes at $t = nT_s$ using an impulse train modulator.
3. **Quantizer (ADC):** Maps each continuous amplitude to one of $2^B$ discrete levels; introduces quantization noise.
4. **Digital Processor:** Executes DSP algorithms (filtering, control, AI).
5. **ZOH (DAC):** Holds each digital value for one $T_s$, producing a staircase waveform with transfer function $H_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT_s})/s$.
6. **Reconstruction LPF:** Smooths the staircase to a continuous signal, approximating the original $x(t)$.

**(b)** Explain the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula** for reconstructing a bandlimited signal from its samples. Why is the sinc function central to this formula? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The Reconstruction Formula:**

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Intuition.**

Each sample $x(nT_s)$ is convolved with a shifted, scaled sinc function centered at $t = nT_s$. The sum of these shifted sincs reconstructs the continuous signal exactly (provided $f_s \geq 2 f_m$).

**Step 3 — Why sinc?**

The sinc function is the **impulse response of the ideal low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_s/2$:

$$
h(t) = T_s \cdot \mathrm{sinc}(t/T_s)
$$

Because multiplying a signal in time by an impulse train corresponds to convolving its spectrum with a frequency-domain impulse train, the inverse operation — reconstruction — is equivalent to **low-pass filtering**, whose ideal time-domain kernel is the sinc.

**Step 4 — Properties of sinc used here:**

- $\mathrm{sinc}(0) = 1$ and $\mathrm{sinc}(k) = 0$ for $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ (zero crossings at non-zero integers).
- These zeros ensure that at $t = nT_s$, only the $n$-th term contributes, while all others contribute zero — guaranteeing **interpolation accuracy at sample points**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

The sinc basis is the unique function that is both bandlimited to $f_s/2$ and orthogonal at the sample instants, making it the natural interpolant for Nyquist-rate sampled signals.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency.** $f_N = 2 f_m$ is the *rate*; $f_N/2$ is the *frequency* (cutoff). Mixing these up costs easy marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ scaling** in the spectrum $X_s(f)$. Examiners expect the exact expression with the $1/T_s$ factor.
> 3. **Not drawing the anti-aliasing filter** explicitly in the block diagram. Its absence is treated as a missing conceptual element.
> 4. **Writing aliasing calculation without the absolute value** — $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ is mandatory.
> 5. **Skipping the reconstruction LPF** in the block diagram. Reconstruction requires *both* the ZOH (DAC) and the smoothing LPF.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The Sampling Process"**

- **Sampling** = continuous-to-discrete time conversion at uniform intervals $T_s$.
- **Sampling frequency:** $f_s = 1/T_s$ in **Hz**; sampling period in **seconds**.
- **Nyquist-Shannon Theorem:** A bandlimited signal ($f_m$ max) can be **perfectly reconstructed** iff $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
- **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2 f_m$ (minimum $f_s$).
- **Nyquist Frequency (Folding Frequency):** $f_s / 2$ (mirror axis).
- **Sampled Spectrum:** $X_s(f) = (1/T_s) \sum_k X(f - k f_s)$ — periodic spectral replicas.
- **Aliasing:** occurs when replicas overlap ($f_s < 2 f_m$). The aliased frequency is $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** analog LPF before ADC, cutoff $= f_s / 2$.
- **Reconstruction:** ideal LPF of gain $T_s$, cutoff $f_s/2$; equivalent to sinc interpolation.
- **Sinc Interpolation:** $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\mathrm{sinc}[(t - nT_s)/T_s]$.
- **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):** $H_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT_s})/s$; produces staircase output, has sinc-shaped droop in frequency.
- **Quantization SNR:** $\approx 6.02B + 1.76$ dB for $B$-bit uniform quantizer.
- **Robotics relevance:** Sampling underpins **every** digital control loop, sensor acquisition, and perception algorithm.
- **Practical rule:** Sample at **10× the closed-loop bandwidth** for stable digital control; place the AAF *before* the sampler, *never* after.
- **Key insight:** Sampling preserves **information**, not the **analog waveform itself** — what is kept is the discrete sequence of impulses weighted by sample amplitudes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quantization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantization** is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude (analog) signal into a discrete-amplitude (digital) signal by representing each sample value with the nearest level from a finite set of $L = 2^{B}$ reconstruction levels, where $B$ is the number of bits (resolution) of the quantizer.

In the broader **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)** pipeline, quantization is the second fundamental operation that follows **sampling** (which discretizes the time axis) and precedes **coding** (which assigns binary codewords to the quantized levels).

$$x[n] \xrightarrow{\text{Sampling}} x(nT_s) \xrightarrow{\text{Quantization}} x_q[n] \xrightarrow{\text{Coding}} b_1 b_2 \dots b_B$$

Where:
- $x(nT_s)$ — discrete-time, continuous-amplitude sample
- $x_q[n]$ — discrete-time, discrete-amplitude quantized sample
- $B$ — word-length (number of bits) of the ADC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantization is an irreversible (many-to-one) operation.** Two infinitely close analog values can map to the *same* digital codeword, meaning **information is permanently lost** during quantization. This loss is the fundamental trade-off for entering the digital domain.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Digital Ruler"

Imagine a carpenter's ruler that can only mark whole centimeters. If a board measures $14.3$ cm, the carpenter must *round* it to $14$ cm. If another board measures $14.7$ cm, it rounds to $15$ cm. Both boards — though physically different — get assigned the *same* "digital" length: 14 cm.

**Quantization works identically:**

- The **continuous wood length** = the analog signal $x(t)$.
- The **ruler's smallest division** ($1$ cm) = the **quantization step size** $\Delta$.
- The **whole-centimeter value assigned** = the **quantized sample** $x_q[n]$.

In robotics, every digital sensor (LIDAR, IMU, depth camera, encoder) is a "digital ruler" measuring the physical world. The **coarseness of the ruler** (number of bits) decides how precisely the robot perceives its environment.

---

## 1.3 Why Quantization Matters in Robotics

A modern autonomous robot operates on **quantized sensor data**:

| Robotics Subsystem | Quantized Quantity | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| LIDAR Range Finder | Distance (m) | $12$–$16$ bits |
| IMU (Accelerometer/Gyro) | Angular velocity / Acceleration | $14$–$24$ bits |
| Vision (Camera) | Pixel intensity | $8$–$12$ bits |
| Wheel Encoders | Angular displacement | $12$–$20$ bits |
| Force/Torque Sensor | Force in N | $16$–$24$ bits |

> [!TIP]
> **Higher bit-depth $\Rightarrow$ finer resolution $\Rightarrow$ lower quantization noise $\Rightarrow$ better robot perception.** However, this increases data bandwidth, memory, and computational load — a critical trade-off in embedded robotic systems (e.g., TurtleBot, Crazyflie drones).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quantization Process — Structured Logic

The quantizer is a memoryless, non-linear device characterized by its **transfer characteristic** $Q(x)$. The fundamental operational steps are:

1. **Input Range Definition:** The analog input $x$ is assumed to lie within a Full-Scale Range (FSR), typically $[-V_{max}, +V_{max}]$.
2. **Partitioning the Range:** The input range is divided into $L = 2^{B}$ equal (uniform quantizer) or unequal (non-uniform quantizer) sub-intervals called **quantization cells** or **decision intervals**.
3. **Decision Thresholds:** Boundaries $x_k$ (where $k = 0, 1, \dots, L$) separate consecutive cells. The cell $C_k = [x_k, x_{k+1})$ contains all input values that map to a single output level.
4. **Output Level Assignment:** Each cell $C_k$ is assigned a single **reconstruction level** $y_k$ (also called *quantization level* or *codeword*).
5. **Quantization Rule:** For any input $x \in C_k$, the output is $x_q = y_k$.
6. **Quantization Error Generation:** The difference $e_q = x_q - x$ is the **quantization error** (also called *quantization noise*).

---

## 2.2 Types of Uniform Quantizers

There are two canonical architectures for uniform quantizers (step size $\Delta$ constant):

### (a) Mid-Rise Quantizer
- Has $L$ reconstruction levels (always even count).
- The first level is $+\Delta/2$ and the last is $-\Delta/2 + V_{max}$ (i.e., the transfer characteristic is *shifted up* by $\Delta/2$).
- Has $L+1$ decision thresholds.

$$x_q = \Delta \cdot \left(\left\lfloor \frac{x}{\Delta} \right\rfloor + \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

### (b) Mid-Tread Quantizer
- Has $L$ reconstruction levels (always odd count when symmetric around zero).
- One level is centered at $0$ (the "tread" of the staircase crosses zero).
- Has $L+1$ decision thresholds.

$$x_q = \Delta \cdot \left(\left\lfloor \frac{x}{\Delta} + \frac{1}{2} \right\rfloor\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mid-tread quantizers are preferred in robotics** because they have a dedicated level for zero (no DC offset), which is critical for accelerometer and gyroscope data when the robot is stationary.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, definition, and boundary condition required to solve KTU 2024 Scheme examination problems on **Quantization**.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of Quantization Levels | $L = 2^{B}$ | $B$ = number of bits |
| 2 | Quantization Step Size (Resolution) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L} = \dfrac{\text{FSR}}{2^{B}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Full-Scale Range | $\text{FSR} = 2 V_{max}$ (for symmetric range $\pm V_{max}$) | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Quantization Error (Mid-Rise) | $-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Quantization Error (Mid-Tread) | $-\dfrac{\Delta}{2} < e_q \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| 6 | Mean of Quantization Error | $\mu_{e} = E\{e_q\} = 0$ (assumed) | Volts (V) |
| 7 | Mean Square Quantization Error | $\sigma_{e}^{2} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$ | V² |
| 8 | Quantization Noise Power | $P_{e} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$ | V² (Watts on 1 Ω) |
| 9 | Signal Power (Full-Scale Sinusoid) | $P_{s} = \dfrac{A^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta^{2} \cdot 2^{2B}}{8}$ | V² |
| 10 | **SQNR (linear)** | $\text{SQNR} = \dfrac{P_{s}}{P_{e}} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2B} = 1.5 \cdot 4^{B}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 11 | **SQNR (in dB)** | $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,B + 1.76$ | **decibels (dB)** |
| 12 | Bits Required for Target SQNR | $B = \dfrac{\text{SQNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits |
| 13 | Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) | $B_{eff} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{measured,dB} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits |
| 14 | μ-Law Compression (USA/Japan) | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$ | $\mu = 255$ standard |
| 15 | A-Law Compression (Europe/India) | $F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}, \ \tfrac{1}{A} \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1$ | $A = 87.6$ standard |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Quantization Analysis |
|---|---|
| **ADC Selection in Robotics** | Determines LIDAR/IMU precision vs. data-rate trade-off. |
| **Audio Engineering** | CD-quality audio uses $B = 16$ bits $\Rightarrow$ SQNR $\approx 98$ dB. |
| **Telecommunications (PCM)** | Companding (A-law/μ-law) uses non-uniform quantization for voice. |
| **Image Processing** | $B = 8$ bits/pixel gives $256$ gray levels (medical imaging, satellites need $B \geq 12$). |
| **Embedded ML (TinyML)** | Model quantization to $8$-bit or $4$-bit integers for on-device inference. |

> [!TIP]
> **The "6 dB per bit" rule is one of the most tested facts in KTU DSP papers.** Memorize: *every additional bit of ADC resolution increases SQNR by approximately **6.02 dB**.*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Square Quantization Error $\sigma_{e}^{2} = \Delta^{2}/12$

### Assumptions (Statistical Model of Quantization Error)

For a **uniform quantizer** operating on a signal that spans many quantization levels and varies rapidly relative to the sampling rate, the quantization error $e_q = x_q - x$ can be modeled as:

1. **Stationary random process** — error statistics do not change with time.
2. **Uniformly distributed** — $e_q$ is equally likely to take any value in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$.
3. **Zero mean** — $\mu_{e} = 0$.
4. **Uncorrelated with the input signal** — $E\{x \cdot e_q\} = 0$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The probability density function (PDF) of the uniformly distributed error is:

$$p(e_q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\Delta}, & -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq \dfrac{\Delta}{2} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2:** The mean of the quantization error is computed as:

$$\mu_{e} = E\{e_q\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral — the integrand $e_q$ is **odd** and the limits are **symmetric**, so the integral vanishes:

$$\mu_{e} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[ \frac{e_q^{2}}{2} \right]_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta^{2}}{8} - \frac{\Delta^{2}}{8} \right) = 0$$

**Step 4:** The mean square value (variance, since mean is zero) is:

$$\sigma_{e}^{2} = E\{e_q^{2}\} = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^{2} \cdot p(e_q)\, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^{2}\, de_q$$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integral of $e_q^{2}$ (an even function):

$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^{2}\, de_q = 2 \int_{0}^{\Delta/2} e_q^{2}\, de_q = 2 \left[ \frac{e_q^{3}}{3} \right]_{0}^{\Delta/2} = 2 \cdot \frac{(\Delta/2)^{3}}{3} = \frac{\Delta^{3}}{12}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the mean square expression:

$$\sigma_{e}^{2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^{3}}{12} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$

### Final Result (Boxed)

$$\boxed{\sigma_{e}^{2} = P_{e} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR)

For a **full-scale sinusoidal input** $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)$ where $A$ is the peak amplitude. The ADC spans $\pm V_{max}$, so the maximum representable peak is $A_{max} = V_{max} = \Delta \cdot 2^{B-1}$ (using the symmetric mid-rise quantizer where the maximum positive level is just below $V_{max}$).

**Step 1:** Signal power of the sinusoid:

$$P_{s} = \frac{A^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2:** For a full-scale sinusoid (peak = $V_{max}$), substitute $A = \Delta \cdot 2^{B-1}$:

$$P_{s} = \frac{(\Delta \cdot 2^{B-1})^{2}}{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2} \cdot 2^{2B-2}}{2} = \Delta^{2} \cdot 2^{2B-3}$$

**Step 3:** Quantization noise power (from Derivation 1):

$$P_{e} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the linear SQNR ratio:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{s}}{P_{e}} = \frac{\Delta^{2} \cdot 2^{2B-3}}{\Delta^{2}/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2B-3} = \frac{12}{8} \cdot 2^{2B} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^{B}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to decibels:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2} \cdot 4^{B}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + 10 \log_{10}(4^{B})$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the logarithmic constants:

$$10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 10 \times 0.1761 = 1.761 \approx 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

$$10 \log_{10}(4^{B}) = 10B \log_{10}(4) = 10B \times 0.6021 \approx 6.02\,B \text{ dB}$$

### Final Result (Boxed)

$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,B + 1.76 \text{ dB}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

> **Problem:** A robotic distance sensor uses a $10$-bit ADC with an input range of $\pm 5$ V. Calculate the **(a)** quantization step size, **(b)** maximum quantization error, **(c)** SQNR, and **(d)** RMS noise voltage.

### Given Data
- $B = 10$ bits
- FSR $= \pm 5$ V $\Rightarrow$ total range $= 10$ V

### (a) Quantization Step Size

$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^{B}} = \frac{10}{2^{10}} = \frac{10}{1024} = 9.7656 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 9.77 \text{ mV}$$

### (b) Maximum Quantization Error

$$e_{q,\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{9.7656 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}$$

### (c) SQNR in dB

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

### (d) RMS Noise Voltage

$$e_{q,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{e}^{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{9.7656 \times 10^{-3}}{3.4641} \approx 2.82 \text{ mV}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Reverse Problem (Finding Number of Bits)

> **Problem:** A digital audio system in a robot's voice-command interface requires a minimum SQNR of $80$ dB. Find the **minimum number of bits** required.

### Solution

Using the SQNR formula:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,B + 1.76$$

Solving for $B$:

$$B = \frac{\text{SQNR}_{dB} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{80 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{78.24}{6.02} \approx 12.99$$

Since $B$ must be an integer, we round **up** to ensure the SQNR *meets or exceeds* the requirement:

$$\boxed{B_{min} = 13 \text{ bits}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Always round UP** when finding the minimum bits for a target SQNR. Rounding down (e.g., to $12$ bits) would give SQNR $\approx 74$ dB — *insufficient* for the requirement.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3 — Non-Uniform Quantization (Companding)

> **Problem:** A robot's microphone samples voice commands. Using **μ-law companding** with $\mu = 255$, find the compressed output for an input normalized amplitude $x = 0.5$.

### Solution

The μ-law compression equation is:

$$F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$$

Substitute $x = 0.5$ and $\mu = 255$:

$$F(0.5) = (+1) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + 255 \times 0.5)}{\ln(1 + 255)} = \frac{\ln(1 + 127.5)}{\ln(256)} = \frac{\ln(128.5)}{\ln(256)}$$

$$F(0.5) = \frac{4.856}{5.545} \approx 0.876$$

### Interpretation

A small input ($x = 0.5$) is **expanded** to a larger value ($0.876$) in the compressed domain, giving it *more quantization levels* in the receiver. This is precisely the trick that lets $8$-bit μ-law achieve the voice quality of $13$-bit linear PCM.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — ADC Pipeline Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the complete signal-processing chain from the analog sensor to the digital processor, highlighting where **Quantization** sits in the pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    analog["Analog Sensor Signal x(t)"] --> lpf["Anti-Aliasing LPF H(f)"]
    lpf --> samp["Sampler x(nTs)"]
    samp --> quant["Quantizer Q(x)"]
    quant --> code["Binary Encoder"]
    code --> dsp["Digital Signal Processor"]
    quant -.->."|e_q = x_q - x|" error["Quantization Error"]
    error --> addn["Additive Noise Model"]
    quant --> addn
    addn --> recon["Reconstructed Signal x_hat(t)"]
```

**Reading the diagram (left to right):**
1. **Analog Sensor** — produces a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal (e.g., LIDAR return).
2. **Anti-Aliasing LPF** — removes frequency content above the Nyquist frequency $f_s/2$ (precedes quantization to prevent aliasing).
3. **Sampler** — discretizes the time axis (output is $x(nT_s)$).
4. **Quantizer** — discretizes the amplitude axis (output is $x_q[n]$); the **focus of this module**.
5. **Binary Encoder** — maps each quantization level to a $B$-bit codeword.
6. **DSP** — performs robot perception, control, and decision-making.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Transfer Characteristics of Mid-Rise and Mid-Tread Quantizers

This Mermaid diagram visualizes the **staircase input–output relationships** of the two canonical uniform quantizers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MR["MID-RISE QUANTIZER (L is EVEN)"]
        mr1["Decision Thresholds: x_k = k·Delta, k = 0,1,...,L"]
        mr2["Reconstruction Levels: y_k = k·Delta + Delta/2, k = 0,1,...,L-1"]
        mr3["Levels: ..., -1.5Δ, -0.5Δ, +0.5Δ, +1.5Δ, ..."]
        mr4["Error Range: -Delta/2 <= e_q <= +Delta/2 (inclusive both ends)"]
        mr5["Output is NEVER exactly zero"]
    end
    subgraph MT["MID-TREAD QUANTIZER (L is ODD)"]
        mt1["Decision Thresholds: x_k = k·Delta + Delta/2"]
        mt2["Reconstruction Levels: y_k = k·Delta, k = -L/2,...,+L/2"]
        mt3["Levels: ..., -2Δ, -Δ, 0, +Δ, +2Δ, ..."]
        mt4["Error Range: -Delta/2 < e_q <= +Delta/2"]
        mt5["Output CAN be exactly zero (preferred for sensors at rest)"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Quantization Error Waveform (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Continuous Input x(t)"] -->|"smooth sine wave"| sub["Subtract Operation"]
    out["Quantized Output x_q(t)"] -->|"staircase approximation"| sub
    sub --> err["Quantization Error e_q(t)"]
    err --> bound["Bounded: |e_q| <= Delta/2"]
    err --> rand["Approximately RANDOM for rapidly varying signals"]
    err --> model["Additive White Noise Model for analysis"]
```

**Engineering interpretation:** When the input signal varies rapidly (high frequency content) and the quantizer has many levels, the error $e_q(t)$ appears as a **pseudo-random** sawtooth-like waveform. This is why the **additive white noise model** is justified — and why the formula $\sigma_{e}^{2} = \Delta^{2}/12$ holds in practice.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — SQNR vs. Number of Bits (The Famous 6 dB/Rule)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B8["B = 8 bits"] -->|"+6.02 dB per bit"| B10["B = 10 bits"]
    B10 -->|"+6.02 dB per bit"| B12["B = 12 bits"]
    B12 -->|"+6.02 dB per bit"| B14["B = 14 bits"]
    B14 -->|"+6.02 dB per bit"| B16["B = 16 bits (CD audio)"]
    B8 -.->|"SQNR = 49.9 dB"| note1["Speech quality (telephone)"]
    B12 -.->|"SQNR = 74.0 dB"| note2["High-fidelity audio"]
    B16 -.->|"SQNR = 98.1 dB"| note3["Studio / CD audio"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Robotics connection:** LIDAR units often use $16$–$24$-bit ADCs because range measurements require both high dynamic range and high SQNR. A 24-bit ADC gives SQNR $\approx 146$ dB — sufficient to detect millimeter-scale reflections on a 100 m range.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define quantization. What is the significance of the quantization step size in an Analog-to-Digital Converter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Quantization is the process of converting a discrete-time continuous-amplitude signal $x(nT_s)$ into a discrete-time discrete-amplitude signal $x_q[n]$ by mapping each sample to the nearest of $L = 2^{B}$ predefined amplitude levels, where $B$ is the number of bits of the ADC.

**Significance of step size (1 mark):** The quantization step size $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^{B}$ determines the **resolution** of the ADC. A smaller $\Delta$ (larger $B$) yields finer resolution, lower quantization error, higher SQNR, and more accurate digital representation — at the cost of increased data rate and memory.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Distinguish between mid-rise and mid-tread quantizers. Which one is preferred for digitizing sensor signals in robotics, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Mid-Rise | Mid-Tread |
|---|---|---|
| Number of levels $L$ | Even | Odd |
| Level at zero | No level at $x = 0$ | Level exists at $x = 0$ |
| Step size formula | $\Delta = \text{FSR}/L$ | $\Delta = \text{FSR}/(L-1)$ |

**Preference for robotics (1 mark):** The **mid-tread quantizer is preferred** for robotics sensors (accelerometers, gyroscopes, encoders) because it provides an exact zero codeword. When the robot is stationary, the sensor output is naturally near zero; a mid-tread quantizer gives a clean digital zero with no offset, simplifying calibration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Signal-to-Quantization Noise Ratio (SQNR)** of a uniform mid-rise quantizer for a full-scale sinusoidal input. State clearly all assumptions used. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A robotic temperature monitoring module uses a $12$-bit ADC with an input voltage range of $0$ to $5$ V (unipolar).
    **(i)** Calculate the quantization step size and the maximum quantization error.
    **(ii)** Determine the SQNR in dB.
    **(iii)** If the temperature sensor produces $0$ to $3$ V (not full-scale), recalculate the actual SQNR. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Solution to Question A (a):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
2. The error has zero mean ($\mu_{e} = 0$).
3. The error is uncorrelated with the input signal.
4. The signal is full-scale, i.e., the input sinusoid has peak amplitude $A = V_{max} = \Delta \cdot 2^{B-1}$.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Quantization noise power:

$$P_{e} = \sigma_{e}^{2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^{2}\, de_q = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^{3}}{12} = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$

**Step 2:** Signal power of full-scale sinusoid:

$$P_{s} = \frac{A^{2}}{2} = \frac{(\Delta \cdot 2^{B-1})^{2}}{2} = \frac{\Delta^{2} \cdot 2^{2B-2}}{2} = \Delta^{2} \cdot 2^{2B-3}$$

**Step 3:** SQNR linear ratio:

$$\text{SQNR} = \frac{P_{s}}{P_{e}} = \frac{\Delta^{2} \cdot 2^{2B-3}}{\Delta^{2}/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2B-3} = 1.5 \cdot 4^{B}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to dB:

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 B \log_{10}(4) = 1.76 + 6.02\,B \text{ dB}$$

#### **Solution to Question A (b):**

**Given:** $B = 12$, FSR $= 5$ V (unipolar, $0$ to $5$ V).

**(i) Step size & max error (2 marks):**

$$\Delta = \frac{\text{FSR}}{2^{B}} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} = 1.2207 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx 1.22 \text{ mV}$$

$$e_{q,\max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} \approx 0.61 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Full-scale SQNR (2 marks):**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Actual SQNR at 3 V peak (3 marks):**

The signal-to-FSR ratio is:

$$\frac{V_{signal,max}}{V_{FS,max}} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6$$

The actual signal power scales as $(0.6)^{2}$:

$$P_{s,actual} = (0.6)^{2} \cdot P_{s,full-scale} = 0.36 \cdot P_{s,full-scale}$$

In dB, the SQNR reduces by:

$$20 \log_{10}(0.6) = -4.44 \text{ dB}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{SQNR}_{actual} = 74.0 - 4.44 = 69.56 \text{ dB}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — July 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **statistical model of quantization error** for a uniform quantizer. Derive the expression for the **mean square quantization error** and state the **four key assumptions** that justify the additive white noise model. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** With neat block diagrams, explain the operation of **A-law and μ-law companding systems**. Compare their characteristics and state one application of each in robotic communication systems. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### **Solution to Question B (a):**

**Explanation of the model (3 marks):**
The quantization error $e_q = x_q - x$ for a uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta$ is a deterministic but complex function of the input. However, for analysis, it is treated as a **random variable** with well-defined statistics. The error waveform resembles a sawtooth that is approximately uncorrelated from sample to sample when the input is "busy" (covers many levels and varies rapidly). This justifies modelling it as **additive white noise** added to the signal.

**Four key assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The error $e_q$ is **uniformly distributed** in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
2. The error is **uncorrelated with the input signal** $x$.
3. The error is **stationary** (statistical properties do not change with time).
4. The error samples are **uncorrelated with each other** (white noise spectrum).

**Derivation of mean square error (2 marks):** *(See Derivation 1 in Section 3.1 of these notes.)*

$$\sigma_{e}^{2} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e_q^{2}\, de_q = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}$$

---

#### **Solution to Question B (b):**

**Block diagram description (text-based Mermaid, 3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in["Analog Input x(t)"] --> comp["Compressor F(x)"]
    comp -->|"smaller dynamic range"| uniq["Uniform Quantizer Q"]
    uniq --> chan["Transmission Channel"]
    chan --> exqd["Decoder (Inverse)"]
    exqd --> exp["Expander F-inverse(y)"]
    exp --> out["Recovered x_hat(t)"]
```

**A-law companding (2 marks):**
- Used in **Europe and India** (ITU-T G.711 standard).
- Compression equation:

$$F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \begin{cases} \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq \dfrac{1}{A} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} < \vert x \vert \leq 1 \end{cases}$$

- Standard constant: $A = 87.6$.
- A-law has a **linear segment** near zero (no singularity), making it robust to small DC offsets — useful in robot microphone arrays.

**μ-law companding (2 marks):**
- Used in **USA, Japan, and North America**.
- Compression equation:

$$F(x) = \text{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1 + \mu)}$$

- Standard constant: $\mu = 255$.
- μ-law is a **pure logarithmic** curve, giving slightly better SNR for very small signals.
- **Robotics application:** μ-law is used in NASA's deep-space communication links to compress robot-to-Earth telemetry from Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance).

**Comparison (extra detail):**

| Feature | μ-law | A-law |
|---|---|---|
| Region | USA / Japan | Europe / India |
| Constant | $\mu = 255$ | $A = 87.6$ |
| Curve shape | Pure logarithmic | Linear-then-logarithmic |
| Behavior near zero | $\ln(\mu \vert x \vert) \to -\infty$ | Linear, finite slope |
| Robotics use | Deep-space comms | Terrestrial voice systems |

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks on Quantization questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in FSR** (writing $\Delta = V_{max}/2^{B}$ instead of $\Delta = 2V_{max}/2^{B}$ for a bipolar range $\pm V_{max}$). The FSR is the *total* range, not the *peak*.
> 2. **Using $20 \log$ instead of $10 \log$** for power ratios. SQNR is a *power* ratio, so it is always $10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_e)$. Using $20 \log$ inflates the answer by a factor of $2$.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert amplitude to power** in sinusoidal signal-power calculations. The factor is $1/2$, not $1$ or $\sqrt{2}$.
> 4. **Rounding DOWN the number of bits** when the question asks for the *minimum* $B$ to meet a target SQNR. Always round **UP** (3 marks lost if the SQNR is *just* below the requirement).
> 5. **Confusing mid-rise with mid-tread** in transfer-characteristic sketches — the $y$-intercept of a mid-rise quantizer is $+\Delta/2$ (not zero!).
> 6. **Forgetting to state the four assumptions** in statistical-model derivations. The examiner awards $2$–$3$ marks just for the assumption list.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section. It is the *cheat sheet* you should read 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition:** Quantization maps a continuous-amplitude sample to one of $L = 2^{B}$ discrete levels.
- **Step size:** $\Delta = \text{FSR}/2^{B}$ — *smallest distinguishable voltage change*.
- **Number of levels:** $L = 2^{B}$ where $B$ is the number of bits.
- **Quantization error (mid-rise):** $-\Delta/2 \leq e_q \leq \Delta/2$.
- **Quantization error (mid-tread):** $-\Delta/2 < e_q \leq \Delta/2$.
- **Mean error:** $\mu_{e} = 0$ (under uniform-distribution assumption).
- **Mean square error (noise power):** $P_{e} = \Delta^{2}/12$ — *memorize this formula!*
- **SQNR formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,B + 1.76$ dB — *the most-tested formula!*
- **Bits for target SQNR:** $B = (\text{SQNR}_{dB} - 1.76) / 6.02$ — *always round up*.
- **Mid-rise:** $L$ even, no zero level, error range $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
- **Mid-tread:** $L$ odd, has zero level, preferred for sensors.
- **6 dB rule:** Each extra bit adds $\approx 6$ dB of SQNR.
- **Statistical model assumptions:** Uniform distribution, zero mean, uncorrelated with signal, stationary.
- **Companding:** μ-law ($\mu = 255$, USA/Japan), A-law ($A = 87.6$, Europe/India).
- **Companding benefit:** $8$-bit companded $\approx$ $13$-bit linear PCM.
- **Robotics context:** LIDAR, IMU, encoders, cameras all rely on quantization precision.
- **Trade-off:** More bits $\Rightarrow$ better SNR but higher data rate / memory / power.
- **Add-on rule:** For a non-full-scale signal of amplitude $A$, subtract $20 \log_{10}(A/V_{max})$ from the full-scale SQNR.
- **ENOB formula:** $B_{eff} = (\text{SNR}_{measured,dB} - 1.76)/6.02$ — used to grade real ADCs with non-idealities.

> **End of Module 1 — Quantization Notes. Proceed to Module 2: Discrete-Time Signals & Systems.** ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Discrete-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Discrete-Time System (DTS)** is a mathematical operator or transformation $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ to an output discrete-time sequence $y[n]$, where the independent variable $n$ belongs to the integer set $\mathbb{Z}$.

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A discrete-time system processes a sequence of numbers $x[n]$ defined only at integer instants $n = \ldots, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ and produces an output sequence $y[n]$ that is also defined only at integer instants. The system is completely characterized by its operator $T\{\cdot\}$.

**Notation conventions used in KTU papers:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $x[n]$ | Input discrete-time signal |
| $y[n]$ | Output discrete-time signal |
| $h[n]$ | Impulse response of the system |
| $T\{\cdot\}$ | System transformation operator |
| $\delta[n]$ | Unit impulse / Kronecker delta function |
| $u[n]$ | Unit step sequence |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Robotic Arm Controller

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine a robotic arm in a KTU-made pick-and-place robot. The arm does **not** move continuously; it receives a command **only** every 10 milliseconds (e.g., "rotate 2° clockwise"). The mapping from the list of commands to the final position is a *discrete-time system*.

Think of a discrete-time system as a **vending machine** that:
1. Accepts coins (input) only at specific moments when you press a button.
2. It does not respond in between button presses.
3. Based on the cumulative input history, it dispenses a product (output) at a precise time instant.

The "machine's rule book" is the system operator $T\{\cdot\}$. Two different vending machines with the same input will produce different outputs because their **internal rules** differ.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Rate ($F_s$):** Typically measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. The reciprocal gives the sampling interval $T_s = 1/F_s$.
- **Nyquist Frequency:** $F_N = F_s / 2$ — the highest representable frequency without aliasing.
- **Unit Impulse Magnitude:** Unity, $\delta[0] = 1$, and $\delta[n] = 0$ for $n \neq 0$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Discrete-Time Systems

Discrete-time systems are classified based on their response characteristics. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following six properties.

### 2.1.1 Static vs Dynamic (Memoryless vs With Memory)
- **Static System:** Output at $n$ depends only on the input at the same instant $n$.
  - Example: $y[n] = (x[n])^2$
- **Dynamic System:** Output depends on past or future input samples.
  - Example: $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ (uses one past sample).

### 2.1.2 Linear vs Non-Linear
A system is **Linear** if it satisfies the **Superposition Principle**: Additivity and Homogeneity (Scaling).

$$T\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Superposition Test (KTU Hot Question):** Apply two inputs separately, scale them, sum the outputs — it must equal the response of the combined scaled input.

### 2.1.3 Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant
A system is **Time-Invariant (TI)** if a time shift in the input causes an identical time shift in the output.

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \implies y[n-n_0] = T\{x[n-n_0]\}$$

If a robot controller produces a different response today versus tomorrow for the *same* command given at a *different* time, it is **Time-Variant**.

### 2.1.4 Causal vs Non-Causal
- **Causal System:** Output at $n$ depends only on present and past inputs ($n \le n_0$). Cannot see the future.
  - **Physical realizability requirement for real-time robotics.**
- **Non-Causal System:** Uses future input samples ($n > n_0$). Used only in offline processing.

### 2.1.5 Stable vs Unstable
**Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) Stability** — A system is stable if every bounded input produces a bounded output.

$$\vert x[n] \vert \le M_x < \infty \implies \vert y[n] \vert \le M_y < \infty$$

For **LTI systems**, BIBO stability is equivalent to absolute summability of the impulse response.

### 2.1.6 Invertible vs Non-Invertible
A system is **Invertible** if distinct inputs produce distinct outputs (the mapping is one-to-one). The inverse system $T^{-1}\{\cdot\}$ exists.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU ESE Module-end exams.**

| Property | Test / Condition | Quick Recall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Memoryless (Static)** | $y[n]$ depends only on $x[n]$ | No $n-1, n+1$ in RHS |
| **Linear** | Superposition holds | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a y_1 + b y_2$ |
| **Time-Invariant** | Delay-in / Delay-out commutes | $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$ |
| **Causal** | No future inputs in $y[n]$ equation | RHS has $n, n-1, n-2$ only |
| **BIBO Stable (LTI)** | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | Impulse response is absolutely summable |
| **LTI Convolution** | $y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k]$ | Sum over $k$ |
| **Impulse Response** | $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$ | Output when input is $\delta[n]$ |
| **Step Response** | $s[n] = T\{u[n]\}$ | Output when input is unit step |
| **LTI relation** | $s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k]$ | Step is running sum of impulse |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Signal Processing for Robotics**, discrete-time systems form the backbone of:
1. **Motor control loops:** PID controllers are discrete-time systems running on microcontrollers.
2. **Sensor fusion:** Kalman filters are LTI or time-varying discrete-time systems.
3. **Audio/Voice interfaces:** Speech recognition pipelines are cascades of LTI and non-linear discrete-time systems.
4. **Image processing in robots:** Each pixel value processed as a sequence $x[n]$ through a 2D discrete-time filter.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Convolution Sum for LTI Systems

The convolution sum is the **most high-yield KTU derivation**. We derive it rigorously below.

### Step 1 — Express the input as a sum of weighted impulses

Any discrete-time signal $x[n]$ can be decomposed into a sum of scaled and shifted impulses $\delta[n-k]$. This is the **sifting property** representation:

$$x[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, \delta[n-k]$$

**Logic:** At time instant $n = k$, the impulse $\delta[n-k]$ is non-zero only when $n=k$, picking out the coefficient $x[k]$.

### Step 2 — Apply the system operator

Let the system $T\{\cdot\}$ act on this decomposed input. Using the linearity property:

$$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} = T\left\{ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, \delta[n-k] \right\}$$

By linearity (scaling and additivity pass through $T\{\cdot\}$):

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, T\{\delta[n-k]\}$$

### Step 3 — Use Time-Invariance

Define the impulse response as the response to a unit impulse at $n=0$:

$$h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$$

By the time-invariance property, the response to $\delta[n-k]$ is simply $h[n-k]$:

$$T\{\delta[n-k]\} = h[n-k]$$

### Step 4 — Combine to obtain the convolution sum

Substituting back, we obtain the canonical convolution sum:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k] \, h[n-k] = x[n] * h[n]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Commutativity of Convolution:** $x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]$. This is a favorite KTU short-answer question.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Convolution of Two Finite-Length Sequences

**Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2024 style]:** Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where $x[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ (for $n = 0, 1, 2$) and $h[n] = \{1, 1\}$ (for $n = 0, 1$). Both are zero elsewhere.

### Step 1 — Identify the lengths
Length of $x$ = 3, length of $h$ = 2. Output length = $3 + 2 - 1 = 4$, indexed $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

### Step 2 — Apply the convolution sum $y[n] = \sum_k x[k] h[n-k]$

For $n = 0$:

$$y[0] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[-k] = x[0] h[0] + x[1] h[-1] + x[2] h[-2] = (1)(1) + 0 + 0 = 1$$

For $n = 1$:

$$y[1] = x[0] h[1] + x[1] h[0] + x[2] h[-1] = (1)(1) + (2)(1) + 0 = 3$$

For $n = 2$:

$$y[2] = x[0] h[2] + x[1] h[1] + x[2] h[0] = 0 + (2)(1) + (1)(1) = 3$$

For $n = 3$:

$$y[3] = x[0] h[3] + x[1] h[2] + x[2] h[1] = 0 + 0 + (1)(1) = 1$$

### Step 3 — Write the final output sequence

$$y[n] = \{1, 3, 3, 1\} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

## 3.3 Derivation: BIBO Stability Condition for LTI Systems

### Step 1 — Start with a bounded input
Assume $\vert x[n] \vert \le M_x$ for all $n$. The output of an LTI system is:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k]$$

### Step 2 — Take absolute value and apply the triangle inequality
$$\vert y[n] \vert = \left\vert \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] \, x[n-k] \right\vert \le \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert \cdot \vert x[n-k] \vert$$

### Step 3 — Apply the boundedness of $x[n]$
Since $\vert x[n-k] \vert \le M_x$:

$$\vert y[n] \vert \le M_x \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert$$

### Step 4 — Conclude stability
For $y[n]$ to remain bounded, we require:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[k] \vert < \infty$$

This is the **absolute summability condition** for BIBO stability of LTI discrete-time systems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Property Identification Rules

Given a system equation, KTU examiners expect fast identification. Use this matrix:

| If the equation contains… | Then it is… |
| :--- | :--- |
| Only $x[n]$ (no past/future) | Static / Memoryless |
| $x[n-1], x[n-2], \ldots$ | Dynamic (with memory) |
| $x[n+1], x[n+2], \ldots$ | Non-Causal |
| Powers like $x^2[n], \log(x[n])$ | Non-Linear |
| Coefficients that change with $n$ (e.g., $n \cdot x[n]$) | Time-Variant |
| Constant coefficients with $x[n-k]$ form | Linear and Time-Invariant |
| $\sum \vert h[n] \vert = \infty$ | Unstable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Discrete-Time System

The following Mermaid block illustrates the high-level functional architecture of a generic DTS as it would appear in a KTU robotics signal chain.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcX[Input Sequence x of n] --> quantA[Anti Alias Filter]
    quantA --> sampA[Sampler at Fs Hz]
    sampA --> procA[DTS Operator T]
    procA --> outA[Output Sequence y of n]
    procA --> memA[Memory Block Holds x of n minus k]
    memA --> procA
    procA --> charA[Characterize via h of n]
    classDef io fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,color:#0B2545
    classDef core fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,color:#0B2545
    classDef aux fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#188038,color:#0B2545
    class srcX,outA io
    class procA,memA core
    class quantA,sampA,charA aux
```

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart for Discrete-Time Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Discrete Time System y of n equals T of x of n] --> q1{Depends on past or future samples}
    q1 -- No --> statA[Static System]
    q1 -- Yes --> dynA[Dynamic System]
    startA --> q2{Superposition holds}
    q2 -- Yes --> linA[Linear System]
    q2 -- No --> nonlinA[Non Linear System]
    startA --> q3{Shift in input equals shift in output}
    q3 -- Yes --> tiA[Time Invariant System]
    q3 -- No --> tvA[Time Variant System]
    startA --> q4{Uses future samples}
    q4 -- No --> causA[Causal System]
    q4 -- Yes --> ncausA[Non Causal System]
    startA --> q5{Sum of absolute h of n is finite}
    q5 -- Yes --> stabA[BIBO Stable System]
    q5 -- No --> ustabA[Unstable System]
```

## 4.3 Convolution Computation Process (Tabular Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[Input x of n] --> flip1[Reverse h of k to get h of minus k]
    flip1 --> shift1[Shift h of minus k by n]
    shift1 --> mult1[Multiply x of k and shifted h]
    mult1 --> sum1[Sum over all k]
    sum1 --> out1[Output y of n]
    sum1 --> loop1[Increment n and repeat]
    loop1 --> shift1
    classDef steps fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#F9AB00,color:#0B2545
    class in1,flip1,shift1,mult1,sum1,out1,loop1 steps
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`in1`, `flip1`, `q1`, etc.). No reserved keywords used. No bold or italic markers inside quoted labels.

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a discrete-time system. Give two examples from robotics. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A discrete-time system is a mathematical operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input sequence $x[n]$ to an output sequence $y[n]$, both defined only at integer instants $n$. Formally: $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$.

**Two robotics examples (2 marks):**
1. **PID Motor Speed Controller:** Input $x[n]$ = desired speed error sampled at $F_s = 1$ kHz; output $y[n]$ = PWM duty cycle applied to the DC motor driver.
2. **Digital Accelerometer Filter:** Input $x[n]$ = raw accelerometer reading; output $y[n]$ = filtered velocity estimate for the robot's inertial navigation.

---

### Q2. State and explain the BIBO stability condition for an LTI discrete-time system. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A discrete-time system is **BIBO stable** if every bounded input $x[n]$ produces a bounded output $y[n]$.

$$\text{If } \vert x[n] \vert \le M_x < \infty \text{ for all } n, \text{ then } \vert y[n] \vert < \infty \text{ for all } n.$$

For an **LTI system**, this is equivalent to the **absolute summability of the impulse response**:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$

If the sum diverges, even a bounded input can drive the output to infinity, making the system unstable.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** With the help of the superposition principle, define a linear discrete-time system. Prove that the system described by $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ is linear but time-variant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the convolution $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where $x[n] = u[n] - u[n-3]$ and $h[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$. Sketch $y[n]$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a)

**Definition (2 marks):** A discrete-time system is **linear** if it satisfies the superposition principle: for any two inputs $x_1[n], x_2[n]$ and scalars $a, b$:

$$T\{a \cdot x_1[n] + b \cdot x_2[n]\} = a \cdot T\{x_1[n]\} + b \cdot T\{x_2[n]\}$$

**Linearity Test (3 marks):** For $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$:
- Let $y_1[n] = T\{x_1[n]\} = n \cdot x_1[n]$
- Let $y_2[n] = T\{x_2[n]\} = n \cdot x_2[n]$
- Combined input: $x[n] = a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]$
- $T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = n (a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]) = a (n x_1[n]) + b (n x_2[n]) = a y_1[n] + b y_2[n]$

Hence, the system is **linear**.

**Time-Invariance Test (2 marks):** Shift input by $n_0$: $x_1[n] = x[n - n_0]$. Output:

$$y_1[n] = n \cdot x[n - n_0]$$

Now shift the original output: $y[n - n_0] = (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0]$.

Since $n \cdot x[n-n_0] \neq (n - n_0) \cdot x[n - n_0]$ in general, the system is **time-variant**. The coefficient $n$ depends on the time index itself, breaking TI.

---

#### Solution to A(b)

**Step 1 — Identify the sequences (2 marks):**
- $x[n] = u[n] - u[n-3]$ is a rectangular pulse of length 3: $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$ and 0 elsewhere.
- $h[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ for $n = 0, 1, 2$.

**Step 2 — Output length (1 mark):** Length $= 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$, indexed $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 3 — Apply the convolution sum (3 marks):**

$$y[0] = x[0] h[0] = (1)(1) = 1$$

$$y[1] = x[0] h[1] + x[1] h[0] = (1)(2) + (1)(1) = 3$$

$$y[2] = x[0] h[2] + x[1] h[1] + x[2] h[0] = (1)(1) + (1)(2) + (1)(1) = 4$$

$$y[3] = x[1] h[2] + x[2] h[1] = (1)(1) + (1)(2) = 3$$

$$y[4] = x[2] h[2] = (1)(1) = 1$$

**Step 4 — Final output (1 mark):**

$$y[n] = \{1, 3, 4, 3, 1\} \text{ for } n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$$

The plot is a symmetric **triangular-like sequence** peaking at $y[2] = 4$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Test whether the following systems are (i) linear, (ii) time-invariant, (iii) causal, (iv) BIBO stable: 
(1) $y[n] = x[n^2]$ 
(2) $y[n] = x[-n]$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an LTI system with impulse response $h[n] = a^n u[n]$ where $\vert a \vert < 1$, derive the step response $s[n]$ and determine BIBO stability. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a)

**System 1: $y[n] = x[n^2]$**

- **(i) Linearity (1 mark):** $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = (a x_1 + b x_2)[n^2] = a x_1[n^2] + b x_2[n^2] = a y_1 + b y_2$. **Linear.**
- **(ii) Time-Invariance (1 mark):** $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[(n)^2 - n_0]$? No — substitute $n \to n-n_0$ in $y[n]$: $y[n-n_0] = x[(n-n_0)^2]$, but $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[n^2 - n_0]$. These are not equal. **Time-Variant.**
- **(iii) Causality (1 mark):** At $n=0$, $y[0] = x[0]$ (causal). At $n=2$, $y[2] = x[4]$, which is a future input. **Non-Causal.**
- **(iv) Stability (1 mark):** If $\vert x[n] \vert \le M_x$, then $\vert y[n] \vert = \vert x[n^2] \vert \le M_x$. **BIBO Stable.**

**System 2: $y[n] = x[-n]$**

- **(i) Linearity (1 mark):** $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a x_1[-n] + b x_2[-n]$. **Linear.**
- **(ii) Time-Invariance (1 mark):** $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[-(n) - n_0]$? No. Substitute $n \to n-n_0$ in $y$: $y[n-n_0] = x[-(n-n_0)] = x[-n + n_0]$, but $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[-n - n_0]$. **Time-Variant** (it is a time-reversal operator).
- **(iii) Causality (1 mark):** At $n=-1$, $y[-1] = x[1]$, which is a future input. **Non-Causal.**
- **(iv) Stability (1 mark):** Bounded input $\Rightarrow$ bounded output (time reversal preserves magnitude). **BIBO Stable.**

---

#### Solution to B(b)

**Step 1 — Define the step response (1 mark):**

$$s[n] = T\{u[n]\} = h[n] * u[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k] u[n-k]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $h[k] = a^k u[k]$ (2 marks):**

$$s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a^k u[k] u[n-k]$$

The product $u[k] u[n-k]$ is non-zero only when $k \ge 0$ and $k \le n$, i.e., $0 \le k \le n$. So:

$$s[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} a^k$$

**Step 3 — Apply geometric series formula (3 marks):**
- For $n < 0$: no valid $k$, so $s[n] = 0$.
- For $n \ge 0$:

$$s[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} a^k = \frac{1 - a^{n+1}}{1 - a}, \quad a \neq 1$$

**Final step response:**

$$s[n] = \begin{cases} 0, & n < 0 \\ \dfrac{1 - a^{n+1}}{1 - a}, & n \ge 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — BIBO stability test (1 mark):**

$$\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert a \vert^n = \frac{1}{1 - \vert a \vert} < \infty \quad \text{(since } \vert a \vert < 1\text{)}$$

The impulse response is **absolutely summable**, so the system is **BIBO stable**. As $n \to \infty$, $s[n] \to 1/(1-a)$, confirming a bounded steady-state.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and lose 2–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Confusing static with memoryless in a delayed system.** $y[n] = x[n] - x[n-1]$ is dynamic, NOT static. Static means $y$ depends ONLY on $x[n]$ at the *same* index.
> 2. **Forgetting to check both additivity and homogeneity** for linearity. KTU examiners award only 1 of 2 marks if you verify just additivity.
> 3. **Mis-indexing convolution.** Off-by-one errors in $n$ vs $k$ indices are the #1 cause of wrong answers. Always sketch $x[k]$ and $h[n-k]$ on the same axis before summing.
> 4. **Stating BIBO stability without absolute summability check.** Writing "system is stable because input is bounded" is incomplete. You MUST show $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ for LTI systems.
> 5. **Forgetting that $y[n] = x[-n]$ is non-causal.** Many students mark it causal because "negative index" feels like past — but $y[-1] = x[1]$ is a *future* input.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Discrete-Time System** is a mapping $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$ where $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **LTI Systems** are fully characterized by their **impulse response** $h[n]$.
- **Convolution Sum:** $y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[n-k] = \sum_{k} h[k] x[n-k]$ (commutative).
- **Six KTU-tested properties:** Static/Dynamic, Linear/Non-linear, TI/TV, Causal/Non-causal, Stable/Unstable, Invertible/Non-invertible.
- **BIBO Stability (LTI):** $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ — absolute summability of impulse response.
- **Causality in equations:** No $x[n+1], x[n+2], \ldots$ on the right-hand side.
- **Time-Invariance trap:** Coefficients depending on $n$ (e.g., $n \cdot x[n]$) make a system time-variant.
- **Convolution output length:** $L_x + L_h - 1$ samples for two finite-length sequences.
- **Step response** is the running sum of the impulse response: $s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k]$.
- **In robotics:** Almost all real-time controllers (PID, Kalman, FIR filters) are LTI discrete-time systems.
- **Quick sanity check:** If a system uses only $x[n]$ on the RHS with constant coefficients, it is **Linear, Causal, Static, TI** (if it has no delay terms). Always verify all four.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Domain Characterization of LTI Discrete-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Domain Characterization of LTI Discrete-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) Discrete-Time System** is a mathematical operator $\mathcal{T}$ that maps an input discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ to an output discrete-time sequence $y[n]$ such that:
