# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Frequency-Response Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following diagram illustrates the magnitude response $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ of all four filter types on a common frequency axis, with the cutoff frequencies $\omega_c$ marked.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inNode[Input Signal x of t] --> splitter{Domain Split}
    splitter --> lpfA[LPF Block]
    splitter --> hpfA[HPF Block]
    splitter --> bpfA[BPF Block]
    splitter --> bsfA[BSF Block]
    lpfA --> lpfOut[Pass omega below omega_c]
    hpfA --> hpfOut[Pass omega above omega_c]
    bpfA --> bpfOut[Pass omega from omega_L to omega_H]
    bsfA --> bsfOut[Reject omega from omega_L to omega_H]
    lpfOut --> sumMix[Reconstruction]
    hpfOut --> sumMix
    bpfOut --> sumMix
    bsfOut --> sumMix
    sumMix --> yNode[Output Signal y of t]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The splitter represents the **parallel analysis** of the same input signal by all four filter types. In practice, a designer chooses only one filter for a given application. The reconstruction block is shown for didactic completeness, illustrating that filters are **selective** operators on the frequency spectrum.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Robot Sensor Filtering Pipeline

For a humanoid robot's IMU + encoder signal chain, the filtering pipeline is:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sensorA[IMU Accelerometer] --> preA[Analog Anti Alias LPF]
    sensorB[IMU Gyroscope] --> preB[Analog Anti Alias LPF]
    sensorC[Wheel Encoder] --> preC[Schmitt Trigger + LPF]
    preA --> adcA[ADC Sampler]
    preB --> adcB[ADC Sampler]
    preC --> adcC[ADC Sampler]
    adcA --> dig1[Digital HPF to Remove DC Bias]
    adcB --> dig2[Digital LPF to Smooth Noise]
    adcC --> dig3[Digital HPF to Remove Drift]
    dig1 --> fusion[Sensor Fusion Kalman Filter]
    dig2 --> fusion
    dig3 --> fusion
    fusion --> ctrl[Robot Controller]
    ctrl --> actA[Motor Driver LPF for PWM smoothing]
    actA --> motorA[DC Motor]
    fusion --> odometry[Odometry Estimator]
    odometry --> nav[Path Planner]
```

> **Key Insight:** Notice that filters appear at **three distinct points** in the robot: (1) analog preprocessing at the sensor, (2) digital processing in the microcontroller, and (3) post-control smoothing at the actuator. This is why **filter design is a system-level concern**, not just a component-level one.

---

## 4.3 Magnitude Response Comparison (Conceptual Mermaid Plot)

```mermaid
graph TB
    axisX[Frequency Axis omega increases left to right]
    lpfCurve["LPF: High at low omega, falls toward 0 at high omega"]
    hpfCurve["HPF: Rises from 0 at low omega, plateaus at 1 at high omega"]
    bpfCurve["BPF: 0 at extremes, peaks at center frequency omega0"]
    bsfCurve["BSF: 1 at extremes, dips to 0 at notch frequency"]
    axisX --- lpfCurve
    axisX --- hpfCurve
    axisX --- bpfCurve
    axisX --- bsfCurve
```

> **Note to KTU students:** While Mermaid cannot render true Bode plots, the conceptual relationship above maps directly to the standard magnitude-versus-frequency graph every examiner expects. You are expected to **hand-draw** such plots in your answer sheets.

---

## 4.4 Analog vs. Digital Filter Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    aIn[Analog Input x t] --> aChain[RC Network Op Amp Stages]
    aChain --> aOut[Analog Output y t]
    dIn[Analog Input x t] --> adc[Digitize via ADC]
    adc --> dsp[Microprocessor FPGA DSP]
    dsp --> dac[Reconstruct via DAC]
    dac --> dOut[Analog Output y t]
```

| Property | Analog Filter | Digital Filter |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Implementation | Op-amps, R, L, C | Algorithm on processor |
| Frequency range | Limited by component parasitics | Limited by sample rate $f_s$ |
| Tunability | Replace components | Change coefficients in code |
| Phase response | Non-linear phase | Linear phase possible (FIR) |
| Robotics use | Sensor front-end, anti-aliasing | IMU fusion, audio beamforming |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "cutoff frequency" of a filter. For a first-order RC low-pass filter, derive an expression for the cutoff frequency in terms of $R$ and $C$.**

**Model Answer:**

The cutoff frequency $f_c$ of a filter is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (approximately $70.71\%$) of its maximum passband value, corresponding to an attenuation of **3 dB**.

For a first-order RC low-pass filter, the transfer function is:

$$H_{LP}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The magnitude is:

$$\vert H_{LP}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

Setting $\vert H_{LP}(j\omega_c) \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \implies 1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2$$

$$\omega_c RC = 1 \implies \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \quad \text{and} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a band-pass filter and a band-stop (notch) filter. State one robotics application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Band-Pass Filter (BPF) | Band-Stop / Notch Filter (BSF) |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Frequency Selection** | Passes a specific band, rejects both lower and higher frequencies | Passes all frequencies except a specific band |
| **Magnitude Response** | Peaks at center frequency $\omega_0$ | Dips to zero (notch) at center frequency $\omega_0$ |
| **Realization** | Cascade of HPF and LPF | Parallel combination of LPF and HPF outputs |
| **Robotics Application** | Extracting vibration signatures (8–12 Hz) from a surgical robot end-effector for tremor compensation | Removing 50 Hz power-line hum from EEG signals used to control a brain-computer-interface (BCI) wheelchair |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice (a) or (b). Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are fully worked alternatives. Pick ONE for your exam preparation.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the transfer function of a first-order **RC low-pass filter**. Plot its magnitude and phase Bode plot, marking the $-3$ dB cutoff point. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A robotic wheelchair's ultrasonic distance sensor outputs a 40 kHz burst with white noise. Design a first-order RC low-pass filter to suppress the noise above 50 kHz while passing the 40 kHz signal with minimum distortion. Specify $R$ and $C$ values. *(7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Draw the circuit.** A series resistor $R$ between input and output node, with capacitor $C$ from output node to ground. [1 mark]

**Step 2: Apply KVL.**

$$V_{in} = iR + V_{out}, \quad i = C \frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

$$V_{in} = RC \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out}$$

**Step 3: Laplace Transform.**

$$V_{in}(s) = (1 + sRC) V_{out}(s)$$

$$\boxed{H_{LP}(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} = \frac{\omega_c}{s + \omega_c}}$$

where $\omega_c = 1/(RC)$. [2 marks]

**Step 4: Magnitude Response.**

$$\vert H_{LP}(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 5: Phase Response.**

$$\angle H_{LP}(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Step 6: Bode plot values.**

- At $\omega \to 0$: $\vert H \vert = 1$ (0 dB), $\angle H = 0°$.
- At $\omega = \omega_c = 1/(RC)$: $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ ($-3$ dB), $\angle H = -45°$.
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to 0$, $\angle H \to -90°$.

Slope of magnitude: $-20$ dB/decade after $\omega_c$. [2 marks]

**Step 7: Sketch Bode plot.**

```
|H|dB
  0 ─────────╲
              ╲
 -3 ───────────╲___   ← Cutoff at ωc
                  ╲
-20               ╲___ ← -20 dB/decade
                          log(ω)
```

[2 marks]

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Design Constraint.** The cutoff frequency $f_c$ must be set to suppress noise above 50 kHz. To pass 40 kHz with minimum distortion, choose $f_c$ slightly above 40 kHz, e.g., $f_c = 50$ kHz. [1 mark]

**Step 2: Compute the RC product.**

$$RC = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 50{,}000} = 3.183 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

[1 mark]

**Step 3: Choose a standard capacitor value.** Take $C = 10$ nF $= 10^{-8}$ F. [1 mark]

**Step 4: Compute R.**

$$R = \frac{3.183 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-8}} = 318.3\ \Omega$$

Use the standard value $R = 330\ \Omega$. [1 mark]

**Step 5: Verify attenuation at noise frequency (say 100 kHz).**

$$\frac{f_n}{f_c} = \frac{100}{50} = 2$$

$$\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 4}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \approx 0.447$$

$$A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(0.447) \approx -6.99 \text{ dB}$$

This attenuation is **insufficient** at 100 kHz. To improve, use a **second-order** filter (two cascaded RC sections or an Sallen-Key topology), achieving $-40$ dB/decade roll-off and approximately $-14$ dB at 100 kHz. [2 marks]

**Step 6: Final design summary.** A second-order Butterworth low-pass filter with $f_c = 50$ kHz is recommended, using $R_1 = R_2 = 330\ \Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 10$ nF in a Sallen-Key configuration with unity-gain op-amp follower. [1 mark]

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With necessary diagrams, explain the working of a **band-pass filter** realized as a cascade of high-pass and low-pass first-order sections. Define center frequency $\omega_0$ and quality factor $Q$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mobile robot's vibration sensor picks up motor-induced vibration at 120 Hz and 180 Hz. The desired control signal lies in the 40 Hz – 80 Hz band. Design a band-pass filter with $\omega_L = 40$ Hz and $\omega_H = 80$ Hz. Compute the center frequency and quality factor. *(7 marks)*

---

### ⭐ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Block diagram.** [1 mark]

```
x(t) → [HPF: ω_L] → [LPF: ω_H] → y(t)
```

**Step 2: HPF stage** (cutoff $\omega_L$):

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s}{s + \omega_L}$$

**Step 3: LPF stage** (cutoff $\omega_H$):

$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{\omega_H}{s + \omega_H}$$

**Step 4: Cascade multiplication:**

$$H_{BP}(s) = H_{HP}(s) \cdot H_{LP}(s) = \frac{\omega_H \cdot s}{(s + \omega_L)(s + \omega_H)}$$

**Step 5: Expand and rewrite** in standard form [1 mark]:

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{\omega_H \cdot s}{s^2 + s(\omega_L + \omega_H) + \omega_L \omega_H}$$

**Step 6: Center frequency.** Defined as the geometric mean of the two cutoffs [1 mark]:

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_L \cdot \omega_H}$$

**Step 7: Bandwidth and Q factor** [1 mark]:

$$B = \omega_H - \omega_L, \quad Q = \frac{\omega_0}{B} = \frac{\sqrt{\omega_L \omega_H}}{\omega_H - \omega_L}$$

**Step 8: Magnitude plot description** [1 mark]:

- Zero gain at $\omega \to 0$ and $\omega \to \infty$.
- Peak gain $Q/\sqrt{2}$ at $\omega = \omega_0$.
- Two asymptotic slopes: $+20$ dB/decade below $\omega_L$ and $-20$ dB/decade above $\omega_H$.

**Step 9: Robotics use case** [1 mark]: A BPF is used in a service robot's microphone array to extract human voice (300–3400 Hz) while rejecting motor and environmental noise.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Convert to rad/s** [1 mark]:

$$\omega_L = 2\pi f_L = 2\pi \times 40 = 80\pi \approx 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_H = 2\pi f_H = 2\pi \times 80 = 160\pi \approx 502.65 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Center frequency** [1 mark]:

$$\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_L \cdot \omega_H} = \sqrt{251.33 \times 502.65} = \sqrt{126{,}330.9} \approx 355.43 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Center frequency in Hz** [1 mark]:

$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{355.43}{2\pi} \approx 56.57 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4: Bandwidth** [1 mark]:

$$B = \omega_H - \omega_L = 502.65 - 251.33 = 251.33 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$B_{Hz} = f_H - f_L = 80 - 40 = 40 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5: Quality factor** [1 mark]:

$$Q = \frac{\omega_0}{B} = \frac{355.43}{251.33} \approx 1.414$$

**Step 6: Interpretation** [1 mark]: Since $Q \approx 1.414$, this is a moderate-selectivity BPF. The motor vibration tones at 120 Hz and 180 Hz lie well above the upper cutoff (80 Hz) and are therefore attenuated by at least $-20$ dB/decade, i.e., a factor of 4 per doubling of frequency. At 120 Hz (1.5× the upper cutoff), the attenuation is approximately $20 \log_{10}(1/1.5) \approx -3.5$ dB, which is **not enough**; a second-order BPF would provide $-40$ dB/decade roll-off, yielding $\approx -14$ dB attenuation at 120 Hz.

**Step 7: Recommendation** [1 mark]: Use a second-order (or higher) BPF for improved roll-off. A two-stage cascade doubles the slope and gives $\approx -14$ dB at 120 Hz and $\approx -20$ dB at 180 Hz.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units.** Writing $f_c = 1/RC$ instead of $\omega_c = 1/RC$ loses 1 mark. Always specify rad/s for $\omega$ and Hz for $f$.
> 2. **Confusing passband with stopband.** Many students swap the definitions; remember passband = where the signal *passes*, stopband = where it is *rejected*.
> 3. **Skipping the magnitude derivation.** Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the step where you set $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and solve for $\omega_c$. Never skip this.
> 4. **Not drawing the Bode plot.** Even a rough sketch with cutoff marked earns 2 marks in Part (a) of any 7-mark question.
> 5. **Mixing up BPF and BSF definitions.** A BPF *passes* a band, a BSF *rejects* a band. Mnemonic: **B-Pass = Passes**, **B-Stop = Stops**.
> 6. **Ignoring robotics context.** KTU 2024 questions often award 1 mark for stating the *real-world robotics application*. Always end with a one-line application statement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ High-Density Revision Checklist

- [x] A **filter** is an LTI system that selectively passes/rejects frequency components of a signal.
- [x] **Convolution theorem:** $y(t) = h(t) * x(t)$ in time domain $\Leftrightarrow Y(\omega) = H(\omega) \cdot X(\omega)$ in frequency domain.
- [x] **Cutoff frequency $\omega_c$** is the $-3$ dB point where the gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its passband value.
- [x] **Roll-off rate** is $20N$ dB/decade, where $N$ is the filter order.
- [x] **LPF:** passes low frequencies, rejects high. Transfer function: $H_{LP}(s) = \omega_c / (s + \omega_c)$.
- [x] **HPF:** passes high frequencies, rejects low. Transfer function: $H_{HP}(s) = s / (s + \omega_c)$.
- [x] **BPF:** passes a band. Transfer function: $H_{BP}(s) = B s / (s^2 + B s + \omega_0^2)$.
- [x] **BSF/Notch:** rejects a band. Transfer function: $H_{BS}(s) = (s^2 + \omega_0^2) / (s^2 + B s + \omega_0^2)$.
- [x] **Center frequency** of a BPF: $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\omega_L \omega_H}$ (geometric mean).
- [x] **Quality factor:** $Q = \omega_0 / B$ (higher $Q$ = narrower band, more selective).
- [x] **Nyquist criterion:** sample rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- [x] **SNR** is the most common metric to evaluate noise reduction quality in dB.
- [x] In **robotics**, filters are used at three levels: sensor front-end, digital signal processing, and actuator command smoothing.
- [x] **Active filters** use op-amps for gain + filtering; **passive filters** use only R, L, C.
- [x] **Complementary filter** = LPF (gyro) + HPF (accelerometer) is the simplest IMU fusion method.
- [x] **Notch filter** is a BSF with very high $Q$, used to eliminate a single tone (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum).

> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 2024 answer sheets, always (1) state the definition, (2) write the formula, (3) show at least one numerical step, (4) draw the Bode sketch, and (5) end with a robotics application. Following this five-part structure on every question typically secures **95%+ of available marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Implementation of Digital Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design and Implementation of Digital Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system that performs mathematical operations on a sampled, discrete-time signal to reduce or enhance specific aspects of that signal. Mathematically, it is implemented as a linear constant-coefficient difference equation:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]$$

where $x[n]$ is the input, $y[n]$ is the output, and the sets $\{b_k\}$ and $\{a_k\}$ are the filter coefficients. The corresponding **transfer function** is:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{1 + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A digital filter is a computational algorithm that transforms a discrete input sequence $x[n]$ into a discrete output sequence $y[n]$ according to a pre-defined frequency-selective specification. The **z-transform** $H(z)$ characterises its behaviour completely in the complex frequency domain.

The two principal families used throughout the KTU syllabus are:

- **FIR (Finite Impulse Response)** filters — when all $a_k = 0$. They are inherently stable and have a *linear phase* response.
- **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response)** filters — when at least one $a_k \neq 0$. They are recursive and can mimic classical analog filter responses efficiently.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Coffee Sieve"**
> Imagine pouring a mixture of coarse coffee grounds and fine powder through a kitchen sieve. The sieve is like a **low-pass filter** — it lets the *fine* particles (low frequencies, i.e. slow, smooth sensor drift) pass through but blocks the *coarse* chunks (high frequencies, i.e. sudden spikes, electrical noise, vibration from robot motors). A digital filter is essentially a mathematical "sieve" applied to sensor data from a robot (e.g., IMU, encoder, LiDAR) to keep only the useful motion information and discard unwanted noise.

**Why does this matter in Robotics?**
In a real robot, every sensor signal is contaminated with:
- **High-frequency noise** from motor commutation, EMI, encoder quantisation → removed by a **low-pass filter**.
- **Low-frequency drift** from temperature changes, bias in gyroscopes → removed by a **high-pass filter**.
- **50/60 Hz power-line interference** → removed by a **band-stop (notch) filter**.

A properly designed digital filter runs in real time inside the robot's microcontroller, shaping the signal *before* it reaches the controller — directly improving trajectory tracking, stability, and safety.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Frequency $F_s$** (Hz) — typically **1 kHz to 50 kHz** in robotic control loops.
- **Normalised cut-off frequency** $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s$ (radians/sample, dimensionless).
- **Digital frequency** $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ or $f \in [-F_s/2, F_s/2]$.
- **Pass-band ripple** $A_p$ (in dB), **Stop-band attenuation** $A_s$ (in dB).
- **Transition width** $\Delta f$ (Hz) between pass-band and stop-band edges.

> [!TIP]
> The **Nyquist frequency** $F_N = F_s / 2$ is the hard upper limit on signals that can be processed. Any analog component above $F_N$ will **alias** into the baseband — a catastrophic distortion that *no filter can undo* after the fact. This is why **anti-aliasing analog pre-filtering** is mandatory in robotic data acquisition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Specifications — The Four-Parameter Problem

Every digital filter design begins with the user defining four real-world parameters. These become the **design contract** between the engineer and the algorithm.

| Specification | Symbol | Typical Value (Robotics) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pass-band edge frequency | $\omega_p$ | 0.1π to 0.4π | Frequencies below this pass with at most $A_p$ dB of ripple |
| Stop-band edge frequency | $\omega_s$ | 0.5π to 0.7π | Frequencies above this are attenuated by at least $A_s$ dB |
| Pass-band ripple | $A_p$ | 0.1 to 3 dB | Maximum allowed amplitude variation in the pass-band |
| Stop-band attenuation | $A_s$ | 40 to 80 dB | Minimum required attenuation in the stop-band |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The trade-off triangle in filter design is *selectivity* (sharp transition from pass- to stop-band), *phase linearity*, and *computational cost*. You can only optimise two of the three at a time.

## 2.2 FIR vs IIR — Strategic Comparison

| Property | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Impulse response length | Finite (length $N$) | Infinite (theoretically) |
| Stability | **Always stable** (all-pole at $z=0$) | Conditionally stable |
| Phase response | **Can be exactly linear** | Non-linear in general |
| Computational cost per sample | Higher (needs $N$ taps) | Lower (recursion) |
| Memory required | Stores $N$ past inputs | Stores $N$ past inputs + past outputs |
| Design complexity | Simpler (windowing / frequency sampling) | Harder (requires analog prototype + transformation) |
| Robotics use case | Sensor fusion pre-filtering, anti-alias | Control-loop compensators, audio equalisation |

## 2.3 FIR Filter Design — The Window Method (Stepwise Logic)

**Why the window method?** Multiplying an ideal (infinitely long) impulse response by a finite *window* function in the time domain *convolves* the ideal frequency response with the window's spectrum, producing a practical, realisable filter.

**Step-by-step design procedure:**

1. **Pick the ideal target response** $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ based on specifications (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, band-stop).
2. **Compute the ideal impulse response** $h_d[n]$ via the inverse discrete-time Fourier transform (IDTFT).
3. **Truncate to length $N$** by multiplying with a window $w[n]$ of length $N$:
   $$h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n], \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$
4. **Verify** the resulting $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ satisfies the original $A_p$, $A_s$ requirements. If not, increase $N$ or change window type.

### 2.3.1 Common Window Functions

| Window | Main-lobe width | Side-lobe level (dB) | Transition width $\Delta\omega$ | Stop-band attenuation (dB) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $4\pi / N$ | $-13$ | $0.92\pi / N$ | **21** |
| Hann (Hanning) | $8\pi / N$ | $-31$ | $3.11\pi / N$ | **44** |
| Hamming | $8\pi / N$ | $-41$ | $3.32\pi / N$ | **53** |
| Blackman | $12\pi / N$ | $-57$ | $5.56\pi / N$ | **74** |
| Kaiser ($\beta = 8$) | Variable | Variable | $\approx 2.93\pi / N$ | **50–80 (tunable)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kaiser Window Rule-of-Thumb (KTU favourite):** For a required stop-band attenuation $A_s$ (dB), the Kaiser parameter $\beta$ is chosen as:
> $$\beta = \begin{cases} 0.1102 (A_s - 8.7) & A_s > 50 \\ 0.5842 (A_s - 21)^{0.4} + 0.07886 (A_s - 21) & 21 \le A_s \le 50 \\ 0 & A_s < 21 \end{cases}$$
> and the filter order $N$ must satisfy $N \ge (A_s - 8) / (2.285 \, \Delta\omega)$, where $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$.

### 2.3.2 Ideal Impulse Response of a Low-Pass FIR Filter

For a low-pass filter with digital cut-off frequency $\omega_c$ and linear phase (constant group delay $\alpha = (N-1)/2$):

$$h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1, \quad h_d[\alpha] = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

## 2.4 IIR Filter Design — The Bilinear Transform Method

### 2.4.1 Why the Bilinear Transform?

The bilinear transform (BLT) is the **workhorse** of IIR design in KTU exams because it **maps the entire analog $s$-plane onto the unit circle of the digital $z$-plane** — eliminating aliasing completely. It is defined as:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad z = \frac{1 + (sT/2)}{1 - (sT/2)}$$

where $T = 1/F_s$ is the sampling period. The pre-warping equation that corrects the non-linear frequency warping is:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The BLT compresses the infinite analog frequency axis $\Omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$ into the finite digital range $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi)$. This is the price of eliminating aliasing — you must **pre-warp** your analog prototype's critical frequencies.

### 2.4.2 Standard Analog Prototype Filters

| Filter | Magnitude-squared response $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2$ | Key feature |
|---|---|---|
| Butterworth | $\dfrac{1}{1 + (\Omega / \Omega_c)^{2N}}$ | **Maximally flat** pass-band |
| Chebyshev-I | $\dfrac{1}{1 + \epsilon^2 T_N^2(\Omega / \Omega_c)}$ | Equi-ripple pass-band |
| Chebyshev-II | $\dfrac{1}{1 + [\epsilon^2 T_N^2(\Omega_s / \Omega)]^{-1}}$ | Equi-ripple stop-band |
| Elliptic | $\dfrac{1}{1 + \epsilon^2 U_N^2(\Omega / \Omega_c)}$ | Equi-ripple in **both** bands |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | General difference equation | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]$ |
| 2 | Transfer function | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ |
| 3 | Frequency response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ |
| 4 | Magnitude (dB) | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ |
| 5 | Group delay | $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d}{d\omega} \angle H(e^{j\omega})$ |
| 6 | Linear-phase condition | $\tau_g(\omega) = \alpha = \dfrac{N-1}{2}$ (constant) |
| 7 | Bilinear transform | $s = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ |
| 8 | Pre-warping | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ |
| 9 | Ideal LPF impulse response | $h_d[n] = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}$ |
| 10 | Order of FIR (Kaiser) | $N \ge \dfrac{A_s - 8}{2.285 \, \Delta\omega}$ |
| 11 | Stability condition | All poles of $H(z)$ must satisfy $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ |
| 12 | Linear-phase symmetry | $h[n] = \pm h[N-1-n]$ (anti-/symmetric) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability test (always check this in KTU exam):** A causal LTI digital filter is BIBO stable **if and only if** all poles of $H(z)$ lie *strictly inside* the unit circle, i.e. $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ for every pole $z_p$.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Robotic Manipulators:** Butterworth low-pass filters smooth encoder feedback before computing joint velocities, eliminating quantisation noise that would otherwise saturate the derivative term in a PD controller.
- **IMU Filtering:** High-pass filters remove DC bias from MEMS gyroscopes; complementary FIR filters fuse accelerometer and gyroscope data.
- **Audio & Speech Processing:** FIR band-pass filters in robot voice interfaces (e.g., Amazon Echo's "Alexa" trigger word detection) isolate the 1–4 kHz human speech band.
- **Image Processing in Vision-Based Robots:** 2D extensions of these 1D filters (separable convolution) perform edge detection and Gaussian smoothing.
- **Control Systems:** IIR notch filters at 50/60 Hz remove power-line hum from analog sensor channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bilinear Transform from Trapezoidal Integration

We derive the BLT by recognising that the $s$-domain integrator $1/s$ corresponds to integration in time, and the *trapezoidal rule* is one of the simplest numerical integrators.

**Step 1 — Trapezoidal approximation of the continuous integral.**

The continuous-time relationship for an integrator is $y(t) = \int_{(k-1)T}^{kT} x(\tau) \, d\tau + y((k-1)T)$. Apply the trapezoidal rule:

$$y(kT) = y((k-1)T) + \frac{T}{2} \big[ x((k-1)T) + x(kT) \big]$$

**Step 2 — Convert to discrete notation.**

Let $y[n] = y(nT)$ and $x[n] = x(nT)$. The recursion becomes:

$$y[n] = y[n-1] + \frac{T}{2} \big( x[n-1] + x[n] \big)$$

**Step 3 — Take the z-transform of both sides.**

$$Y(z) = z^{-1} Y(z) + \frac{T}{2} \big( z^{-1} X(z) + X(z) \big)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the integrator transfer function.**

$$Y(z) \big( 1 - z^{-1} \big) = \frac{T}{2} X(z) \big( 1 + z^{-1} \big)$$

$$\frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = H_I(z) = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Recover the BLT.**

In the analog world, the integrator is $H_a(s) = 1/s$. Equating $H_I(z) = 1/s$ at $z = e^{sT}$ (substituting $z^{-1} = e^{-sT}$):

$$\frac{1}{s} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + e^{-sT}}{1 - e^{-sT}}$$

Applying the half-angle identity $\dfrac{1 + e^{-sT}}{1 - e^{-sT}} = \coth(sT/2)$, and using $\coth(x) \approx 1/x + x/3 + \cdots$, inverting yields:

$$\boxed{\; s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} \;}$$

This is the **Bilinear Transform**, a one-to-one mapping of the $j\Omega$-axis onto the unit circle $e^{j\omega}$. ∎

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pre-Warping Equation

The BLT introduces a non-linear frequency compression. The analog frequency $\Omega$ corresponding to a desired digital frequency $\omega$ is found by substituting $s = j\Omega$ and $z = e^{j\omega}$ into the BLT:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega}}{1 + e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Step 1 — Factor the exponentials.**

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\omega/2} (e^{-j\omega/2} - e^{j\omega/2})}{e^{j\omega/2} (e^{-j\omega/2} + e^{j\omega/2})} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\omega/2} \cdot (-2j \sin(\omega/2))}{e^{j\omega/2} \cdot (2 \cos(\omega/2))}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify.**

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{-2j \sin(\omega/2)}{2 \cos(\omega/2)} = -\,\frac{2j}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Equate real and imaginary parts.**

$$\boxed{\; \Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right) \;}$$

This equation must be applied to **every critical frequency** of the analog prototype (pass-band edge, stop-band edge, notch frequency) *before* applying the BLT. Failure to pre-warp is the **#1 reason** for KTU students losing marks in IIR design problems. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Ideal Low-Pass FIR Impulse Response

We want a filter with $H_d(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega \alpha}$ for $\vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c$, and $0$ elsewhere. Apply the IDTFT:

$$h_d[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the definition of $H_d$.**

$$h_d[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{-j\omega \alpha} e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega (n - \alpha)} \, d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$h_d[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega (n - \alpha)}}{j (n - \alpha)} \right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi j (n - \alpha)} \left( e^{j\omega_c (n - \alpha)} - e^{-j\omega_c (n - \alpha)} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's identity.**

The numerator becomes $2j \sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))$:

$$h_d[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j (n - \alpha)} \cdot 2j \sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha)) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Special case $n = \alpha$ (the centre tap).**

By L'Hôpital's rule, the limit evaluates to the width of the pass-band:

$$h_d[\alpha] = \lim_{n \to \alpha} \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed form.**

$$\boxed{\; h_d[n] = \begin{cases} \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}, & n \neq \alpha \\[4pt] \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}, & n = \alpha \end{cases} \quad \text{where } \alpha = \frac{N-1}{2} \;}$$

This is the *sinc* function, sampled and centred at $\alpha$. After windowing with $w[n]$, it becomes the actual FIR coefficients $h[n]$. ∎

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — Bilinear Transform IIR Design

**Problem (KTU Style):** Design a digital Butterworth low-pass filter with the following specifications using the BLT method.
- Pass-band edge: $f_p = 1\text{ kHz}$, $A_p \le 1\text{ dB}$
- Stop-band edge: $f_s = 2\text{ kHz}$, $A_s \ge 15\text{ dB}$
- Sampling frequency: $F_s = 8\text{ kHz}$

**Step 1 — Compute digital frequencies.**

$$\omega_p = 2\pi \frac{f_p}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1000}{8000} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.25\pi \text{ rad}$$

$$\omega_s = 2\pi \frac{f_s}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{2000}{8000} = \frac{\pi}{2} = 0.5\pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Pre-warp to analog frequencies (with $T = 1/8000 = 0.000125$ s).**

$$\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{0.000125} \tan\!\left(\frac{\pi/4}{2}\right) = 16000 \cdot \tan(0.3927) = 16000 \cdot 0.4142 = 6627.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_s = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{2}\right) = 16000 \cdot \tan(\pi/4) = 16000 \cdot 1 = 16000 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the filter order $N$ for Butterworth.**

For Butterworth, the order is given by:

$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10} \left( \sqrt{\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}} \right)}{\log_{10}(\Omega_s / \Omega_p)}$$

Substituting $A_s = 15$, $A_p = 1$:

$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10} \sqrt{\dfrac{10^{1.5} - 1}{10^{0.1} - 1}}}{\log_{10}(16000 / 6627.4)} = \frac{\log_{10} \sqrt{\dfrac{30.62}{0.2589}}}{\log_{10}(2.414)} = \frac{\log_{10}(10.87)}{0.3828} = \frac{1.0362}{0.3828} = 2.71$$

So $N = 3$. Round **up** — never down — to satisfy attenuation.

**Step 4 — Find the analog Butterworth prototype with $\Omega_c = \Omega_p$.**

The normalised prototype for $N = 3$:

$$H_a(s') = \frac{1}{(s')^3 + 2(s')^2 + 2(s') + 1}$$

**Step 5 — Frequency-scale** by substituting $s' = s / \Omega_p$:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_p^3}{s^3 + 2\Omega_p s^2 + 2\Omega_p^2 s + \Omega_p^3}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the BLT** with $T = 0.000125$. The transformation $s = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ yields a rational $H(z)$ whose coefficients can be obtained by polynomial division or symbolic expansion.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exams, you typically only need to show the *form* of $H(z)$ and the locations of its poles — full numerical expansion of the $z^{-1}$ polynomial is usually not required unless explicitly asked.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

| Specification | FIR Choice | IIR Choice |
|---|---|---|
| Phase linearity is critical (e.g., image processing, control loop) | ✅ **FIR with linear phase** | ❌ IIR has non-linear phase |
| Very sharp transition needed with low order | ❌ FIR needs very high $N$ | ✅ IIR (Butterworth, elliptic) |
| Real-time robotic control on low-power MCU | ❌ FIR is heavy | ✅ IIR is efficient |
| No tolerance for instability | ✅ **FIR is always stable** | ❌ IIR can be unstable |
| Must mimic an existing analog sensor response | ❌ | ✅ IIR via BLT |
| Need tunable group delay (constant across frequency) | ✅ Symmetric FIR | ❌ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Design Flow for Digital Filter Realisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Problem Statement"] --> node2["Choose FIR or IIR Based on Specs"]
    node2 --> node3["Determine Digital Frequencies wp and ws"]
    node3 --> node4["Select Design Method"]
    node4 --> node5["FIR Window Method"]
    node4 --> node6["FIR Frequency Sampling"]
    node4 --> node7["IIR Bilinear Transform"]
    node4 --> node8["IIR Impulse Invariance"]
    node5 --> node9["Compute Ideal hd n"]
    node9 --> node10["Apply Window w n"]
    node10 --> node11["Verify Magnitude Specs"]
    node7 --> node12["Pre Warp Critical Frequencies"]
    node12 --> node13["Pick Analog Prototype"]
    node13 --> node14["Apply BLT Mapping"]
    node14 --> node15["Verify Stability"]
    node11 --> node16["Choose Realisation Structure"]
    node15 --> node16
    node16 --> node17["Direct Form I"]
    node16 --> node18["Direct Form II"]
    node16 --> node19["Cascade Form"]
    node16 --> node20["Parallel Form"]
    node17 --> node21["Implement on Target Hardware"]
    node18 --> node21
    node19 --> node21
    node20 --> node21
    node21 --> node22["Test with Real Sensor Data"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** This flowchart captures the standard KTU-recommended design pipeline. Notice the critical **verification loops** after the FIR window step and after the IIR pre-warping step. Skipping these is the most common cause of failed designs in lab courses.

## 4.2 Realisation Structure — Direct Form I (FIR and IIR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["x n"] --> nodeB["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Multiply b0"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Multiply b1"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Multiply b2"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Multiply b3"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Sum Node"]
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ["y n"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["Multiply -a1"]
    nodeK --> nodeN["Multiply -a2"]
    nodeM --> nodeI
    nodeN --> nodeI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The top row realises the **feed-forward (FIR) part** with coefficients $b_k$; the bottom row realises the **feedback (IIR) part** with coefficients $-a_k$. Direct Form I uses a *separate* delay line for input and output, leading to $M + N$ delay elements. It is conceptually simple but memory-inefficient for high-order filters.

## 4.3 Realisation Structure — Direct Form II (Canonical Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP["x n"] --> nodeQ["Sum Node"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Delay z^-1 Middle Signal w n"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Delay z^-1"]
    nodeR --> nodeU["Multiply b0"]
    nodeS --> nodeV["Multiply b1"]
    nodeT --> nodeW["Multiply b2"]
    nodeU --> nodeX["Sum Output"]
    nodeV --> nodeX
    nodeW --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeY["y n"]
    nodeR --> nodeZ["Multiply -a1"]
    nodeS --> nodeAA["Multiply -a2"]
    nodeZ --> nodeQ
    nodeAA --> nodeQ
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Canonical Property:** Direct Form II uses only $\max(M, N)$ delay elements — half of Direct Form I — by sharing a single *intermediate signal* $w[n]$ that flows between the feedback and feed-forward parts. This is the **canonical (minimum-memory)** realisation and is the KTU-preferred structure for implementation on resource-constrained microcontrollers.

## 4.4 Cascade and Parallel Realisation Overview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["x n"] --> nodeSec1["Second Order Section 1 b01 b11 b21 a11 a21"]
    nodeSec1 --> nodeSec2["Second Order Section 2 b02 b12 b22 a12 a22"]
    nodeSec2 --> nodeSec3["Second Order Section K b0K b1K b2K a1K a2K"]
    nodeSec3 --> nodeOut["y n"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Interpretation:** The transfer function $H(z)$ is factorised into $K$ second-order sections (biquads):
> $$H(z) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \frac{b_{0k} + b_{1k} z^{-1} + b_{2k} z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k} z^{-1} + a_{2k} z^{-2}}$$
> Each biquad runs sequentially. **Pairing rule:** Pair poles with their *nearest* zeros so that each biquad has a peaky magnitude response only where it is needed — this improves numerical stability on fixed-point DSP chips used in robot controllers.

## 4.5 Magnitude Response Comparison (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeL["Frequency Axis 0 to pi"] --> nodeP["Pass Band Region"]
    nodeP --> nodeT["Transition Band"]
    nodeT --> nodeS["Stop Band Region"]
    nodeT -.-> nodeTS["Width Delta omega"]
    nodeP -.-> nodeAP["Ripple Ap in dB"]
    nodeS -.-> nodeAS["Attenuation As in dB"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Interpretation:** The **transition width** $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ is the most expensive parameter — halving it roughly *doubles* the required filter order $N$ in both FIR and IIR cases. This is why KTU exam problems often quote specific $\Delta\omega$ values — to make the order calculation unambiguous.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
> **Differentiate between FIR and IIR digital filters. Mention any three advantages of FIR filters over IIR filters in robotic sensor signal processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Impulse response | Finite, length $N$ | Infinite (theoretically) |
| Recursion | Non-recursive ($a_k = 0$) | Recursive ($a_k \neq 0$) |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditionally stable |
| Phase | Can be **exactly linear** | Non-linear in general |
| Memory | $N$ past input samples | $N$ past inputs + $M$ past outputs |

**Three advantages of FIR for robotics:**
1. **Inherent stability** — all poles are at the origin ($z=0$), so the filter is always BIBO stable. Critical for safety-critical robotic control loops.
2. **Linear phase response** — preserves the *shape* of waveforms (no phase distortion), which is essential for accurate sensor waveform reproduction (e.g., ECG/EMG signals in bio-robots, vibration analysis).
3. **No limit cycles** in fixed-point arithmetic — important when the filter runs on a low-cost robot microcontroller (e.g., Arduino, STM32) without a floating-point unit.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **Explain the concept of pre-warping in the bilinear transform. Why is it necessary in IIR filter design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The bilinear transform maps the analog $s$-plane onto the digital $z$-plane via $s = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$. This mapping is **non-linear in frequency** — the entire analog frequency axis $\Omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$ is squeezed into the digital range $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi)$ via the relation:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

This means that a frequency $\omega_d$ in the digital domain corresponds to a *different* analog frequency $\Omega_a = (2/T)\tan(\omega_d/2)$. If we design an analog filter for $\omega_d$ and blindly apply the BLT, the resulting digital filter will have its cut-off at a *wrong* frequency.

**Pre-warping** is the corrective procedure: for every critical digital frequency $\omega_k$ (pass-band, stop-band, notch), we compute the corresponding analog frequency $\Omega_k$ using the equation above, design the analog filter for $\Omega_k$, and *then* apply the BLT. This guarantees that the final digital filter meets the original specifications at $\omega_k$.

It is **necessary** because the BLT is a one-to-one map but not a linear map — without pre-warping, all frequency specifications get shifted, and the design would fail to meet the KTU-given $f_p$, $f_s$ requirements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3. (A)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3] **(14 Marks)**
> **Design a digital FIR low-pass filter using the Hamming window with the following specifications:**
> - Pass-band edge: $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad
> - Stop-band edge: $\omega_s = 0.35\pi$ rad
> - Stop-band attenuation: $A_s \ge 50$ dB
> - Sampling frequency: $F_s = 10$ kHz
>
> **(a)** Determine the filter order $N$ and the cut-off frequency $\omega_c$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the filter coefficients $h[n]$ and sketch the magnitude response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Order and Cut-off Frequency (7 marks)

**Step 1:** For a Hamming window, the transition width is $\Delta\omega \approx 3.32\pi / N$, giving:

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{3.32\pi}{\Delta\omega} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2:** Compute $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p = 0.35\pi - 0.20\pi = 0.15\pi$.

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{3.32\pi}{0.15\pi} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 22.13 \right\rceil = 23$$

Round to next odd integer for Type-I linear-phase symmetry: $N = 23$ (already odd). Good.

**Step 3:** The cut-off frequency is taken at the **midpoint of the transition band**:

$$\omega_c = \frac{\omega_p + \omega_s}{2} = \frac{0.20\pi + 0.35\pi}{2} = 0.275\pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to Hz for completeness: $f_c = 0.275 \times 5000 = 1375$ Hz.

**Step 5:** Group delay $\alpha = (N-1)/2 = 11$ samples.

**Step 6:** Verify $A_s \ge 50$ dB — Hamming window guarantees $A_s \approx 53$ dB. ✅

### Part (b) — Filter Coefficients and Magnitude Response (7 marks)

**Step 1:** The ideal impulse response of the LPF is:

$$h_d[n] = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)} = \frac{\sin(0.275\pi (n - 11))}{\pi (n - 11)}, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, 22$$

with $h_d[11] = \omega_c / \pi = 0.275$.

**Step 2:** The Hamming window is:

$$w[n] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, 22$$

**Step 3:** The final coefficients are $h[n] = h_d[n] \cdot w[n]$.

**Sample computation for $n = 0$:**

$$h_d[0] = \frac{\sin(0.275\pi (0 - 11))}{\pi (0 - 11)} = \frac{\sin(-3.025\pi)}{-11\pi} = \frac{\sin(-0.025\pi)}{-11\pi} \approx \frac{-0.0785}{-34.56} \approx 0.00227$$

$$w[0] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(0) = 0.08$$

$$h[0] = 0.00227 \times 0.08 \approx 0.000182$$

**Sample computation for $n = 11$ (centre):**

$$h_d[11] = 0.275, \quad w[11] = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(\pi) = 0.54 + 0.46 = 1.00$$

$$h[11] = 0.275 \times 1.00 = 0.275$$

By symmetry, $h[22 - n] = h[n]$. The full table of 23 coefficients is symmetric.

**Step 4:** Magnitude response sketch:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF["Frequency 0 to pi"] --> nodePB["Pass Band 0 to 0.20 pi Ripple lt 0.1 dB"]
    nodePB --> nodeTB["Transition 0.20 pi to 0.35 pi"]
    nodeTB --> nodeSB["Stop Band 0.35 pi to pi Attenuation gt 50 dB"]
```

The sketch shows flat pass-band with small ripple, gradual roll-off through the transition, and stop-band at least 50 dB below the pass-band level. The exact $H(e^{j\omega})$ can be computed in MATLAB/Python as $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{22} h[n] e^{-j\omega n}$. ∎

> **Q3. (B) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3] **(14 Marks)**
> **Design a digital IIR Butterworth low-pass filter using the bilinear transform method with the following specifications:**
> - Pass-band edge: $f_p = 1.5$ kHz, $A_p \le 1$ dB
> - Stop-band edge: $f_s = 3$ kHz, $A_s \ge 20$ dB
> - Sampling frequency: $F_s = 8$ kHz
>
> **(a)** Determine the filter order $N$ and the pre-warped analog frequencies. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Write the analog prototype transfer function and apply the BLT to obtain the digital filter transfer function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Order and Pre-Warped Frequencies (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Convert to digital frequencies.

$$\omega_p = 2\pi \frac{1500}{8000} = 0.375\pi \text{ rad}, \quad \omega_s = 2\pi \frac{3000}{8000} = 0.75\pi \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2:** Pre-warp using $\Omega = (2/T) \tan(\omega/2)$ with $T = 1/8000 = 0.000125$ s, so $2/T = 16000$.

$$\Omega_p = 16000 \tan(0.1875\pi) = 16000 \times 0.6682 = 10691 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\Omega_s = 16000 \tan(0.375\pi) = 16000 \times 3.0777 = 49243 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Butterworth order formula:

$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10} \sqrt{(10^{0.1 A_s} - 1) / (10^{0.1 A_p} - 1)}}{\log_{10}(\Omega_s / \Omega_p)} = \frac{\log_{10} \sqrt{(10^2 - 1)/(10^{0.1} - 1)}}{\log_{10}(49243 / 10691)}$$

$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10} \sqrt{99/0.2589}}{\log_{10}(4.606)} = \frac{\log_{10}(19.56)}{0.6631} = \frac{1.291}{0.6631} = 1.95$$

So $N = 2$.

**Step 4:** Choose the design cut-off $\Omega_c = \Omega_p$ (could also use $\Omega_c = \Omega_s / (10^{0.1 A_s} - 1)^{1/2N}$ — alternative).

### Part (b) — Analog Prototype and BLT (7 marks)

**Step 1:** The normalised 2nd-order Butterworth prototype is:

$$H_a(s') = \frac{1}{(s')^2 + \sqrt{2} s' + 1}$$

**Step 2:** Frequency-scale to $\Omega_c = 10691$ rad/s: substitute $s' = s/\Omega_c$.

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2} \Omega_c s + \Omega_c^2} = \frac{1.143 \times 10^8}{s^2 + 15120 s + 1.143 \times 10^8}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the BLT: $s = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = 16000 \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into $H_a(s)$ and simplify:

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$$

Using the standard BLT substitution formulas for a 2nd-order denominator $s^2 + ps + q$:

$$a_1 = \frac{-8 + 2pT + 2qT^2}{4 + 2pT + qT^2}, \quad a_2 = \frac{4 - 2pT + qT^2}{4 + 2pT + qT^2}$$

With $p = \sqrt{2} \Omega_c = 15120$ and $q = \Omega_c^2 = 1.143 \times 10^8$ and $T = 0.000125$:

- $pT = 1.890$, $qT^2 = 1.786$
- $a_1 = (-8 + 3.780 + 3.572) / (4 + 3.780 + 1.786) = -0.648 / 9.566 = -0.0677$
- $a_2 = (4 - 3.780 + 1.786) / 9.566 = 2.006 / 9.566 = 0.2097$

The numerator gain is adjusted to make $H(1) = 1$ (DC gain normalisation):

$$K = \frac{1 + a_1 + a_2}{b_0 + b_1 + b_2}, \quad b_0 = b_2 = K q T^2, \quad b_1 = 2 K q T^2$$

After scaling: $b_0 = 0.2019$, $b_1 = 0.4038$, $b_2 = 0.2019$.

**Final transfer function:**

$$\boxed{\; H(z) = \frac{0.2019 + 0.4038 z^{-1} + 0.2019 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.0677 z^{-1} + 0.2097 z^{-2}} \;}$$

**Step 5:** Stability check — solve $z^2 - 0.0677 z + 0.2097 = 0$ to get poles $z_{1,2} = 0.0338 \pm j 0.4563$. Magnitude $\vert z \vert = \sqrt{0.00114 + 0.2082} = \sqrt{0.2094} = 0.4576 < 1$ ✅. Filter is **stable**. ∎

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting pre-warping** (–3 to –4 marks). The most common and most expensive error. Always pre-warp *every* critical frequency.
> 2. **Rounding filter order down** instead of up. Filter order $N$ must be the **ceiling** of the computed value to guarantee specifications.
> 3. **Skipping the stability check.** Even for IIR filters, always compute the pole magnitudes and verify $\vert z_p \vert < 1$.
> 4. **Wrong window symmetry.** Hamming/Hann windows require $N$ odd for Type-I linear phase. Hann and Hamming have a zero at $n=0$ and $n=N-1$, which can cause issues if not handled.
> 5. **Not stating sampling frequency $T$ explicitly** in BLT substitutions — leads to silent numerical errors.
> 6. **Confusing `z` (lowercase) with `Z` (uppercase)** in the BLT formula — a transcription mistake that wrecks the entire solution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (high-density bullets):**

- A **digital filter** is governed by a difference equation and characterised by the transfer function $H(z)$.
- **FIR filters** have $a_k = 0$ — always stable, can have linear phase, designed by windowing or frequency sampling.
- **IIR filters** have $a_k \neq 0$ — recursive, conditionally stable, designed via BLT or impulse invariance.
- The **Bilinear Transform** is defined as $s = (2/T) \cdot (1 - z^{-1})/(1 + z^{-1})$ — it avoids aliasing but **pre-warps** frequencies.
- **Pre-warping formula:** $\Omega = (2/T) \tan(\omega / 2)$ — applied to *every* critical frequency.
- **Ideal LPF impulse response** is the sampled **sinc function:** $h_d[n] = \sin(\omega_c (n-\alpha)) / (\pi (n-\alpha))$.
- **FIR order (Kaiser):** $N \ge (A_s - 8) / (2.285 \, \Delta\omega)$ — pre-multiply for Hamming/Blackman: $N \approx 3.3\pi / \Delta\omega$.
- **Butterworth order:** $N \ge \log_{10} \sqrt{(10^{0.1 A_s} - 1) / (10^{0.1 A_p} - 1)} / \log_{10}(\Omega_s / \Omega_p)$ — round **up**.
- **Hamming window** → $A_s \approx 53$ dB, $\Delta\omega \approx 3.32\pi / N$.
- **Hann window** → $A_s \approx 44$ dB, $\Delta\omega \approx 3.11\pi / N$.
- **Blackman window** → $A_s \approx 74$ dB, $\Delta\omega \approx 5.56\pi / N$.
- **Stability test:** all poles of $H(z)$ must satisfy $\vert z_p \vert < 1$.
- **Direct Form I** uses $M + N$ delays; **Direct Form II (canonical)** uses $\max(M, N)$ delays.
- **Cascade (biquad)** form is preferred for fixed-point implementation — numerically robust.
- **Group delay** of a linear-phase FIR: $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ (constant across all frequencies).
- **Nyquist limit:** $f_{\max} = F_s/2$ — anti-aliasing analog filter **must** precede the ADC in any robotic data acquisition.
- **In robotics**, FIR is preferred for sensor-signal pre-filtering; IIR is preferred for closed-loop control compensators and audio.
- **Biquad second-order section** is the universal building block for high-order IIR implementations.
- Always plot the **magnitude response in dB** to verify pass-band ripple and stop-band attenuation — the linear plot hides these details.
- **Linear phase** is achieved in FIR by enforcing $h[n] = h[N-1-n]$ (symmetric) or $h[n] = -h[N-1-n]$ (anti-symmetric).
- For the **KTU exam**: state the formula, substitute, simplify, verify stability, and **comment on phase linearity** — examiners award marks for this conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Reduction Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT402 - SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Noise Reduction Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Noise Reduction (Denoising)** is the process of removing unwanted random perturbations (noise) from a measured or observed signal while preserving the integrity of the underlying true signal of interest. In the context of **Signal Processing for Robotics**, noise reduction is a critical preprocessing stage that conditions signals from sensors such as **LiDAR**, **cameras (RGB-D, stereo)**, **IMUs (Inertial Measurement Units)**, **encoders**, and **microphones** so that downstream algorithms (SLAM, object detection, path planning) operate reliably.

Mathematically, a corrupted measurement $y[n]$ is modeled as:

$$y[n] = x[n] + \eta[n]$$

where $x[n]$ is the desired true signal and $\eta[n]$ is the additive noise component, typically modeled as a stochastic process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Noise reduction in robotics is not merely "cleaning" a signal — it directly impacts the **state-estimation accuracy** and **control-loop stability** of the robot.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to listen to a friend speaking in a crowded restaurant. The friend's voice is the **true signal** $x[n]$, and the chatter, clatter of plates, and background music constitute the **noise** $\eta[n]$. Your brain performs a remarkable denoising operation — it focuses on the **frequencies of the friend's voice** (a form of *frequency-selective filtering*) and tracks temporal changes in volume and direction (a form of *state estimation*, similar to a Kalman filter).

In robotics, this problem is omnipresent:
- A **camera mounted on a mobile robot** captures an image blurred by **Gaussian noise** from low-light sensor conditions.
- An **IMU's accelerometer** produces jittery readings due to **thermal and quantization noise**.
- A **microphone array** on a humanoid robot captures speech buried in **environmental noise** and **echoes**.

The denoising algorithm is the robot's "auditory/visual cortex" that extracts the meaningful information.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metric

The single most important figure of merit for any denoising operation is the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**:

$$\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} \right) \;\;\; [\text{dB}]$$

where $P_{\text{signal}}$ and $P_{\text{noise}}$ are the respective average powers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Benchmark:** A clean speech signal typically has **SNR > 40 dB**. Robotic sensor data is acceptable for most control loops when **SNR ≥ 20 dB**. Below **10 dB**, the noise is perceptually dominant and the signal becomes unreliable.

## 1.4 Classification of Noise in Robotic Systems

| Noise Type | Physical Origin | Typical Distribution | Common in |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian (AWGN)** | Thermal agitation, electronic shot noise | Normal: $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Cameras, IMUs, LiDAR intensity |
| **Salt-and-Pepper (Impulse)** | Pixel defects, transmission errors | Sparse, random spikes | Old cameras, lossy links |
| **Pink (1/f) Noise** | Semiconductor flicker | Power spectral density $\propto 1/f$ | MEMS gyroscopes |
| **Quantization Noise** | A/D conversion | Uniform $\mathcal{U}(-\Delta/2, \Delta/2)$ | All digital sensors |
| **Periodic (Line) Noise** | Power-line interference (50/60 Hz) | Sinusoidal | Mains-powered sensors |
| **Speckle Noise** | Coherent wave interference | Multiplicative | SAR, ultrasound, LiDAR |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of denoising technique is dictated **directly** by the noise type. AWGN is best tackled by **linear filters**; impulse noise requires **order-statistic (median) filters**; state-dependent noise demands **Kalman filtering**.

## 1.5 Broad Categories of Noise Reduction Techniques

1. **Time-domain linear filters** — Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, FIR/IIR low-pass.
2. **Order-statistic (non-linear) filters** — Median, Weighted Median, Min/Max filters.
3. **Optimal linear filters** — Wiener filter (statistically optimal in MSE sense).
4. **State-space recursive estimators** — Kalman Filter, Extended Kalman Filter (EKF), Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF).
5. **Transform-domain methods** — Wavelet thresholding, Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) gating.
6. **Adaptive methods** — LMS / NLMS filters (cancel periodic / correlated noise).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time-Domain Linear Filters

### 2.1.1 Moving Average (MA) Filter
The **Moving Average filter** replaces each sample with the arithmetic mean of a finite window of $L$ past samples:

$$y[n] = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} x[n-k]$$

**Operational Logic (Why & How):**
- *Why it works:* Averaging independent noise samples reduces noise variance by a factor of $L$ (zero-mean noise cancels partially). The signal component, being correlated across samples, is preserved.
- *How:* Convolves the input with a rectangular kernel $h[n] = \frac{1}{L}$ for $n = 0, 1, \dots, L-1$.
- *Limitation:* Introduces a **lag** of $\frac{L-1}{2}$ samples and blurs sharp transients (step edges).

**Variance reduction property:**

$$\text{Var}\{y[n]\} = \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SNR improvement} = 10 \log_{10}(L) \;\; \text{dB}$$

### 2.1.2 Exponential Moving Average (EMA) / First-Order IIR
A recursive low-pass filter with infinite impulse response and only **one** tunable parameter $\alpha \in (0,1)$:

$$y[n] = (1-\alpha)\, y[n-1] + \alpha\, x[n]$$

- *Why it works:* A weighted average giving more weight to recent samples — efficient in memory ($O(1)$ storage).
- *How:* Implemented as a digital integrator with gain $\alpha$.
- *Common usage:* Inertial sensor smoothing, battery state-of-charge estimation in mobile robots.

## 2.2 Order-Statistic Filter: The Median Filter

The **Median Filter** is the workhorse for **impulse/salt-and-pepper noise** removal in robotic vision:

$$y[n] = \text{median}\{x[n-M], \dots, x[n], \dots, x[n+M]\}$$

where the window size is $2M+1$.

**Why it works (intuition):** In a windowed sample set, an impulse-noise spike will always lie at the extreme (min or max) of the sorted values. The median ignores the extreme outliers entirely. Therefore, the spike is **completely eliminated** while edges (which form a majority in local samples) are preserved — unlike a moving average, which would smear edges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Edge-preservation theorem:** For 1-D signals, a median filter with window size $2M+1$ perfectly removes any impulse noise pattern containing fewer than $M+1$ consecutive corrupted samples.

## 2.3 Optimal Linear Filter: The Wiener Filter

The **Wiener filter** $W(e^{j\omega})$ is the **MSE-optimal** linear time-invariant filter that minimizes $E\{(x[n] - y[n])^2\}$ given knowledge of the signal and noise power spectral densities.

In the frequency domain:

$$W(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{S_{xx}(e^{j\omega})}{S_{xx}(e^{j\omega}) + S_{\eta\eta}(e^{j\omega})} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{S_{\eta\eta}(e^{j\omega})}{S_{xx}(e^{j\omega})}}$$

where $S_{xx}$ and $S_{\eta\eta}$ are the power spectral densities (PSDs) of signal and noise, respectively.

- *Why it works:* At frequencies where the signal is strong (high $S_{xx}$), $W \to 1$ (passes through). Where noise dominates, $W \to 0$ (suppresses).
- *Limitation:* Requires stationary statistics of both signal and noise — an assumption often violated in real robotic systems.

## 2.4 The Kalman Filter — The Roboticist's Workhorse

The Kalman Filter is a **recursive, optimal, linear, Gaussian state estimator**. It is the cornerstone of modern robotics (used in **SLAM**, **INS/GPS fusion**, **arm control**, and **UAV stabilization**).

**State-space model:**

$$x_k = A_k x_{k-1} + B_k u_k + w_k \quad ; \quad w_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q_k)$$

$$z_k = H_k x_k + v_k \quad ; \quad v_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R_k)$$

where $x_k$ is the state vector, $z_k$ is the measurement, $u_k$ is the control input, $A_k, B_k, H_k$ are system matrices, and $Q_k, R_k$ are the **process and measurement noise covariances**.

**The two-step predict-update cycle:**

1. **Predict (Time Update):**

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} = A_k \hat{x}_{k-1\mid k-1} + B_k u_k$$

$$P_{k\mid k-1} = A_k P_{k-1\mid k-1} A_k^{\top} + Q_k$$

2. **Update (Measurement Update):**

$$K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^{\top} \left( H_k P_{k\mid k-1} H_k^{\top} + R_k \right)^{-1}$$

$$\hat{x}_{k\mid k} = \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k \left( z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k\mid k-1} \right)$$

$$P_{k\mid k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\mid k-1}$$

The **Kalman Gain $K_k$** automatically balances the trust between the model prediction and the noisy measurement:
- If $R_k$ is small (sensor is reliable) $\Rightarrow$ $K_k$ is large $\Rightarrow$ trust the measurement.
- If $Q_k$ is small (model is reliable) $\Rightarrow$ $K_k$ is small $\Rightarrow$ trust the prediction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Kalman dominates robotics noise reduction:** It is **causal** (real-time), **recursive** ($O(n^2)$ memory regardless of history), and **statistically optimal** for linear-Gaussian systems — and can be extended via EKF/UKF to nonlinear systems like quadrotor attitude estimation.

## 2.5 Adaptive Filter: LMS / NLMS

Used for **active noise cancellation** (e.g., cancelling motor noise in a robot's microphone):

$$y[n] = \mathbf{w}^{\top}[n] \mathbf{x}[n]$$

$$e[n] = d[n] - y[n]$$

$$\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu e[n] \mathbf{x}[n]$$

where $\mathbf{w}$ is the adaptive weight vector, $d[n]$ is the desired signal, and $\mu$ is the step size (learning rate).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments on this topic. Pipe symbols are written using `\vert` to prevent Markdown table corruption.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Parameter | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Signal-Noise Model | $y[n] = x[n] + \eta[n]$ | $\eta$ is stochastic | — |
| 2 | SNR | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s / P_\eta)$ | Ratio of powers | **dB** |
| 3 | MA Filter Output | $y[n] = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} x[n-k]$ | Window length $L$ | — |
| 4 | MA Variance Reduction | $\sigma_y^2 = \sigma_\eta^2 / L$ | Independent samples | — |
| 5 | SNR Improvement (MA) | $\Delta\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10} L$ | Function of $L$ | **dB** |
| 6 | EMA Filter | $y[n] = (1-\alpha)y[n-1] + \alpha x[n]$ | Smoothing $0 \lt \alpha \lt 1$ | — |
| 7 | Median Filter | $y[n] = \text{med}\{x[n-M], \dots, x[n+M]\}$ | Half-window $M$ | — |
| 8 | Wiener Filter (Freq.) | $W(e^{j\omega}) = S_{xx}/(S_{xx} + S_{\eta\eta})$ | PSDs of signal and noise | — |
| 9 | Kalman State Predict | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} = A_k \hat{x}_{k-1\vert k-1} + B_k u_k$ | State matrix $A_k$ | state-units |
| 10 | Kalman Covariance Predict | $P_{k\vert k-1} = A_k P_{k-1\vert k-1} A_k^{\top} + Q_k$ | Process noise $Q_k$ | state$^2$ |
| 11 | Kalman Gain | $K_k = P_{k\vert k-1} H_k^{\top} (H_k P_{k\vert k-1} H_k^{\top} + R_k)^{-1}$ | Innovation covariance | — |
| 12 | Kalman State Update | $\hat{x}_{k\vert k} = \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1} + K_k (z_k - H_k \hat{x}_{k\vert k-1})$ | Innovation $z_k - H\hat{x}$ | state-units |
| 13 | Kalman Covariance Update | $P_{k\vert k} = (I - K_k H_k) P_{k\vert k-1}$ | Posterior covariance | state$^2$ |
| 14 | LMS Weight Update | $\mathbf{w}[n+1] = \mathbf{w}[n] + 2\mu e[n]\mathbf{x}[n]$ | Step size $\mu$ | — |
| 15 | Wavelet Thresholding | $\hat{w}_j = \text{soft}(w_j, \lambda) = \text{sgn}(w_j)\max(\vert w_j\vert - \lambda, 0)$ | Threshold $\lambda$ | coefficient |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Filter Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Camera image denoising | Gaussian / Bilateral / Median | Preserves edges while removing AWGN / salt-pepper |
| IMU gyroscope data | Kalman / Complementary filter | Fuses gyro + accelerometer with model-based prediction |
| Mobile robot wheel-encoder smoothing | EMA / MA | Removes electrical switching noise |
| EKF-based SLAM | Extended Kalman Filter | Nonlinear state and observation models |
| Active noise-cancelling headsets | LMS / NLMS adaptive filter | Cancels correlated periodic noise |
| LiDAR point cloud denoising | Voxel grid + statistical outlier | Removes floating speckle points |
| Robot speech recognition front-end | Wiener / Spectral Subtraction | Enhances speech SNR before ASR |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SNR Improvement of the Moving Average Filter

**Goal:** Show that averaging $L$ i.i.d. AWGN samples reduces the output noise variance by a factor of $L$.

**Given:**
- Input $y[n] = x[n] + \eta[n]$ with $\eta[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_\eta^2)$ and i.i.d. samples.
- Filter output $y_{\text{out}}[n] = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} y[n-k]$.

**Step 1 — Substitute the input model:**

$$y_{\text{out}}[n] = \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} \big( x[n-k] + \eta[n-k] \big)$$

**Step 2 — Split into signal and noise terms using linearity of expectation:**

$$y_{\text{out}}[n] = \underbrace{\frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} x[n-k]}_{\text{signal component}} \; + \; \underbrace{\frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} \eta[n-k]}_{\text{noise component}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the output noise variance.** Since the $\eta[n-k]$ are independent and identically distributed with zero mean and variance $\sigma_\eta^2$:

$$\text{Var}\{ y_{\text{out}}[n] \} = E\left\{ \left( \frac{1}{L} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} \eta[n-k] \right)^2 \right\}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the squared sum** (cross-terms vanish because samples are uncorrelated):

$$= \frac{1}{L^2} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} \sum_{j=0}^{L-1} E\{\eta[n-k]\,\eta[n-j]\}$$

**Step 5 — Use the white-noise property** $E\{\eta[m]\,\eta[\ell]\} = \sigma_\eta^2 \delta[m-\ell]$:

$$= \frac{1}{L^2} \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} \sigma_\eta^2 = \frac{L \cdot \sigma_\eta^2}{L^2} = \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude with SNR improvement.** Since the input signal power is approximately unchanged for slowly varying $x[n]$:

$$\Delta\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal,out}}}{P_{\text{noise,out}}}\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal,in}}}{P_{\text{noise,in}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(L) \;\; \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Valuable Result:** Doubling the window length $L$ gives only **+3 dB** improvement — a fundamental trade-off in averaging-based denoising.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Median Filter Eliminates a Single Impulse

**Goal:** Show that a median filter of window size $2M+1$ completely removes a single isolated impulse.

**Setup:** Consider samples $\ldots, x[-1], x[0], x[1], \ldots$ in a window $[n-M, n+M]$.
- Assume exactly one sample, say $x[n]$, is corrupted: $x[n] = A$ where $\vert A\vert$ is much larger than any other sample.
- The remaining $2M$ samples are "clean" values $x_{\text{clean},k}$.

**Step 1 — Form the sorted set of size $2M+1$:**

$$\{x_{\text{clean},1}, x_{\text{clean},2}, \ldots, x_{\text{clean},2M}, \; A \}$$

**Step 2 — Sort the values.** Since $\vert A\vert \gg \vert x_{\text{clean},k}\vert$ for all $k$, the value $A$ will sit at one of the two extremes of the sorted list (either the minimum or the maximum).

**Step 3 — Select the median.** The median is the element at position $M+1$ of the sorted list. Because there are $2M$ clean samples distributed around the median position, and the single impulse $A$ is pushed to the boundary:

$$\text{median}\{x[n-M], \ldots, x[n+M]\} = x_{\text{clean},\text{median}}$$

The impulse is **completely removed**.

**Step 4 — Generalization.** The same logic shows that a median filter of window size $2M+1$ can eliminate any impulse-noise pattern containing **up to $M$ consecutive corrupted samples** (since up to $M$ values on each side can be removed while leaving at least $M+1$ clean values that "outvote" them).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why median filters are **edge-preserving** — a sharp step in the underlying signal forms a majority of values on one side and is unaffected.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Wiener Filter (MSE Optimality)

**Goal:** Derive the FIR Wiener filter coefficients that minimize $E\{e^2[n]\}$ where $e[n] = x[n] - y[n]$ and $y[n] = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{u}[n]$ with $\mathbf{u}[n] = [u[n], u[n-1], \ldots, u[n-L+1]]^{\top}$.

**Step 1 — Define the MSE cost function:**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{e^2[n]\} = E\{(x[n] - \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{u}[n])^2\}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the quadratic:**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\{x^2[n]\} - 2 \mathbf{w}^{\top} E\{x[n]\mathbf{u}[n]\} + \mathbf{w}^{\top} E\{\mathbf{u}[n]\mathbf{u}^{\top}[n]\} \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce compact notation:**

$$\mathbf{p} = E\{x[n]\mathbf{u}[n]\} \quad ; \quad \mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{u}[n]\mathbf{u}^{\top}[n]\}$$

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_x^2 - 2 \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ and set to zero (necessary condition for a minimum):**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2 \mathbf{p} + 2 \mathbf{R} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the Wiener-Hopf equations:**

$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1} \mathbf{p}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to frequency domain (using PSD relations $S_{xx}(\omega) = \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{r}_x$ etc.):**

$$W(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{S_{xx}(e^{j\omega})}{S_{xx}(e^{j\omega}) + S_{\eta\eta}(e^{j\omega})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Wiener filter is **offline** (requires knowledge of autocorrelation matrices) — for online robotics applications, the **Kalman filter** is its time-domain, recursive counterpart under the linear-Gaussian assumption.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Denoising Filter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU 2024 exam point** — students are routinely asked to justify a filter choice.

| Noise Type / Scenario | Recommended Filter | Justification | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| AWGN on 1-D sensor signal (encoder, IMU axis) | **MA / EMA / Butterworth LPF** | Optimal MSE reduction for white noise | Edge lag, blurring transients |
| Impulse / salt-and-pepper in image | **Median filter** | Perfect removal of isolated spikes, edge-preserving | High computational cost ($\mathcal{O}(L \log L)$) |
| AWGN on image with edge preservation | **Bilateral / Non-Local Means** | Preserves structure while smoothing | Slow; requires tuning |
| Periodic / correlated noise (e.g., 50 Hz mains) | **Adaptive LMS / Notch filter** | Cancels narrowband interference | Needs reference signal for LMS |
| Sensor fusion (IMU + GPS + encoder) | **Kalman / EKF** | Optimal recursive estimator, handles multi-rate sensors | Requires system model + noise covariances |
| State estimation with nonlinear dynamics (quadrotor) | **EKF / UKF / Particle Filter** | First-order linearization or sigma-point transform | Computationally heavier than KF |
| Time-frequency non-stationary noise (e.g., speech) | **Wavelet thresholding / STFT gating** | Sparsity in time-frequency plane | Requires basis selection |
| LiDAR point cloud speckle | **Statistical Outlier Removal (SOR)** | Removes points far from local mean | Sensitive to neighbourhood size |
| Image with mixed Gaussian + impulse | **Median → Gaussian cascade** | First removes spikes, then smooths | Two-stage cost |
| Slowly varying state with low noise | **Complementary filter** | $y = \alpha \cdot \text{low-pass} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \text{high-pass}$ | Simple, single tunable $\alpha$ |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A robot's wheel encoder produces velocity readings $y[n] = v[n] + \eta[n]$ where $v[n]$ is the true velocity (a constant $1.0$ m/s) and $\eta[n] \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 0.16)$ i.i.d. (i.e., $\sigma_\eta = 0.4$ m/s).

A 4-point moving average filter is applied. Find:
1. The output noise standard deviation.
2. The output SNR.
3. The input SNR.
4. The SNR improvement in dB.

**Solution:**

**Part 1:** Using the variance reduction formula:

$$\sigma_{y_{\text{out}}}^2 = \frac{\sigma_\eta^2}{L} = \frac{0.16}{4} = 0.04$$

$$\sigma_{y_{\text{out}}} = \sqrt{0.04} = 0.2 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Part 2:** Output signal power $\approx v^2 = 1.0^2 = 1.0$ (signal is DC-like, unaffected). Output noise power $= 0.04$:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{out}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{1.0}{0.04} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(25) = 10 \times 1.3979 \approx 13.98 \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Part 3:** Input noise power $= 0.16$:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{in}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{1.0}{0.16} \right) = 10 \log_{10}(6.25) = 10 \times 0.7959 \approx 7.96 \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Part 4:** SNR improvement:

$$\Delta\text{SNR} = 13.98 - 7.96 = 6.02 \;\; \text{dB}$$

**Verification using the direct formula:** $\Delta\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}(L) = 10 \log_{10}(4) = 6.02$ dB. ✓

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Robotic Denoising Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Robotic Sensor] --> B[Signal Acquisition and ADC]
    B --> C[Preprocessing and Calibration]
    C --> D{Noise Type Identification}
    D -->|AWGN| E[Linear Filter: MA / Butterworth LPF]
    D -->|Impulse| F[Median Filter]
    D -->|Periodic 50 60 Hz| G[Notch or LMS Adaptive Filter]
    D -->|Multi-Sensor Fusion| H[Kalman or EKF]
    E --> I[Post-Processing and Feature Extraction]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Downstream Module: SLAM / Control / Perception]
    J --> K[Actuator or Decision Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pipeline begins with a noisy raw sensor (e.g., a noisy LiDAR scan). The block `D` performs **automatic noise classification** (a high-end feature) and routes the signal to the appropriate filter bank. The output is a clean, denoised signal ready for higher-level robotic tasks.

---

## 4.2 Kalman Filter Predict-Update Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Initialise x0, P0] --> P1[Predict State: xhat k equals A xhat k-1 plus B u k]
    P1 --> P2[Predict Covariance: Pk equals A Pk-1 A transpose plus Qk]
    P2 --> M1[Receive Measurement zk]
    M1 --> M2[Compute Kalman Gain: Kk equals P H transpose times inverse of H P H transpose plus R]
    M2 --> M3[Update State: xhat k equals xhat k-1 plus Kk times innovation zk minus H xhat k-1]
    M3 --> M4[Update Covariance: Pk equals I minus Kk H times Pk-1]
    M4 --> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `P1`, `M2`) to avoid conflicts with reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold/italic markdown inside double-quoted strings.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Filter Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Choose a Denoising Filter] --> Q2{Is noise Gaussian white}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Do you need edge preservation}
    Q3 -->|No| Q4[Use MA or EMA or Butterworth LPF]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q5[Use Bilateral or Non Local Means]
    Q2 -->|No| Q6{Is noise impulse type}
    Q6 -->|Yes| Q7[Use Median Filter]
    Q6 -->|No| Q8{Is noise correlated periodic}
    Q8 -->|Yes| Q9[Use Adaptive LMS or Notch]
    Q8 -->|No| Q10{Are multiple sensors available}
    Q10 -->|Yes| Q11[Use Kalman or EKF Sensor Fusion]
    Q10 -->|No| Q12[Use Wiener Filter if stationary stats known]
```

---

## 4.4 1-D Median Filter Operation — Window Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[x at n minus 3 equals 12] --> W
    A2[x at n minus 2 equals 13] --> W
    A3[x at n minus 1 equals 200 impulse] --> W
    A4[x at n equals 14] --> W
    A5[x at n plus 1 equals 11] --> W
    A6[x at n plus 2 equals 13] --> W
    A7[x at n plus 3 equals 12] --> W
    W[Sorted: 11 12 12 13 13 14 200] --> M
    M[Median equals 13] --> Y[y at n equals 13 output clean]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The impulse value **200** is pushed to the maximum of the sorted set; the median (middle element of an odd-sized window) is **13**, completely ignoring the spike.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Signal-to-Noise Ratio" (SNR). What is the minimum SNR typically required for reliable operation of a robotic control loop?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is defined as the ratio of the average signal power to the average noise power, expressed in decibels (dB):
>
> $$\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left( \frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}} \right) \;\; \text{dB}$$
>
> For reliable operation of a robotic control loop (e.g., motor speed control, sensor-based feedback), a minimum **SNR of approximately 20 dB** is generally required. Below 10 dB, the noise becomes perceptually dominant and the controller's decisions become unreliable, leading to instability or oscillation. (3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark practical value)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State two advantages and one disadvantage of the median filter over the moving average filter for image noise removal in robotics.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Advantages of Median Filter:**
> 1. It **completely removes** isolated impulse (salt-and-pepper) noise that commonly occurs in low-cost robotic cameras and lossy transmission channels.
> 2. It is **edge-preserving** — sharp transitions in the image (e.g., object boundaries) are not blurred, unlike the moving average which smears them.
>
> **Disadvantage:**
> 1. It has a **higher computational cost** ($\mathcal{O}(L \log L)$) compared to a moving average ($\mathcal{O}(L)$), and produces a **non-linear output** that is harder to analyse mathematically.
> (3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Kalman filter** algorithm for noise reduction in robotic state estimation. Write the **predict** and **update** equations and explain the role of the **Kalman Gain $K_k$**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A robotic temperature sensor produces noisy readings with true temperature $T = 50°C$ and noise standard deviation $\sigma_\eta = 2°C$. A 5-point moving average filter is applied. Compute: (i) Output noise standard deviation, (ii) Output SNR, (iii) SNR improvement in dB.

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### (a) Kalman Filter Explanation

The **Kalman filter** is a recursive, optimal state estimator for linear dynamical systems corrupted by Gaussian noise. It is the **de facto standard** for state estimation in robotics (SLAM, IMU fusion, UAV control).

**Block diagram of the predict-update loop:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State Predict xhat k given k-1] --> U1
    P2[Covariance Predict Pk given k-1] --> K1
    S[Measurement zk] --> IN[Innovation: zk minus H xhat]
    U1 --> IN
    IN --> K1[Kalman Gain Computation]
    K1 --> U2[State Update xhat k]
    K1 --> U3[Covariance Update Pk]
    U2 --> P1
    U3 --> P2
```
